Research paper
A comparative study of one-step and two-step approaches for MAPbI3
perovskite layer and its inﬂuence on the performance of mesoscopic
perovskite solar cell
Minhuan Wang a,1, Yulin Feng a,1, Jiming Bian a,⇑, Hongzhu Liu a, Yantao Shi b,⇑
a Key Laboratory of Materials Modiﬁcation by Laser, Ion and Electron Beams (Ministry of Education), School of Physics, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China
b State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China
a r t i c l e
i n f o
Article history:
Received 29 September 2017
In ﬁnal form 5 December 2017
Available online 7 December 2017
Keywords:
A. Organic-inorganic halide perovskite
A. Thin ﬁlms
B. Solution process
B. Spin coating
D. Photovoltaic properties
a b s t r a c t
The mesoscopic perovskite solar cells (M-PSCs) were synthesized with MAPbI3 perovskite layers as light
harvesters, which were grown with one-step and two-step solution process, respectively. A comparative
study was performed through the quantitative correlation of resulting device performance and the crys-
talline quality of perovskite layers. Comparing with the one-step counterpart, a pronounced improve-
ment in the steady-state power conversion efﬁciencies (PCEs) by 56.86% was achieved with two-step
process, which was mainly resulted from the signiﬁcant enhancement in ﬁll factor (FF) from 48% to
77% without sacriﬁcing the open circuit voltage (Voc) and short circuit current (Jsc). The enhanced FF
was attributed to the reduced non-radiative recombination channels due to the better crystalline quality
and larger grain size with the two-step processed perovskite layer. Moreover, the superiority of two-step
over one-step process was demonstrated with rather good reproducibility.
 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction
Solar energy is considered to be the most promising new energy
resource due to its advantages of cleanness, sustainability, and
safety. Organic-inorganic halide perovskite (OIHP) materials, with
the general formula ABX3 (where A is a monovalent cation, B is a
divalent metal ion and X is a halide), have emerged as a class of
semiconductor materials with unprecedented potential for solar
cell application [1–5]. The past few years witnessed the extremely
rapid development of OIHP photovoltaics, since the ﬁrst embodi-
ment of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with a power conversion efﬁ-
ciency (PCE) of 3.8% in 2009, the device performance has shown
astonishing growth rate and certiﬁed values of 22.1% was achieved
within a few years of academic research, approaching that of its
commercialized rivals, such as state-of-the-art polycrystalline sili-
con, cadmium telluride (CdTe) and copper indium gallium selenide
(CIGS) photovoltaics, while these photovoltaic materials have been
developed for over several decades [3–10]. There is increasing con-
sensus that PSCs will exhibit the shortest energy payback time
(0.2 years) of all existing photovoltaic technologies. The great
success of OIHP materials can be attributed to its unique outstand-
ing features compared with other traditional semiconductors such
as crystalline Si and GaAs, mainly including tunable bandgap, high
absorption coefﬁcient (the absorption coefﬁcient of CH3NH3PbI3
(MAPbI3) in the visible light region is more than one order of mag-
nitude larger than that of silicon), long-range and balanced carrier
diffusion
length,
especially
high
crystallinity
despite
low-
temperature processing [11,12]. So far, all reported PSCs contained
a multilayer architecture that normally includes a thin layer of per-
ovskite for light absorbing, which was sandwiched between the
hole-transport layer (HTL) layer and the electron-transport layer
(ETL) [9,12]. The working principle and performance depend criti-
cally on the device structure and properties of each layer. Notably,
the perovskite layer play a vital role for the overall photovoltaic
performance considering the fact that the perovskite layer is
responsible for the light absorption and generation of photo-
induced carriers. Thus, delicate control of the crystallization behav-
ior of perovskite layer is critical for high-efﬁciency PSCs. Beneﬁting
from their ionic nature, the semiconductors in the OIHP material
family
generally
have
good
solubility,
and
hence
low-
temperature solution processes have been generally believed to
be the best suitable approach for high quality perovskite layer
[12–15]. One-step and two-step spin-coating were the most com-
monly used solution processes for perovskite ﬁlm. The properties
of perovskite layer may varied greatly depending on one-step or
two-step process [16,17]. Therefore, a quantitative study on PSCs

important for further improving the device performance.
In this study, the mesoscopic perovskite solar cells (M-PSCs)
with
the
structure
of
Ag/Spiro-OMeTAD/CH3NH3PbI3
(herein
MAPbI3)/SnO2/FTO were synthesized, where the MAPbI3 perovskite
layers were grown with one-step and two-step solution process,
respectively. A comparative study was performed through the
quantitative correlation of resulting device performance and the
crystalline quality of perovskite layers. Comparing with the one-
step counterpart, a dramatic enhancement in ﬁll factor (FF) by
nearly 50% was observed while the open-circuit voltage (Voc) and
short-circuit current density (Jsc) only changed slightly, accord-
ingly the PCEs was improved by 56.86% (from 10.27% to 16.11%).
These results indicate that the two-step processed perovskite layer
might be more suitable and preferred for the potential application
in PSCs devices.
2. Experimental details
2.1. Device fabrication
The M-PSCs in current study were structured as Ag/Spiro-
OMeTAD/MAPbI3/SnO2/FTO/glass, as shown in Fig. 1a, where FTO
refers to ﬂuorine-doped tin oxide (15 X/sq) and worked as sub-
strate and anode, Spiro-OMeTAD denotes 2,20,7,70-tetrakis(N,N-d
i-p-methoxyphenylamine)-9,90-spirobiﬂuorene
and
served
as
HTL. The corresponding energy band diagram structure for the
multilayer sandwich device with perovskite light harvesting layer
was illustrated in Fig. 1b, where the favorable energy band align-
ment for solar cell application was conﬁrmed. Speciﬁcally, Spiro-
OMeTAD served as HTL with electron-blocking properties, while
FTO-SnO2 served as ETL with hole-blocking properties. On expo-
sure to light, charge carriers are generated in the MAPbI3 per-
ovskite
photoactive
layer,
and
electrons
and
holes
are
subsequently extracted and collected by their respective contacts,
ETL and HTL. Two series of perovskite ﬁlms and M-PSC device sam-
ples were fabricated, where the MAPbI3 perovskite layer was
grown with one-step or two-step process, respectively. The pre-
treatment of FTO glass substrate and deposition of FTO-SnO2 based
ETL has been reported in our previous report [12]. For one-step
process, the precursor solutions were prepared by the mixture of
PbI2 (99%) and MAI (99%) in a stoichiometric ratio dissolved in a
mixture solution of DMSO (99.8%) and DMF (99.9%) with a volume
ratio of 1:4. The MAPbI3 perovskite layer was deposited directly on
the FTO-SnO2 ETL by spin coating at 3000 rpm for 50 s, followed by
immediately annealing at 80 C for 40 min. While for the two-step
process, spin coating deposition of PbI2 was ﬁrst performed at
5000 rpm for 5 s and dried at 75 C for 30 min, followed by subse-
quent spin coating of MAI solution (30 mg/mL) at 5000 rpm for 30
s and heating at 150 C for 20 min in glovebox, then the MAPbI3
perovskite ﬁlm was formed by the interdiffusion of PbI2 and MAI
layer. Both one-step and two-step parameters have been optimized
for high quality MAPbI3 perovskite ﬁlm with better morphology.
Next, the p-type Spiro-OMeTAD HTL was deposited on the per-
ovskite layer due to its relatively higher conductivity, easier ﬁlm
formation, weak absorption in the visible region. Finally, fabrica-
tion of M-PSC device was completed by thermal evaporation of a
silver cathode (about 60 nm) atop HTL. The device working area
was ﬁxed at 0.12 cm2 as deﬁned by the overlap of the FTO sub-
strate and the Ag cathode, whereas the photovoltaic active area
was 0.07 cm2 deﬁned by the mask area for PCE measurement. All
the devices process were carried out in a glove box to avoid expos-
ing to ambient atmosphere.
2.2. Characterizations
The crystallographic characterizations were evaluated by X-ray
diffraction (XRD) in regular h-2h scanning mode using a LabXRD-
6000
(CuKa1:
k = 0.154056 nm,
Voltage = 40 kV,
Current = 30
mA). The microstructures of the perovskite ﬁlm and device were
observed by a ﬁeld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-
SEM; S4800). The J-V characteristics were measured using Keithley
2450 source meter at room temperature, and the photovoltaic
response of the M-PSCs was obtained by recording the J-V curves
under the irradiation of Solar simulator AM 1.5G (100 mW cm2,
PEC-L15, Japan), the light intensity was calibrated with a standard
Si photodiode.
3. Results and discussion
Fig. 2 shows the typical h-2h XRD patterns of the MAPbI3 ﬁlms
grown with one-step and two-step process, respectively. As can
be seen from Fig. 2, the ﬁlm with two-step process shows the
well-deﬁned sharp XRD peaks with much better preferred orienta-
tion and pure MAPbI3 phase. It has been reported that the MAPbI3
photoactive layer with this speciﬁc orientation would be favorable
for high efﬁcient charge extraction [9]. While for the sample with
one-step process, some miscellaneous peaks besides orthorhombic
MAPbI3 were detected, especially those from residual PbI2 and

The mechanism was speculated to be related to the too rapid
crystallization proceed of hybrid perovskite due to the strong ionic
interaction between the metal cation (Pb2+) and the halide anions
(I), as well as the uneven mixing and incomplete chemical conver-
sion [19]. The inset in Fig. 2 shows the crystal structure of MAPbI3
perovskite material, MA+ and I ions formed cubic close packed
structure with Pb2+ ions ﬁlled in the gap between the octahedral.
Beneﬁting from such excellent intrinsic spatial symmetry of the
perovskite structure and the low crystallization activation energy
barrier, perovskite thin ﬁlms with excellent crystallinity and low
defect density can been fabricated by the low-temperature two-
step processes, which is a major advantage for commercialization
considering the low processing cost [9].
Fig. 3a and b show the top-view SEM images of MAPbI3 ﬁlm
grown with one-step and two-step process, respectively. Clearly,
the dense and full coverage perovskite ﬁlm composed of close-
packed grains was obtained using both process, and no observable
pin-holes, which are desired to minimize leakage current for solar
cells application. While the grain boundaries were much clearer
for two-step process than the dim one for one-step process. The dis-
tribution of grain size can be directly estimated by the statistical
histogram in Fig. 3c and d, and the Gauss simulation curves were
also plotted with purple solid lines according to the center dots of
each histogram (blue2 dash-dotted lines). By contrast, more standard

Gauss distribution with narrower diameter range of 150–300 nm was
obtained in Fig. 3d, suggesting much improved homogeneity and
enlarged grain size by two-step process. Fig. 3e and f show the
cross-section SEM images of the M-PSC device where the MAPbI3 per-
ovskite layers were grown with one-step and two-step process,
respectively. By contrast, more clear morphology with well-deﬁned
thickness and interfaces corresponding to the Ag/Spiro-OMeTAD/
MAPbI3/SnO2/FTO heterostructure was observed from the sample
with two-step strategy, whereas much fuzzy image with hazy inter-
face was observed for the sample with one-step process. Combined
with the XRD analysis (Fig. 2), it can be concluded that the problems
with one-step process such as incomplete conversion and uncon-
trolled particle sizes of perovskite, can be largely alleviated by two-
step process. There is already consensus that many key material prop-
erties of two-step solution-processed ionic OIHP ﬁlms, such as light
absorption, charge trap depth, exciton binding energies and charge
diffusion length are comparable with or superior to the best proper-
ties of meticulously fabricated conventional photovoltaic materials
(for example, Si, GaAs and CdTe), while which are generally grown
by high temperature and vacuum based techniques [20–22]. Well
controls on interface crystallinity, homogeneity, and morphology
are believed to be crucial to obtain highly performance and repro-
ducible PSCs devices, as will be conﬁrmed in the following section.
Fig. 4(a) shows the J-V curves of the M-PSC devices measured
under the irradiation of solar simulator AM 1.5, where the per-
ovskite photoactive layers were grown by one-step and two-step
process, respectively. Since the PCE of the M-PSC is determined
by the product of JSC, VOC, FF (a critical factors that determine the
bias dependence of the photocurrent), and closely related to the
Rs (series resistance, summed total of body resistance, surface
resistance, electrode resistance, and contact resistance, etc.) and
Rsh (shunt resistance, a side drain resistor, which coursed by the
unclean surfaces and the internal defects of the solar cells). All
these parameters deduced from Fig. 4(a) are summarized in
Table 1. According to Table 1, the exceptional low FF of 48% was
indentiﬁed to be the bottleneck factor for efﬁcient PCE of one-
step M-PSCs. Generally, according to the Shockley-Queisser model,
radiative recombination is an inevitable loss mechanism and set
the upper FF limit to 90% for PSCs at room temperature [23,24].
However, in a vast majority of cases, PSCs exhibit much lower
FFs and even today’s highest performing cells with power conver-
sion efﬁciencies of 21.6% and 22.1% exhibit FF of average 78% and
80%, respectively [24,25]. These results suggest additional charge
transport losses must be present during the device operation. For
current case, the exceptional low FF was speculated to be caused
by the additional non-radiative recombination losses by excess
grain boundary channels (as conﬁrmed by Fig. 3(a)), inefﬁcient
charge extraction by the high Rs (22.5 X cm2), as well as current
leakage through the low Rsh (1973.8 X cm2). In contrast to its
one-step counterpart, the signiﬁcant enhancement in FF from
48% to 77% (increased by 60.4%) was achieved by two-step strat-
egy, mainly due to the reduced non-radiative recombination losses
by the perceivably enlarged grain size (as conﬁrmed by Fig. 3(b)) In
addition, the greatly decreased Rs (from 22.5 to 3 X cm2) and
appreciably enhanced Rsh (from 1973.8 to 7929.5 X cm2), also con-
tribute to the superior performance of device with two-step pro-
cess. While the Voc and Jsc varied slightly due to the same device
conﬁguration and the identical ETL and HTL. As a result, a pro-
nounced improvement in the steady-state PCEs by 56.86% was
achieved, i.e., the PCE value was increased from 10.11% (for tradi-
tional one-step process) to 16.11% (for two-step strategy). In addi-
tion, the performance difference of the device with one-and two-
step process was also demonstrated by the J-V characteristics mea-
sured in dark ambient (Fig. 4b). Similar diode-like rectifying behav-
ior was observed for both devices, conﬁrming the formation of n-i-
p heterojunction within the Ag/Spiro-OMeTAD/MAPbI3/SnO2/FTO
structure. However, the forward current density (J) at speciﬁc bias
differs distinctively. That is, at the speciﬁc bias voltage of 1.0 V, the
J for M-PSC with two-step method is 12.44 mA/cm2, while it was
only 0.14 mA/cm2 for its one-step counterpart (as shown in the
enlarged J-V curve in the inset of Fig. 4b). The nearly two orders
of magnitude enhancement in forward J can be attributed to the
signiﬁcantly reduced Rs due to the better morphology and crys-
tallinity, which was consistent with above observation in Fig. 4(a).

In spite of the high photovoltaic efﬁciency, the reproducibility is
a major concern for practical application. To further corroborate
the superiority of two-step process over one-step process for pho-
tovoltaic application, and also check the reproducibility of both
fabrication process, more than 10 device samples were synthesised
with the same process. The dependence of device performance
(including PCEs, FF, Rs and Rsh) on the samples was shown in
Fig. 5. Although there is a small ﬂuctuation for each parameter in
individual M-PSCs device, the value of PCE and FF of all devices
with two-step process are higher than its counterpart with one-
step process, regardless of the samples, further conﬁrming the
overall superiority of two-step process over one-step process with
rather good reproducibility, and the average value of each param-
eter (including PCE, FF, Rs, Rsh) are in good agreement with the
trend from Fig. 4. There are common consensus in the PSCs com-
munity that the performance varied in a reasonable range even
the devices were fabricated in one batch. [1] The current variations
in performance parameters of M-PSCs were in reasonable range.
4. Conclusion
The M-PSCs with the architecture of Ag/Spiro-OMeTAD/MAPbI3/
SnO2/FTO were synthesized, the MAPbI3 perovskite photoactive
layers were grown with one-step and two-step solution process,
respectively. A comparative study was performed through the
quantitative correlation of resulting device performance and the
crystalline quality of perovskite layers. Comparing with the one-
step counterpart, a pronounced improvement in the steady-state
power conversion efﬁciencies (PCEs) by 56.86% was achieved with
two-step process, which was mainly resulted from the signiﬁcant
enhancement in ﬁll factor (FF) from 48% to 77% without sacriﬁcing
the open circuit voltage (Voc) and short circuit current (Jsc). More-
over, the superiority of two-step over one-step process was
demonstrated with rather good reproducibility. These results
would shed a light on quantitative understanding the correlation
of growth strategies of perovskite layer and performance of M-
PSCs, and thus facilitate further boosting the device performance.

Reversible degradation-assisted interface engineering via Cs4PbBr6 
nanocrystals to boost the performance of CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells 
Wenwen Liu a, Mengsha Cao a, Jing Zhang a, Jun Jiang a, Haiyan Yu a, Xiaomin Hao a, 
Jing Zhang a, Huafei Guo a, Bijun Fang a, Ningyi Yuan a, Xihao Fan a,c, Shuai Zhang a,*, 
Jianning Ding b,** 
a Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, 
213164, Jiangsu, PR China 
b Research Center of Micro/Nano Science and Technology, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, Jiangsu, PR China 
c Fortune Energy Technology Co., Ltd., Suzhou, 215000, Jiangsu, PR China   
H I G H L I G H T S  
G R A P H I C A L  A B S T R A C T  
• Decoration with Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals 
and subsequent reversible degradation. 
• The decoration improves crystal quality 
and suppresses trap state density. 
• Strengthening of the built-in electric 
field 
are 
also 
achieved 
by 
the 
decoration. 
• PSC VOC and moisture stability are 
simultaneously improved.  
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Perovskite solar cells 
CsPbI2Br 
Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals 
Energy loss 
Moisture stability 
A B S T R A C T   
CsPbI2Br perovskites are promising candidates for photovoltaic applications owing to the trade-off between the 
optoelectronic properties and phase stability of cesium-based inorganic perovskites. However, the major short­
comings of CsPbI2Br perovskite solar cells (PSCs), namely energy loss and poor moisture resistance, still need to 
be addressed. In this work, reversible degradation-assisted decoration with Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals (NCs) is used 
to passivate the perovskite/hole transporting material (HTM) interface. Moisture-induced Cs4PbBr6 decompo­
sition products (CsPbBr3 and CsBr) and CsPbI2−xBr1+x (x > 0), generated as a result of halide anion exchange 
between CsPbI2Br and Cs4PbBr6 or its decomposition products, are found to improve the quality of the CsPbI2Br 
film and suppress the trap state density. In addition, the decoration achieves desirable energy-level alignments at 
the CsPbI2Br/HTM interface and raises the Fermi level of the CsPbI2Br film, thereby strengthening the built-in 
electric field between the perovskite and HTM. As a result, the optimized CsPbI2Br PSC has a champion effi­
ciency of 15.52% with an open-circuit voltage of 1.30 V (mean value: 1.29 V). The unencapsulated CsPbI2Br film 
and device both exhibit high air stability (~40% humidity), and the latter retains 90% of its initial efficiency 
after a 500 h aging test.

1. Introduction 
Organic-inorganic hybrid lead halide perovskites are promising next- 
generation photovoltaic (PV) materials [1,2] with a long carrier diffu­
sion length, high carrier mobility and chemical-defect tolerance [3,4]. 
Within more than a decade, the champion power conversion efficiency 
(PCE) of these PV materials-based perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has been 
boosted to a surprising certified value of over 25% [5]. However, 
because of the volatile nature of organic cations (e.g., methylamine and 
formamidine) in hybrid perovskites, these PSCs are characterized by 
thermal instability [6]. To overcome the instability problem due to 
organic cations, a series of inorganic CsPbX3 (X: I, Br, or mixed halide) 
perovskites with good heat resistance properties as well as tolerance to 
oxygen and ultraviolet rays have been developed. Among these CsPbX3 
perovskites, CsPbBr3 possesses good phase stability and the highest 
moisture resistance, but its wide bandgap (~2.3 eV) makes it inappro­
priate for PV applications [7]. On the other hand, Yu et al. reported a 
certified PCE over 20% for CsPbI3, which has a more suitable bandgap 
(~1.7 eV) [8]. Nevertheless, its non-ideal tolerance factor gives rise to a 
spontaneous and rapid transformation from the photoactive α phase to 
the unfavorable δ phase at room temperature, and additional approaches 
such as the introduction of the quantum dots effect, surface passivation 
engineering, and 2D/3D configuration are necessary to stabilize the 
α-CsPbI3 phase [9–11]. 
Considering the trade-off between bandgap and stability, the mixed 
halide CsPbI2Br has attracted intensive attention because of its superior 
phase stability compared with CsPbI3 and narrower bandgap (~1.9 eV) 
than CsPbBr3, which makes it suitable for the front cell of perovskite- 
silicon hybrid tandem applications [12]. However, CsPbI2Br still un­
dergoes a rapid phase transition from the α-to the δ-phase when kept in 
uncontrolled ambient conditions. Humidity is a major factor responsible 
for the α-phase instability of CsPbI2Br [13]. On the other hand, the en­
ergy loss (Eloss) of PSCs based on CsPbI2Br, defined as Eloss = Eg – eVOC 
(Eg, e, and VOC are the bandgap, elementary charge, and open-circuit 
voltage, respectively), is much higher than that of hybrid 
perovskite-based PSCs [7]. Consequently, many efforts have been 
devoted to fabricating high-quality CsPbI2Br films and/or optimizing the 
interface design to increase the phase stability and/or lower Eloss, 
including metal-cation-doping [14–16], CsPbI2Br crystallization control 
[12,16–19], charge transporting layer modification [20,21], surface 
passivation of CsPbI2Br film [7,22], and so on. However, it continues to 
be difficult to simultaneously improve both the stability and VOC. For 
instance, CsPbI2Br PSCs decorated with evaporated CsBr had an 
improved VOC of 1.27 V, but the improved stability against moisture was 
not revealed [7]. All-inorganic CsPbI2Br PSCs prepared by a hot air 
method in coordination with rubidium cation incorporation revealed a 
champion VOC of 1.32 V, but their aging test was carried out for only 
120 h [15]. Even the PSC with a record VOC of 1.43 V maintained only 
~80% of the initial PCE after 400 h aging test in ambient air with low 
relative humidity (RH, 10–20%) [23]. Although PSCs subjected to 
gradient thermal annealing and a green anti-solvent demonstrated 
robustness against moisture (RH: ~30%), the mean VOC only reached 
1.23 V [17]. 
In this work, we utilize the reversible degradation (RD) of CsPbI2Br 
[24] to assist with interface engineering via Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals 
(NCs). Moisture is used to induce the phase transformation of CsPbI2Br 
from α to δ and also to decompose Cs4PbBr6 into CsPbBr3 and CsBr [25]. 
Thermal annealing enables the degradation of CsPbI2Br to be reversed, 
and also makes it possible to prepare CsPbI2−xBr1+x (x > 0) via halide 
anion exchange between CsPbI2Br and Cs4PbBr6 [25] or its decompo­
sition products [7,26,27]. The Br-enriched perovskites (CsPbBr3 and 
CsPbI2−xBr1+x) and CsBr (or CsI1−yBry) can passivate the defects in the 
CsPbI2Br film, and the former further offers higher resistance to mois­
ture. The treatment boosts both the VOC and stability of the PSCs against 
humidity, achieving a champion PCE of 15.52% with a VOC of 1.30 V 
(mean value: 1.29 V). Additionally, the unencapsulated PSC retained 
90% of its initial PCE after aging in air (RH: ~40%) for 500 h. 
2. Experimental 
Materials: Lead(II) bromide (PbBr2, 98%), lead(II) iodide (PbI2, 
99.99%), cesium iodide (CsI, 99.99%), and hole transporting material 
(HTM), 2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis[N,N-di(4-methoxyphenyl)amino]-9,9′-spirobi­
fluorene (spiro-OMeTAD, 99.8%), were purchased from Xi’an p-OLED 
Technology Corp. (China). Anhydrous solvents, chlorobenzene (CB, 
99.5%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), and dimethylformamide 
(DMF, 99.8%), were obtained from Alfa Aesar. 4-tert-butylpyridine 
(96%) 
and 
lithium 
bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide 
(Li-TFSI, 
99.5%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. All other solvents and 
chemicals were received from commercial suppliers without further 
purification. 
Device Fabrication: The process of cleaning Fluorine-doped tin oxide 
(FTO)-coated glass plates as well as the preparation of a dense TiO2 
blocking layer (c-TiO2) on the FTO and the spiro-OMeTAD solution was 
carried out as reported in our previous papers [28,29]. Cs4PbBr6 NCs 
were synthesized according to a published method [25,30] and were 
dispersed in hexane at the desired concentration after centrifugation. 
Experimental procedures and TEM characterizations of the NCs are 
provided in the Supporting Information. 
The control CsPbI2Br film was prepared as follows. The CsPbI2Br 
precursor solution was obtained by dissolving 0.2075 g PbI2, 0.1652 g 
PbBr2, and 0.2338 g CsI in 1 mL of a mixed solvent (DMSO: DMF = 1:4 
(v/v)). The precursor solution was spin-coated onto the c-TiO2 at 1000 
rpm for 10 s, 4000 rpm for 30 s, and 5500 rpm for 25 s, and 100 μL of the 
antisolvent (CB) was added 10 s after commencement of the last spin- 
coating step. Then, the film was placed on hot stages for two-step 
annealing (42 ◦C and 170 ◦C), and the annealing time is controlled to 
be 1 min and 15 min. 
The control CsPbI2Br film was further treated by casting 100 μL of 
the Cs4PbBr6 NC suspension. Ten seconds later, the film was removed 
from the glove box and exposed to ambient air (~30% humidity). After 
ca. 2 min, the film was observed to have undergone slight degradation, 
which was reversed by another annealing step. The film after this 
treatment is denoted as Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br. The control CsPbI2Br 
film treated only by exposure to air and annealing is denoted as RD- 
CsPbI2Br. Finally, the HTM solution was spin-coated onto the perovskite 
films at 5000 rpm for 30 s, and 80 nm-thick Au electrodes were 
deposited on the HTM layer via thermal evaporation. The PSC devices 
based on Cs4PbBr6-RD- and RD-CsPbI2Br are denoted as Cs4PbBr6-RD- 
and RD-PSCs, respectively. 
Characterization: A Shimadzu UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV3600, 
detection wavelength ranging from 3300 to 185 nm) was used to record 
UV–vis spectra. A Bruker diffractometer (D2 PHASER A26-X1) with Cu- 
Kα radiation was used to acquire XRD patterns, and the characteriza­
tions were carried out in glove box. FESEM (Zeiss SUPRA55) was 
employed to characterize the sample morphologies. A source meter 
(Keithley 2400) and a solar simulator (ORIEL Solar 3A–94023A, 
America) were used to record J-V curves. The EQE spectra were 
measured using a solar cell quantum efficiency measurement system 
(QEXL, PV Measurements, Inc.). Steady PL and TRPL decay spectra were 
measured at room temperature using a Horiba DeltaFlex PL system with 
excitation of a 520-nm pulsed laser. dA Zahner IM6ex electrochemical 
workstation was used to carry out EIS measurements (applying 0.9 V 
bias and 10 mV perturbation). HRTEM images were captured using a 
transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100HR). XPS was per­
formed using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III with a monochromatic Al Kα X- 
ray source. 
3. Results and discussion 
The perovskite films were characterized by XRD and UV–vis spec­
trophotometry at different stages of treatment, as shown in Figs. 1 and

S1. The control CsPbI2Br film has three main XRD peaks at 14.6◦, 20.8◦, 
and 29.5◦, assigned to the (100), (110), and (200) planes of the cubic (α) 
phase, respectively (Fig. 1a) [31]. Compared with the control film, the 
other perovskite films, subjected to different treatments, had similar 
XRD patterns, indicating that the purity of the cubic phase of CsPbI2Br is 
greatly retained. Fig. 1b shows the partially enlarged XRD patterns of the 
CsPbI2Br (100) and (200) facets. Dip-coating with the Cs4PbBr6 NCs 
gives rise to a new diffraction peak at 12.5◦, assigned to the (110) planes 
of the Cs4PbBr6 rhombohedral phase (Fig. 1a) [25], but it does not affect 
the diffraction peaks belonging to CsPbI2Br (Fig. 1b). After exposure to 
air, a new diffraction peak appeared at 9.9◦(Fig. 1a), and the CsPbI2Br 
(100) and (200) peaks shifted to a slightly lower angle (~0.05◦) 
(Fig. 1b). The new diffraction peak provides evidence for the generation 
of the CsPbI2Br yellow (δ) phase, and the peak shift corresponds to the 
lattice constant expansion, indicating that moisture partially destroys 
the CsPbI2Br cubic crystal lattice [32]. The surviving Cs4PbBr6 peak 
suggests that the decomposition of Cs4PbBr6 is probably incomplete. 
After annealing, the diffraction peak of the δ-CsPbI2Br phase could no 
longer be detected, in accordance with the reported reversible 
transformation from the α-to the δ-CsPbI2Br phase [24]. Notably, the 
diffraction peak of Cs4PbBr6 also disappeared, and the CsPbI2Br cubic 
(100) and (200) peaks shifted to a slightly higher angle (<0.1◦). Because 
the ionic radius of I−(2.20 Å) is larger than that of Br−(1.96 Å), the 
Br-enriched perovskite, CsPbBr3, and CsPbI2−xBr1+x, are responsible for 
the peak shift [33]. Furthermore, this slight peak shift suggests that the 
CsPbBr3 and CsPbI2−xBr1+x perovskite may only assemble on the 
CsPbI2Br perovskite surface and/or at the perovskite grain boundaries 
[7]. The additional Br−may originate not only from the Cs4PbBr6 
decomposition products (CsBr and CsPbBr3), but also from Cs4PbBr6 
itself, which is regarded as a CsBr-rich structure with good ion-diffusion 
properties [25]. Different annealing temperatures and times in the RD 
process were studied to optimize RD-CsPbI2Br film preparation 
(Table S1, Figs. S1a and b), and annealing at 300 ◦C for 4 min was 
concluded to yield the film with the highest XRD peak intensity. The 
UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1c) also reveal the alterations in the 
CsPbI2Br films after different treatments. It should be noted that storage 
in ambient air weakens the absorbance of the α-CsPbI2Br phase, whereas 
the absorption peak of the δ-CsPbI2Br phase at approximately 400 nm

intensifies [33], demonstrating the transformation from the α-to the

of the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film becomes slightly larger (~0.02 eV) 
than that of the control. The results also indicate the generation of 
Br-enriched perovskites. On the other hand, the RD-CsPbI2Br films have 
the same bandgap as the control (Figs. S1c and d). 
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were con­
ducted for the control and Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br films to further 
explore the impact of RD-assisted Cs4PbBr6 NC decoration on the 
chemical state of the CsPbI2Br films. Fig. 1e and f shows the I 3d and Br 
3d core levels, respectively. Notably, the I 3d spectrum of the Cs4PbBr6- 
RD-CsPbI2Br film exhibits two peaks at 618.6 and 630.1 eV; thus, the 
binding energy is 0.2 eV lower than that of the control (Fig. 1e). The Br 
3d peaks, corresponding to Br−in CsPbI2−xBr1+x (red dotted lines in 
Fig. 1f), reveal the same trend. Furthermore, the two additional peaks on

eV (green dotted lines in Fig. 1f) were assigned to CsBr or CsI1−yBry [34, 
35]. CsI1−yBry may originate from CsBr after halide anion exchange with 
CsPbI2Br. Reportedly, the binding energy of halides is not changed by a 
slight fluctuation in the I/Br ratio of mixed halide perovskites or simple 
physical mixing [36]. Therefore, the origin of the shifts in the binding 
energy is presumably the electrostatic interaction between the Cs cat­
ions of CsBr or CsI1−yBry and halide species in perovskite [37]. 
Different treatment for CsPbI2Br films and PSC device scheme are 
illustrated in Fig. 2a. The morphologies of the different CsPbI2Br films 
were analyzed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy 
(FESEM) to capture images showing top and cross-sectional views, as 
shown in Fig. 2b‒h and S2‒‒4. The control film had a polycrystalline 
grain texture with pinholes and cracks (Fig. 2b and f), and its thickness 
and mean crystal size were measured to be ca. 450 and 480 nm, 
respectively. Following plain RD treatment (Fig. 2a), the pinholes were 
observed to have disappeared, and the mean crystal size to have 
increased (e.g., 690 nm for annealing at 300 ◦C), but cracks still existed 
(Fig. 2c, g, and S2). The dip-coated Cs4PbBr6 NCs were observed to be 
preferentially located at the grain boundaries (Fig. S3) and are usually 
thought to be film flaws and cause poor stability. After RD treatment, the 
NCs were no longer visible, and the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film was 
quite compact with fewer pinholes and shallow cracks (Fig. 2d, e, and 
h). According to Fig. 2f, g, and h, the control, RD- and Cs4PbBr6-RD- 
CsPbI2Br films have similar thickness (ca. 450 nm), indicating that the 
Cs4PbBr6 NC decoration and RD treatment have no obvious effect on 
film thickness. Cs4PbBr6 NCs have been reported to undergo conversion 
into CsPbBr3 NCs by stripping CsBr via reaction with water [25]. The 
ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) 
spectra, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. S5) indicate that the 
Cs4PbBr6 NCs could also be transformed into CsPbBr3 NCs after contact 
with moisture in ambient air. In these characterizations of Fig. S5, RH 
was controlled to be ~60%, and the contacting time was prolonged to 5 
min to acquire enough detection signal belong to CsPbBr3. The decom­
position may fill the vacancies between grains, and CsPbI2−xBr1+x (x >
0), resulting from halide anion exchange between CsPbI2Br and CsBr or 
CsPbBr3, as well as CsPbBr3, are responsible for the increasingly brighter 
grains as the concentration of the Cs4PbBr6 NCs in the suspension in­
creases (Fig. S4), which implies the generation of Br-enriched perovskite 
[38]. 
To further our understanding at the microscopic scale of the evolu­
tion of the crystal structure of the CsPbI2Br films after treatment, the 
films were examined using high-resolution transmission electron mi­
croscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 3a exhibits a typical control film with the lattice 
fringes of the α-CsPbI2Br phase (110) plane according to a measured d- 
spacing of 4.23 Å. The measured d-spacing value matches well with that 
(4.25 Å) obtained from the XRD (110) plane results (Fig. 1a, using 
equation (2)dsinθ = nλ, θ = 10.4◦, λ = 1.54056 Å). For the Cs4PbBr6-RD- 
CsPbI2Br film, a crystal grain boundary and three different lattice fringe 
patterns are clearly observed (Fig. 3b). The lattice fringes enclosed by 
the red boxes (I, II, and III) are distinguished by their lattice parameters 
of 2.50, 3.36, and 3.48 Å, consistent with the CsBr (or CsI1−yBry) (111) 
plane (JCPDS card No. 5-588), α-CsPbBr3 (or CsPbI2−xBr1+x) (111) plane 
(JCPDS card No. 54-0752), and α-CsPbI2Br (111) plane, respectively. 
Fig. S6 also shows three different lattice fringes. Lattice fringes with two 
d-spacings of 3.36 and 4.12 Å are observed in the area demarcated by the 
red box, and were assigned to the α-CsPbBr3 (111) and (110) planes 
(JCPDS card No. 54-0752), respectively. Moreover, near the red box, the 
lattice fringes indicated by the green box have a d-spacing of 4.16 Å, 
which lies between the d-spacings of the CsPbBr3 (4.12 Å) and CsPbI2Br 
(110) planes (4.25 Å), and represent the CsPbI2−xBr1+x region. The 
HRTEM results support the above analysis of the XRD and XPS results. 
RD-assisted Cs4PbBr6 NC decoration indeed generates Br-enriched 
perovskite (i.e., CsPbBr3 and CsPbI2−xBr1+x), and CsBr or CsI1−yBry 
also exists in the perovskite films. 
The effects of RD treatment and decoration with Cs4PbBr6 NCs on the 
photovoltaic parameters and reproducibility of the CsPbI2Br PSCs were 
studied. For plain RD treatment, optimization of the annealing tem­
perature improved the reproducibility of the device performance 
remarkably, with annealing at 300 ◦C yielding the smallest standard 
deviation and the best mean photovoltaic parameters, as shown in 
Fig. S7 and Table S2. However, enhancement of the photovoltaic pa­
rameters was unremarkable, and the champion values of the different 
samples were close to each other. These results are consistent with the 
reported RD features of CsPbI2Br [24]. Fig. S8 summarizes the extent to 
which the photovoltaic performance of Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br-based 
devices is affected by the concentration of the Cs4PbBr6 NC suspensions. 
The results indicate that RD-assisted decoration of the Cs4PbBr6 NCs can 
further boost the photovoltaic performance of the device, and an NC 
concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 yields optimized parameter values. 
Fig. 4a‒d and Table 1 directly compare the photovoltaic parameters of 
the three types of devices under the optimized preparation conditions. 
The mean values of the short-circuit current density (JSC), VOC, and fill 
factor (FF) increased in the order of the control, RD-, and 
Cs4PbBr6-RD-PSCs, and their standard deviations decreased in the same 
order. As a result, the mean PCE increased from 7.79% (the control 
PSCs) to 15.06% (the Cs4PbBr6-RD-PSCs). The enhanced amplitudes of 
the mean JSC, VOC, and FF were calculated to be 12.5%, 27.7%, and 
34.4%, respectively, indicating that the PCE improvement is mainly 
ascribed to the enhancement of the latter two parameters. Therefore, it 
is presumed that more effective carrier transport at the perovskite/HTM 
interface and the lower trap-state density may be the main cause of the 
boosted photovoltaic performance [39]. The enhanced JSC can be 
ascribed to two reasons. On one hand, after the RD treatment and 
Cs4PbBr6 decoration, more compact CsPbI2Br films with fewer pinholes

The current-voltage (J-V) curves of the best performing devices 
belonging to each of the three categories are shown in Fig. 4e. The 
Cs4PbBr6-RD-PSC has a maximum PCE of 15.52%, along with JSC, VOC, 
and FF values of 15.22 mA cm−2, 1.30 V, and 78.22%, respectively, 
which are superior to those of the other two PSCs. The hysteresis effects 
of the three devices were also characterized (Fig. S9). The control PSC 
exhibited largely diminished FF and PCE (Fig. S9a) with the calculated 
hysteresis index (HI) of 21.6% (HI = (PCEreverse – PCEforward)/PCEreverse) 
[41]. The HI values decreased in the order of the control, RD-, and 
Cs4PbBr6-RD-PSCs, reaching a minimum value of 2.9% (Fig. S9c). The 
hysteresis may be suppressed by relieving the charge accumulation and 
imbalanced charge transfer at interfaces, which usually cause enlarged 
J-V hysteresis [41]. EQE spectra were used to confirm the accuracy of 
the J-V results (Fig. 4e). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra 
(Fig. 4f) revealed a significant contribution in the wavelength range of 
300–650 nm and a maximum value at approximately 400 nm. The EQE 
values were consistent with the variation in the JSC (Fig. 4e). The inte­
grated JSC values (12.52, 13.87, and 15.00 mA cm−2 for the control, RD-,

The charge separation and recombination processes of the perovskite 
films on glass substrates were investigated by steady PL and time- 
resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy. The control film 
exhibited a PL peak at 664 nm near its optical-bandgap edge. The PL 
intensity was enhanced dramatically by RD and Cs4PbBr6-RD treatment, 
accompanied by a sequential blue shift of the PL peaks (Fig. 5a). The 
difference in the optical bandgap between the control and Cs4PbBr6-RD- 
CsPbI2Br is negligible (Fig. 1d); therefore, the results can be explained 
by the passivation effect of the Br-enriched perovskites and/or CsBr (or 
CsI1−yBry), which results in fewer intraband traps and suppressed charge 
recombination [42]. The results of the TRPL decay measurements 
(Fig. 5b) were fitted using the following bi-exponential formula, and 
Table 2 summarizes the fitted parameters. 
I(t) = A1e−t/τ1 + A2e−t/τ2

where t is the time after optical excitation, I (t) is the luminescence in­
tensity at t, τ1 and τ2 are the fast and slow transient lifetimes that 
correspond to the surface and bulk properties, respectively, and A1 and 
A2 are coefficients that represent the contributions of the fast and slow 
lifetimes, respectively [43]. In addition, equation (2) was used to 
calculate the average PL decay lifetime (τave): 
τave =

The three TRPL lifetimes are all gradually prolonged in the order of 
the control, RD-, and Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br films, and each lifetime of 
the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film is several times longer than that of the 
control. The results demonstrate that the nonradiative decay rate is 
suppressed, and this is expected to considerably lower the trap and 
defect density after the treatment. 
Fig. S10a‒c show the space charge limited current (SCLC) charac­
terization results for electron-only devices, and the trap density can be 
obtained by filling the trap-filling limit voltage (VTFL) in these figures 
according to equation (S1) in the Supporting Information. The VTFL is 
0.75, 0.58, and 0.30 V, and the corresponding density of the electron 
trap-state was calculated to be 3.48 × 1015, 2.69 × 1015, and 1.39 ×
1015 cm−3 for the control, RD-, and Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br films, 
respectively. The lower trap density confirms that defect passivation by 
the RD-assisted decoration with the Cs4PbBr6 NCs was successful. 
To estimate the charge-transfer kinetics, electrochemical impedance 
spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out by irradiating the 
optimized PSCs with light (AM 1.5 G), as presented in Fig. 6a. An 
equivalent circuit, including an internal series resistance (RS) and a 
charge-transfer resistance (RCT) (Fig. 6a inset), was used to fit the 
Nyquist plots, and the extracted parameters are summarized in Table S3. 
The continuously decreasing RS and RCT indicate decreased charge 
recombination and improved interfacial charge transfer as a result of the 
RD-assisted decoration with the Cs4PbBr6 NCs, which contributed to the 
increased FF and diminished hysteresis of the PSCs (Figs. 4 and S9) [37, 
44]. On the basis of the EIS results, interfacial charge transfer was 
concluded to have improved. This implies that the energy levels of the 
CsPbI2Br film were influenced by the RD-assisted decoration; thus, ul­
traviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were taken. 
As shown in Fig. 6b and c, the valence band maxima (VBM) of the 
control and the optimized Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br films are determined 
to be −5.65 and −5.61 eV according to low kinetic energy cutoff (Ecutoff) 
and high kinetic energy initiation (Ei) (using equation Φ = 21.22 – 
(Ecutoff – Ei)) [45,46], and the conduction band minima (CBM) are 
calculated to be −3.72 and −3.66 eV (using the equation ECBM = EVBM +
Eg), respectively. Because the CsPbBr3 and CsPbI2−xBr1+x perovskites 
may only assemble on the CsPbI2Br perovskite surface and/or at the 
perovskite grain boundaries (based on the slight XRD peak shift 
(Fig. 1b)), and UPS characterization also focuses on solid surface states, 
it is inferred that the depth of the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film is close to 
that of the pure CsPbI2Br film, and its VBM and CBM are similar to those 
of the control film. Therefore, the VBM of the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br 
film and HTM form a cascade alignment, and the CBM of the film forms 
an energy barrier (Fig. 6d), which can enhance hole transport from the 
perovskite to HTM and suppress charge recombination. Moreover, the 
Fermi level of the Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film is raised from −3.95 to 
−3.83 eV (using the equation Ef = 21.22 – Ecutoff), and is closer to its 
CBM than the control film (Fig. 6d), which indicates that the former film 
predominantly has n-type characteristics. Therefore, the built-in electric 
field between the n-type CsPbI2Br and p-type HTM is strengthened, 
resulting in a stronger driving force for photogenerated carrier separa­
tion and a leading VOC of 1.30 V [12]. 
Aging tests were carried out on unencapsulated CsPbI2Br films and 
PSCs in air with relative humidity of approximate 40%. Fig. 7a shows 
photographic images of the CsPbI2Br films after the different forms of 
treatment. The control film undergoes extensive degradation within

only 25 min, as the appearance of the film transforms from dark brown 
to transparent yellow, indicating a phase transformation from α to δ 
[24]. It is noteworthy that RD and Cs4PbBr6-RD treatment can delay the 
degradation, particularly for the optimized Cs4PbBr6-RD-CsPbI2Br film, 
which only shows degradation at the film edge after 20 days of testing. 
The PCE evolution of unencapsulated PSCs over a 500 h aging test is 
shown in Fig. 7b, c, and S11. The control PSC underwent marked PCE 
decay, and its absolute PCE decreased from 10.95 to 3.4%, retaining 
approximately 30% of its initial performance for 500 h. Contrary to this, 
the PCE stability of the RD and Cs4PbBr6-RD PSCs improved consider­
ably. The absolute PCE of the latter only decreased from 15.52 to 
13.97%, retaining 90% of its initial efficiency after 500 h. The improved 
stability of the film and device can be ascribed to the more compact 
perovskite film with fewer pinholes and cracks, larger crystal size, and 
Br-enriched perovskite induced by the RD-assisted decoration of the 
Cs4PbBr6 NCs, which enhances the phase stability of α-CsPbI2Br. 
Furthermore, the trap state passivation and charge-transfer enhance­
ment (Figs. 5 and 6) can also improve the PSC stability [39]. 
4. Conclusions 
In summary, the interface between the CsPbI2Br perovskite and the 
HTM was passivated by RD-assisted decoration of the Cs4PbBr6 NCs. The 
decoration generates CsBr (or CsI1−yBry) and Br-enriched perovskites 
(CsPbBr3 and CsPbI2−xBr1+x) through moisture-induced Cs4PbBr6 
decomposition and subsequent halide anion exchange during annealing. 
Improved crystal quality, suppressed trap state density, enhanced 
moisture resistance of the optimized CsPbI2Br film, and a strengthened 
built-in electric field between the perovskite and HTM were demon­
strated. The unencapsulated device showed a high VOC of 1.30 V, and 
retained 90% of its initial efficiency after a 500 h aging test in air (~40% 
humidity).

Optical properties and mechanical induced phase transition of CsPb2Br5 
and CsPbBr3 nanocrystals
Yuehong Yin a, Yukun Liu b, Guohua Cao a,⁎, Zhaoyue Lv c, Haitao Zong a, Yujie Cheng b,  
Qifan Dong a, Chen Liu a, Ming Li a, Baoqing Zhang b,⁎
a School of Physics and Electronic Information Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China 
b School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, China 
c School of Physics, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China 
a r t i c l e  i n f o
Article history: 
Received 19 December 2022 
Received in revised form 10 February 2023 
Accepted 24 February 2023 
Available online 28 February 2023
Keywords: 
CsPb2Br5 
CsPbBr3 
Structure transition 
Orange emission 
Yellow emission
a b s t r a c t
The Cs-Pb-Br system possesses excellent optical properties and unique phase transition characteristics due 
to soft lattice structures. Herein, a simple and green synthetic method based on self-assembly in Dimethyl 
formamide (DMF) is proposed to prepare CsPb2Br5 nanocrystals (NCs) with typical tetragonal structure, 
which exhibit orange emission and small size around 3.87 nm. Interestingly, the phase transition from 
CsPb2Br5 to CsPbBr3 occurs easily in DMF through mechanical induction, while the metastable mixed-phase 
structural CsPbBr3 NCs with an average size of about 5 nm appear and show bright yellow emission. 
Afterwards, in combination with the supersaturation crystallization method, stable cubic CsPbBr3 NCs with 
13 nm size are obtained and display the effective green emission. The synthesized CsPb2Br5 and steady- 
state CsPbBr3 NCs exhibit good optical stability in solution, while the metastable CsPbBr3 NCs undergo a 
change in emission from yellow to green with increasing time.
© 2023 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. 
1. Introduction
All-inorganic lead halide perovskites nanocrystals (IPNCs) have 
been developed as promising materials for optical applications in the 
fields of lasers, photo detectors and light-emitting diodes [1–3], 
because of their many advantages such as tunable band gap [4], 
amazing tolerance to surface defects [5,6]
and high photo­
luminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) [7,8]. Among them, Cs-Pb-Br 
systems (CPBs) have attracted increasing interest owing to their 
unique optical properties and sensitive ionic structures. CPBs pos­
sess complicated phase structures with three compounds: CsPbBr3, 
CsBr-rich Cs4PbBr6 and PbBr2-rich CsPb2Br5, which have been con­
firmed to demonstrate distinctive characteristics. CsPbBr3 nano­
crystals (NCs) have been reported extremely high green emission 
efficiency with PLQY of 92.3% [9] and amplified spontaneous emis­
sion [10–13]. Meanwhile, CsPb2Br5 material has unique photo­
physical properties, such as increased exciton binding energy, 
reduced fluorescence decay times and notable optical nonlinearity 
[14]. Owing to the similar formation energy, multiphase-coexistence 
(MPC) and phase inversion frequently crop up during the 
preparation of CPBs, which could greatly affect the structure and 
luminescence performance. Nanocrystals of different CPBs compo­
sitions have already been designed by various methods, for example 
solvothermal method [5], colloidal synthesis [13], heat injection 
method [14] and mechanochemical synthesis [15].
The inorganic perovskites of CsPbBr3 synthesized by solution 
method are often accompanied by the products of CsPb2Br5 or 
Cs4PbBr6 under insufficient synthesis conditions [16]. The solvent or 
ligand plays an important role in the phase composition of final 
products. In solution, Pb ions can combine with bromide ions, co­
ordinated solvent molecules or ligand to form a series of PbXn
2−n 
(n ≥ 2, X = Cl, Br, I) polyhedral framework [17,18]. Ding has reported 
that Pb2+ prefers to be surrounded by eight-coordination sites in 
water, and CsPb2Br5 can be transformed from CsPbBr3. The Pb2+ six- 
coordinated crystal (i.e., Cs4PbBr6) can be produced in di­
methylsulfoxide (DMSO) or dimethylformamide (DMF) [16]. More­
over, the inter-conversion of ternary Cs-Pb-Br compounds is 
correlated with physical or chemical treatment conditions, espe­
cially the transition between CsPbBr3 and CsPb2Br5 can usually be 
achieved in different environments [19,20]. Lu has reported that 
orthorhombic CsPbBr3 NCs were prepared by all-solid-state high- 
energy ball milling, and almost entirely transformed into CsPb2Br5 
NCs after soaking in water for 8 h [17]. Pradhan et al. synthesized the 
Cs-Pb-Br system by solvent-free mechanochemical method and

text:
found that water vapor treatment promotes the phase transition 
from optically active orthorhombic CsPbBr3 to inactive tetragonal 
CsPb2Br5 perovskite [15]. The route of crystallization is dominantly 
controlled by thermodynamic or kinetic processes and distinctly 
depends on the crystallization technique. From previous reports, 
CsPbBr3 NCs prepared by mechanical force are prone to phase 
transition in solvents or dispersions. However, whether mechanical 
forces exert a direct effect during the phase transition of IPNCs re­
mains ambiguous and has been rarely reported.
The luminescence properties of CPBs are influenced by many 
factors, especially composition and structure. Most of the reported 
CsPbBr3 NCs exhibit intense green emission and high quantum yield. 
However, CsPb2Br5 NCs present a contradictory topic in terms of 
optical properties. Jiang and co-workers reported that tetragonal 
CsPb2Br5 nanosheets with a large indirect band gap of 2.979 eV show 
PL inactive behavior [21,22]. Dursun et al. demonstrated the intrinsic 
CsPb2Br5 does not display appreciable intrinsic PL [23,24]. On the 
other hand, Wang claimed that the bright green surface emission has 
been observed at the edges of single crystal CsPb2Br5 microplatelets 
with the size of 10 µm. They proposed that polycrystalline phase is 
responsible for the luminescence [25]. Yu et al. found that 200 nm 
CsPb2Br5 NCs synthesized by a facile precipitation reaction display 
the enhanced photoluminescence (PL) in the visible region (512 nm) 
[26]. The tetragonal CsPb2Br5 NCs prepared by the structural tran­
sition from CsPbBr3 NCs present an abrupt increase in green fluor­
escence [17]. Although the green emission was observed from 
CsPb2Br5, the source of luminescence has not been clarified. Zhang 
et al. suggested that the visible emission of CsPb2Br5 nanoparticles 
comes from CsPbBr3, which emerges as a by-product of the ther­
mally induced phase transition between CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 
[15,27]. Several research groups supported that the PL of CsPb2Br5 is 
mainly derived from embedded CsPbBr3 [28,29]. The reported lu­
minescent CsPb2Br5 perovskite compounds are mainly concentrated 
in two-dimensional large-sized nanosheets or microplates (sizes 
from 200 nm to 10 µm). The structural variability in large-size 
samples increases the complexity of the analysis. Preparing small- 
sized CsPb2Br5 nanoparticles with single shape is beneficial for 
probing the emitting sources, but few has been reported so far. 
Another noticeable feature is that tetragonal CsPb2Br5 and cubic 
CsPbBr3 have displayed green emission properties, but there are few 
reports on other wavelengths in the same model.
In this work, we synthesized small-sized tetragonal CsPb2Br5 and 
cubic CsPbBr3 nanocrystals by a simple and green self-assembly 
method 
combined 
with 
mechanical 
force-driven 
approach. 
Multicolor luminescence with high efficiency was obtained, realizing 
orange emission from tetragonal CsPb2Br5 NCs with 3 nm, as well as 
yellow and green luminescence from CsPbBr3 NCs with different 
sizes. The structural and optical properties were systematically in­
vestigated and the phase transition was carefully characterized. Our 
work provides an effective method for discovering the fundamental 
optical properties of CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 NCs and further exploring 
the phase transition process of CPBs.
2. Experimental section
2.1. Preparation of CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 nanocrystals
All the reagents were purchased from Aladdin and used directly 
without further purification. In a typical synthesis of CsPb2Br5 NCs, 
PbBr2 (0.4 mmol) and CsBr (0.4 mmol) were mixed by glass rod in a 
beaker, and then added in 10 mL DMF to dissolve naturally. CsPb2Br5 
NCs were obtained in this colorless solution. The above mixed so­
lution was further stirred by glass rod, and metastable CsPbBr3 NCs 
(M-CsPbBr3 NCs) were gained. Subsequently, oleic acid (OA 1 mL) 
and oleylamine (OAm, 0.5 mL) were added in solution of M-CsPbBr3 
NCs. Under vigorous stirring, 1 mL of the mixed solution was quickly 
added into toluene (10 mL) to achieve the last stable CsPbBr3 NCs (L- 
CsPbBr3 NCs). All samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 
10 min, and then dried in a vacuum at 70 ℃ for 6 h to obtain solid 
powers.
2.2. Characterization
Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 
NCs were conducted by an X-ray diffractmeter (Shimadzu XRD- 
6000, Co Kα radiation, λ = 0.154060 nm). The morphology observa­
tions were performed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) 
and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI 
Talos F200X). The elemental analyses were studied by X-ray pho­
toelectron spectra (XPS) using Escalab 250XI (Thermo Fisher). The 
luminescent properties were recorded by a spectrophotometer 
(Fluorolog-3, HORIBA Scientific). Quinine sulfate and fluorescein 
were used as the standard during the PLQY measurements. The 
images were constructed with a cell phone.
3. Results and discussion
The schematic illustration of detailed preparation of CsPb2Br5 
and CsPbBr3 NCs is exhibited in Fig. 1. PbBr2 and CsBr were dissolved 
in DMF to form a colorless solution with a small amount of un­
dissolved powder at the bottom of the beaker. Self-assembly beha­
vior occurred among the Pb2+, Cs+ and Br- ions based on the reaction 
expressed as:  
CsBr+2PbBr2+DMF
CsPb2Br5·DMF                                          
(1) 
The solution presented a bright orange luminescence under the 
UV irradiation, which was CsPb2Br5 NCs. Then, the mixed solution 
was stirred with a glass rod and remained colorless, but showed a 
yellow luminescence. The results indicated the phase transition was 
produced due to the action of mechanical force, and the reaction 
occurred can be proposed as:  
CsPb2Br5·DMF+CsBr →CsPbBr3·DMF                                         
(2) 
So metastable CsPbBr3 NCs (M-CsPbBr3) were formed in DMF. 
More interestingly, when a small amount of M-CsPbBr3 NCs was put 
into DMF again, the emission changed from yellow to orange, 
meaning that a reversible phase transition existed between CsPb2Br5 
and M-CsPbBr3. Finally, referring to the supersaturated crystal­
lization (SR) method, oleic acid and oleylamine were added to the 
mixed solution, and then a small amount of above solution was put 
into the "poor" toluene solvent. The last stable CsPbBr3 NCs (L- 
CsPbBr3) with bright green emission was obtained. In the present 
work, we analyzed the structure, optical properties and stability of 
Fig. 1. Schematic illustration of the detailed operation of CsPb2Br5 NCs and 
CsPbBr3 NCs.
Y. Yin, Y. Liu, G. Cao et al. 
Journal of Alloys and Compounds 947 (2023) 169439
2

label: related
text:
all samples, and systematically explored the mechanisms of struc­
ture formation and evolution.
3.1. Structural, morphological and compositional characterization
The XRD patterns of three samples are shown in Fig. 2. From the 
curve of CsPb2Br5 NCs, there are obvious characteristic diffraction 
peaks of pure tetragonal perovskite. The diffraction peaks are located 
at 11.6°, 18.1°, 23.9°, 29°, 33.3°, 37.8° and 47.6°, which correspond to 
the (002), (112), (210), (213), (310), (313) and (413) planes of the 
tetragonal CsPb2Br5 (PDF#25–0211), respectively. The synthesized 
CsPb2Br5 NCs have intense and sharp diffraction peaks with no im­
purity peaks detected, indicating a highly ordered structure and 
good crystalline quality under self-assembled conditions. M-CsPbBr3 
NCs displays the apparent diffraction peaks at 15.3°, 21.6°, 30.5°, 
34.5°, 37.8° and 43.9°, corresponding to the (100), (110), (200), (210), 
(211), and (220) planes of cubic CsPbBr3 (PDF#54–0752). Compared 
with pure CsPb2Br5 NCs, three additional diffraction peaks at 
2θ= 11.6°, 29° and 33.3° are observed, which belong to (002), (213) 
and (310) planes of tetragonal CsPb2Br5. The results imply that M- 
CsPbBr3 NCs contains the dual-phase structure of CsPb2Br5 and 
CsPbBr3, and the transition from the tetragonal to cubic phase takes 
place gradually due to the stirring. Therefore, M-CsPbBr3 NCs is in 
the metastable state. L-CsPbBr3 NCs present the diffraction peaks of 
cubic CsPbBr3 structure, and the peaks belonging to M-CsPbBr3 in 
the superimposed blue column are no longer present, implying the 
formation of better cubic crystals.
To further understand the morphology and process of the phase 
transition, TEM and HRTEM were performed for three samples. As 
seen in Fig.3. a1, CsPb2Br5 NCs demonstrate the shape diversity, for 
example, the near-spherical nanoparticles and irregular particles 
have been found. In addition, they present good dispersion and 
uniformity distribution in DMF. HRTEM results show that the in­
terplanar distances are 0.276 and 0.283 nm in Fig. 3a2, which are 
consistent with the crystal plane (310) and (300) of CsPb2Br5, re­
spectively. The particle sizes are mostly around 3 nm, with an 
average crystal size of 3.87 nm (see Fig. 3a3). CsBr and PbBr2 tend to 
form the ionic state, and the solvent molecules or Br ions have 
stronger ion-interactions with Pb (II) to coordinate and form PbBr8
6- 
anion by self-assembly. As a result, CsPb2Br5 NCs are synthesized 
due to an ultra-fast nucleation rate in DMF. The M-CsPbBr3 NCs re­
mains spherical with good dispersion, and the average particle size 
increases to 5.12 nm. It is worth noting that the sizes show an ap­
proximate normal distribution as shown in Fig. 3b2 and b3. The 
lattice spacing is measured to be 0.284 nm, which is in agreement 
with the (200) lattice plane of cubic CsPbBr3. These results imply 
that the phase transition (CsPb2Br5·DMF+CsBr→CsPbBr3·DMF) can 
occur due to stress induction, which accelerates the connection of 
stray Pb2+, Br- and Cs+ ions and impels the growth of cubic CsPbBr3 
NCs. Under the SR, the pure cubic CsPbBr3 NCs are rapidly formed, 
and the average particle size further increases to 13.2 nm (Fig.3c2 
and c3). The clear stripes in HRTEM indicate the enhanced crystal­
linity of CsPbBr3 with a lattice spacing of 0.587 nm, which corre­
sponds exactly to the (100) plane of cubic CsPbBr3. These are 
consistent with the XRD results. In the crystallization process, the 
IPNCs show obviously different morphology and structure due to the 
influence of the ionic bonding state.
X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to in­
vestigate the elemental composition and surface states of the syn­
thesized CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 NCs, as shown in Fig. 4. All samples 
exhibit clearly the signals of Cs, Pb and Br. The three peaks at 724 eV, 
137 eV and 68.3 eV are related to Cs-3d, Pb-4 f and Br-3d, respec­
tively. Quantitative XPS analysis reveals that the ratio of Cs:Pb:Br in 
CsPb2Br5 NCs is 1:1.5:4.5 which is close to the stoichiometry. M- 
CsPbBr3 
NCs 
presents 
clear 
cesium-rich 
composition 
(i.e. 
Cs:Pb:Br=1:0.6:2.6). Furthermore, L-CsPbBr3 NCs displays the in­
creasing bromine contents with the molar ratio of 1:1:3.5, which is 
similar to lead halide of CsPbBr3. It is evident that the tetragonal 
CsPb2Br5 NCs are easily transformed into cubic CsPbBr3 NCs by 
stirring in DMF solution containing residual CsBr, and the crystal 
phase structure is gradually improved and perfected. The crystal 
phase of inorganic perovskites is diverse and prone to change, as 
reported in the literature [20]. More significantly, the crystal nucleus 
of CsPbBr3 is formed firstly, and lots of defects are introduced on the 
surface during the growth process [18]. The Br/Pb ratios of M- 
CsPbBr3 NCs and L-CsPbBr3 NCs are 4.33 and 3.5 respectively, in­
dicating that the metastable CsPbBr3 NCs have more abundant Br 
atoms than steady state CsPbBr3 NCs [16]. For all samples, the HRXPS 
spectra of Cs-3d, Pb-4 f and Br-3d are further described in Fig. 
S1(ESI), where the binding energy of the same element shows a 
slight chemical shift owing to structure differences. For the CsPb2Br5 
NCs, Cs appears two peaks belonging to Cs-3d3/2 and Cs-3d5/2, and 
their binding energies are 737.98 and 724.07 eV respectively. The M- 
CsPbBr3 NCs and L-CsPbBr3 NCs show the shift towards low binding 
energy due to the integrity of crystal structure. The Pb-4 f spectrum 
exhibits two clear peaks at 138 and 143 eV, corresponding to 4f7/2 
and 4f5/2 with a spin-orbit splitting energy of 4.9 eV. The Br-3d 
peaks can be fitted by two peaks with binding energies of 69.2 and 
68.1 eV, which correspond to the inner and surface Br ions. Com­
pared with CsPb2Br5 NCs, the Br elemental values of M-CsPbBr3 NCs 
shift toward lower energies of 69.08 and 68.06 eV, while those of L- 
CsPbBr3 NCs shift toward higher energies of 69.41 and 68.3 eV, as 
shown in Table S1(ESI). The difference in energy might be mainly 
due to the integrity of crystal structure, for example, M-CsPbBr3 is 
the mixed-phase while L-CsPbBr3 is relatively pure cubic phase 
structure. Br ions on the surface play a significant role in the optical 
performance of IPNCs, and the Br-rich environment can passivate the 
electron traps on the surface and improve the luminescence [29].
3.2. Optical characterization
The optical properties of the synthesized products are shown in 
Fig. 5. Three samples display different luminescence characteristics. 
An orange emission is observed for CsPb2Br5 NCs. M-CsPbBr3 NCs 
with 5 nm (less than the exciton Bohr radius of 7 nm) exhibit a 
yellow emission. In contrast, L-CsPbBr3 NCs with an increased dia­
meter of 13 nm present the intense green emission, which is con­
sistent with the literature [30]. The International Commission on 
illumination (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of all samples are cal­
culated and shown in Fig. 5b. The results suggest that the Cs-Pb-Br 
perovskites can emerge with novel optical properties beyond green 
luminescence.
Fig. 2. XRD patterns of CsPb2Br5, M-CsPbBr3 and L-CsPbBr3 NCs. 
Y. Yin, Y. Liu, G. Cao et al. 
Journal of Alloys and Compounds 947 (2023) 169439
3

label: related
text:
The adsorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectra were char­
acterized to explore the optical divergence of three samples, as 
presented in Fig. 5c. Both CsPb2Br5 NCs and M-CsPbBr3 NCs have no 
obvious absorption peaks, and the similar absorption edges are 
displayed at about 340 nm. However, L-CsPbBr3 NCs shows two 
distinct absorption peaks at about 355 nm and 370 nm and an ab­
sorption edge at 420 nm, which are in good agreement with the 
CsPbBr3 NCs prepared under the SR [13]. The steady-state PL spectra 
of three samples exhibit different emission peaks, with CsPb2Br5 NCs 
at 601 nm, M-CsPbBr3 NCs at 559 nm and L-CsPbBr3 NCs at 514 nm. 
In addition, L-CsPbBr3 NCs have a high symmetric band with the 
narrow full width at half maximums (FWHM) of 20 nm due to the 
good crystal structure. It is worth mentioning that the other two 
samples reflect the wide FWHM (64 nm for CsPb2Br5 NCs and 83 nm 
for M-CsPbBr3 NCs). The corresponding PL spectra based on Gaussian 
fitting show two peaks in Fig. S2. For CsPb2Br5 NCs, there are two 
emission peaks at 548 nm and 602 nm with a low percentage of 11% 
for the former and 89% for the latter, implying that CsPb2Br5 NCs 
with small size present orange emission. The M-CsPbBr3 NCs exhibit 
two close emission peaks at 552 nm and 565 nm, with 92% of the 
emission coming from the long wavelength, so the yellow emission 
appears. None of them gives green emission. The PLQY of three 
samples are quite different, which was calculated by relative method 
using fluorescein and quinine sulfate as the reference [31]. The PLQY 
is 23.8% for CsPb2Br5 NCs, 28.7% for M-CsPbBr3 NCs and 61% for L- 
CsPbBr3 NCs. In this work, Br-based IPNCs, either tetragonal CsPb2Br5 
or cubic CsPbBr3, are successfully prepared to achieve fluorescence 
emission other than green.
In order to illustrate the origin of special luminescence, the op­
tical band gap and lifetime are further analyzed. The optical band 
gap (Eg) is obtained on the basis of Tauc plot, which is the intercept 
value of (αhν) 2 versus photon energy hν curve, where α is the

(a3, b3 and c3).
Y. Yin, Y. Liu, G. Cao et al.

absorption coefficient, as shown in Fig. S3. For CsPb2Br5 NCs, the 
wide Eg is estimated to be 3.48 eV. It is considered to originate from 
the layered structure (adjacent Pb2Br5 layers separated by a Cs-layer 
along the c-axis) and the short Pb-Br bonds, as reported by Huang 
Feng et al. [16,22,32]. M-CsPbBr3 NCs show a large Eg value of 
3.73 eV, which is attributed to the quantum size effect caused by 
smaller size than the exciton Bohr radius (∼ 7 nm). On the other 
hand, Eg is formed between two sets of anti-bonding orbitals, so the 
vacancy states residing within the valance band (VB) and conduction 
band (CB) may form the benign shallow defects due to high defect 
tolerance, which may affect the optical performance in the me­
tastable state [33,34]. For L-CsPbBr3 NCs with large size, there are 
two sharp absorption “slopes” located at 3.04 eV and 2.88 eV, re­
spectively. The band gaps may be related to intrinsic and surface 
defects [35].
The time-resolved photoluminescence decay curves were mea­
sured to clarify the luminescence process, as shown in Fig. 6. The 
average lifetime (τAvg) can be calculated by the fitting parameters τ1, 
τ2, τ3, A1, A2 and A3, and the data are shown in the Table 1.

[36,37]. CsPb2Br5 NCs are described by an exponential function with 
a single time constant, and exhibit the shortest PL lifetime of 1.47 ns, 
which might originate from the exciton relaxation [38]. M-CsPbBr3 
NCs show a bi-component decay profile, with a relatively short 
lifetime of 7.34 ns (45.26%) and a long lifetime of 14.54 ns (54.74%). 
Owing to the enrichment of Br, the excitons captured by the shallow 
trap states give rise to the second faster component. Therefore, the 
short and long components can be described by the interaction 
between excitons and phonons and between excitons and defects, 
respectively [37]. The average life is 11.28 ns, which is longer than 
that of CsPb2Br5 NCs. For L-CsPbBr3 NCs, the time-resolved PL mea­
surement reveals a slightly increased lifetime of 3.66 ns (28.42%) for 
the short component, 20.18 ns (71.58%) for the long component, and 
15.49 ns for τAvg. Compared with the M-CsPbBr3 NCs, L-CsPbBr3 NCs 
show a larger proportion of long decay lifetime. The emission of 
CsPbBr3 
NCs 
prepared 
by 
SR 
shows 
typical 
excitonic 
photoluminescence characteristics that are mainly from free and 
bound-excitons. The literatures pointed out that the high exciton 
binding energy would ensure the generation of excitons at room 
temperature and their rapid recombination, thus inducing out­
standing PL properties [13,18]. The process of exciton recombination 
can be easily controlled by fine-tuning the preparation process, 
which reflects different lifetimes [39,40].
3.3. Optical stability
Owing to the strong ionic of bonding and the dynamic nature of 
ligand binding [41], the optical stability of IPNCs is the bottleneck 
limiting their practical application. To assess the optical stability, the 
photographs of three samples were taken with the same mobile 
phone in a darkroom at regular intervals (8 h), and then the light 
intensity distribution of the photographs was simulated and esti­
mated using the image processing tool in Matlab software. When the 
reflectivity is constant, the grayscale value is positively correlative 
with light intensity [42]. Figs. 7 and 8 show the grayscale values for 
different time ranges, and the corresponding photographs are given 
in the insets. Three samples demonstrate significantly different op­
tical changes. CsPb2Br5 NCs and L-CsPbBr3 NCs show a clear decrease 
in luminescence intensity during the first 24 h, with the grayscale 
value of the former dropping from 156 to 115, and the latter from 
163 to 112. Subsequently, the luminescence intensity remains stable

label: related
text:
for a relatively long period of time between 24 and 324 h. Notably, 
there is no significant change in the luminescence color (see the 
insets of Figs. 7a and 7b), meaning that the CsPb2Br5 NCs and L- 
CsPbBr3 NCs possess good optical stability in solution. However, the 
luminescence color of M-CsPbBr3 NCs clearly evolves over time 
(Fig. 9). The sample shows the yellow emission in the first two hours, 
and then becomes to gray white within 15–72 h. With the further 
increasing time to 96 h, the emission changes to cyan and green, and 
then stabilizes in green at 240 h. It is evident that metastable 
CsPbBr3 NCs with rich-defect states are unstable, and the structure 
spontaneously approaches completeness as time increases.
3.4. Structural transition
The synthesis mechanism as well as inter-conversion between 
tetragonal CsPb2Br5 and cubic CsPbBr3 NCs is represented by a 
schematic diagram in Fig. 9. It is clearly shown that CsPb2Br5 can be 
completely converted to the CsPbBr3 NCs under mechanical activa­
tion. It is well known that CsBr and PbBr2 are readily dissolved in 
DMF and form the ions. When lead halide precursor is dissolved in 
aqueous solution (DMF or DMSO), solvent molecules or Br ions can 
be coordinated with Pb (II) to form PbBrn
2−n anion, such as PbBr2, 
PbBr3
− and PbBr4
2−species [16,41]. In the present work, the [PbBr8]6- 
hendecahedral units (eight-coordinate sites) are formed in DMF 
solution, realizing the preparation of tetragonal CsPb2Br5 NCs on the 
basis of self-assembly. Furthermore, the cesium lead halide per­
ovskites are easily redecomposed in DMF due to their soft ionic 
nature [20]. Therefore, CsPb2Br5 NCs are dissolved in polar solvents 
to form free ions under the action of driving force (glass rod mixing). 
As a result, the CsPbBr3 NCs are obtained through crystalline phase 
transition. However, it is noted that their crystal structures are not 
complete. XPS results describe that the element ratio of Cs, Pb and Br 
is 1:0.6:2.6, indicating the existence of Pb and Br defects, as shown in 
the local enlarged simulation diagram. The special yellow emission 
might be related to the abundant defect structure. These results are 
different from the reported green emission of CsPbBr3 NCs.
The metastable CsPbBr3 NCs could gradually be reformed to 
CsPb2Br5 when dissolved into DMF. Fig. S4 shows the absorption and 
PL spectra of CsPb2Br5 NCs obtained before and after the reversible 
phase transition. Their absorption curve displays good coincidence. 
There is no significant shift of the emission peaks except for the 
decrease of PL intensity, meaning that the interatomic bonding in 
CsPbBr3 NCs is weak and structurally metastable. In this process, 
both the tetragonal CsPb2Br5 and metastable CsPbBr3 NCs undergo 
the reversible phase transitions driven by mechanical force in the 
DMF. Finally, the steady-state CsPbBr3 NCs with green emission are 
obtained in combination with the SR method and show a good cubic 
structure.
4. Conclusions
In summary, Br based small size all inorganic perovskite nano­
crystals exhibit multi-wavelength fluorescence emission and re­
versible phase transition in DMF. Pure tetragonal CsPb2Br5 NCs with 
size of 3.87 nm are obtained through a simple and green self-as­
sembly method in DMF, and present the orange emission at 601 nm 
with PLQY of 23.8%. Metastable CsPbBr3 NCs show the increased 
particle size of 5.12 nm and a rare yellow emission at 558 nm with 
PLQY of 28%, which are prepared through the phase-transition from 
tetragonal CsPb2Br5 under mechanical force. Stable cubic CsPbBr3 
NCs with the average size of 13.2 nm can be formed effectively by the 
SR method and exhibit the typical green emission with PLQY of 61%. 
CsPb2Br5 and L-CsPbBr3 NCs possess the good optical stability. More 
interestingly, the small size metastable CsPbBr3 NCs grow sponta­
neously and tend to green emission with the time increasing. The 
variable optical properties of Br-based all-inorganic perovskite NCs 
are manifested, which provides an idea for exploring multicolor 
CPBs. The growth and phase transition mechanism of IPNCs involved 
in multicolor emission is useful to develop the large-scale synthesis 
method for CsPb2Br5 and CsPbBr3 NCs.
