Full paper
Thermal-electrical dual management for efficient and stable wide-bandgap 
perovskite solar cells
Jiangjingzi Feng a, Benlin He a,*
, Zhe Yang a, Jia Li a, Minghao Zhang a, Bangbang Yang a,  
Zhenwei Wu a, Haiyan Chen a, Jialong Duan b, Qunwei Tang b,*
a School of Materials Science and Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, PR China
b Institute of Carbon Neutrality, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, PR China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Carbon-based wide-bandgap perovskite solar 
cells
Thermal-electrical dual management
SMIP/h-BNQDs
Strain release
Self-healing
A B S T R A C T
The efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are critically dependent on the robust charge transport 
and thermal dissipation within the perovskite film. Here, a high-quality perovskite film with thermal and 
electrical conductivity dual enhancement are successfully constructed by using the self-synthesized conductive 
and thermally conductive self-healing supramolecular ionic polymer/hydroxylated boron nitride quantum dots 
(SMIP/h-BNQDs) additives to fabricate high-performance carbon-based PSCs without hole transport layer (HTL). 
Theoretical and experimental results indicate that the introduction of highly thermally conductive SMIP/h- 
BNQDs additives not only delays the crystallization process and passivates defects of perovskite film through 
the strong interactions of coordination and hydrogen bonds formed between them and perovskite components for 
improved film quality, but also accelerates heat transfer and dissipation within the perovskite film, thereby 
reducing the device operating temperature. The electrical conductivity of the perovskite film is also enhanced 
due to the incorporation of conductive SMIP/h-BNQDs, which facilitates charge transport. Moreover, the 
introduction of the self-healing SMIP/h-BNQDs additives significantly releases the residual tensile strain and 
suppresses the phase separation of the wind-bandgap perovskite film, endowing it with self-repair capability 
under mild thermal treatment. Consequently, the carbon-based HTL-free wide-bandgap PSCs fabricated in 
ambient air achieve a remarkable power conversion efficiency of 17.49 % and excellent operational stability 
under continuous illumination. The optimal device maintains 84.8 % of the initial efficiency after being stored 
for 432 h under air conditions of 25 ◦C and 25 % relative humidity, and the efficiency is restored to 93.4 % of the 
initial value after thermal treatment at 60 ◦C, demonstrating outstanding stability and self-healing performance.
1. Introduction
Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have rapidly emerged as a research 
hotspot in the field of photovoltaics due to their excellent optoelectronic 
properties, tunable bandgap, and low-cost solution processability [1–4]. 
In particular, the wide-bandgap (WBG) PSCs have attracted significant 
attention for their high open-circuit voltage (VOC) and potential for 
constructing tandem photovoltaic devices [5–7]. To reduce the 
manufacturing cost and process complexity of PSCs, extensive research 
has focused on the preparation of perovskite films in an ambient air and 
the development of carbon-based hole transport layer-free (HTL-free) 
devices [8,9]. Relevant results have effectively decreased the production 
difficulty and cost of PSCs, accelerating their commercialization process 
[8–10]. However, the rapid crystallization process of liquid-phase 
preparation techniques and the inherent soft lattice characteristics of 
perovskites lead to poor film quality, which severely restricts the 
photovoltaic performance development of air-processed carbon-based 
HTL-free PSCs [11–13].
A large number of additives, such as inorganic salts, small organic 
molecules, and polymers, have been employed to improve the quality of 
perovskite film, thereby effectively enhancing the efficiency and sta­
bility of PSCs [14–19]. Among these additives, the self-healing polymers 
stand out due to their unique molecular structures and strong passiv­
ation capabilities [20–22]. For instance, Finkenauer et al. incorporated a 
self-healing thiourea-triethylene glycol polymer additive to passivate 
defects in the perovskite film by its hydrogen-bonding thiourea and 
ether groups, and also enable film healing property at moderately 
elevated temperatures via synergistic grain growth and solid-state

text:
diffusion processes, which delivered a power conversion efficiency 
(PCE) of 17.4 % and high thermal stability for MAPbI3 PSCs [23]. Zhang 
et al. designed a thermally triggered self-healing polyurethane (PU) as 
additives to repair crystal defects of the perovskite film by the dynamic 
disulfide-exchange reaction under heat treatment at 80 ◦C, which 
enhanced the efficiency and stability of CsPbIBr2 PSCs and allowed the 
PU-modified device’s PCE to recover over 90 % after heat treatment 
[22]. Ge et al. obtained a supramolecular adhesive (AD-23) additive 
with a suitable glass transition temperature and high density of 
hydrogen bond donors and acceptors via random copolymerization of 
acrylamide and n-butyl acrylate, which not only exhibited a supramo­
lecular passivation effect for the perovskite film but also dynamically 
repaired the bending induced damaged areas after heating at 70 ◦C, 
achieving a PCE of 20.50 % for the AD-23 based PSCs [24]. However, the 
self-healing polymers are typically non-conductive, their distribution at 
grain boundaries of the perovskite film inevitably forms potential bar­
rier for carrier transport, leading to a significant decrease in carrier 
mobility and extraction [25,26]. Moreover, the thermal management is 
another critical factor affecting perovskite film quality and device sta­
bility [27–29]. Although the external encapsulation techniques can 
effectively block moisture and oxygen ingress [27,30], the operating 
temperature of PSCs under standard illumination (AM 1.5 G) can reach 
55–65 ◦C [31]. Additionally, the internal thermal generation from car­
rier motion (Joule heating), recombination heat, and photon thermali­
zation effects further exacerbates the temperature rise [32]. Notably, the 
perovskite active layer is the primary source (>90 %) of heat during 
PSCs operation [33,34]. The elevated temperature can induce perov­
skite phase transitions and lattice strain, which further impairs the 
perovskite crystal stability and device performance [27,35,36]. There­
fore, the employment of multifunctional self-healing additives to 
construct a perovskite film with “thermal and electrical conductivity 
dual advantages” is an effective approach to maximize perovskite film 
quality, as well as the efficiency and long-term stability of PSCs.
Herein, a supramolecular ionic polymer (SMIP) with self-healing and 
conductive properties, synthetized by polymerization of citric acid (CA) 
and N,N,N’,N’-tetramethyl-1,3-propane diamine (TMPDA), is self- 
assembled via hydrogen bonding with highly thermally conductive hy­
droxylated boron nitride quantum dots (h-BNQDs) to form a SMIP/h- 
BNQDs composite capable of simultaneous thermal and electrical 
management. This SMIP/h-BNQDs composite is used as an additive to 
assist in the preparation of high-quality WBG perovskite film in air, 
further enhancing the photovoltaic performance of carbon-based HTL- 
free PSCs. The high thermal conductivity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs addi­
tives and their strong interactions with the perovskite through coordi­
nation and hydrogen bonding not only delay the crystallization process 
for improved grain size and crystallinity and passivate defect states, but 
also accelerate thermal dissipation within the perovskite film to reduce 
the device operating temperature. The introduction of self-healing and 
conductive SMIP/h-BNQDs also significantly increases the electrical 
conductivity of perovskite film, endows it with stress release, self- 
healing and phase separation suppression. Consequently, the carbon- 
based HTL-free WBG PSCs with SMIP/h-BNQDs fabricated in ambient 
air achieve an outstanding PCE of 17.49 %. Moreover, the champion 
device retains 94.2 % of the initial efficiency after 238 h of continuous 
illumination operation and 84.8 % of the initial efficiency after storage 
for 432 h in air at 25 ◦C and 25 % relative humidity (RH), demonstrating 
remarkable long-term stability. Further, after thermal treatment at 60 
◦C, the device efficiency is recovered to 93.4 % of the initial value, 
indicating that the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs gives PSCs excellent 
self-healing performance.
2. Results and discussion
In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Fig. S1, compared 
to the reactants CA and TMPDA, the product shows an additional peak at 
about 1582 cm⁻¹ , corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration 
of COO⁻. The broad band between 2200 and 2750 cm⁻¹ is attributed to 
NH+ , indicating that the proton transfer reaction occurs between CA 
and TMPDA to form SMIP. Furthermore, the crystal diffraction peaks of 
CA disappear after reaction with TMPDA, and the resulting product is in 
an amorphous state, as shown in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in 
Fig. S2. Fig. S3 shows that TMPDA transitions from a colorless trans­
parent liquid to a gel state after reaction with CA. These phenomena 
further confirm the successful synthesis of SMIP. The dynamic reversible 
ionic interactions in SMIP endow it with excellent self-healing properties 
[25,37]. As shown in Fig. S4, the uniform N, N-dimethylformamide 
(DMF) dispersion of h-BNQDs emits an intense blue fluorescence under 
UV light irradiation, indicating the typical quantum dot structure of 
h-BNQDs. The appearances of the -OH peak at 3420 cm⁻¹ in the FTIR 
spectrum (Fig. S5) and at 4.22 ppm in the proton nuclear magnetic 
resonance (1H NMR) spectrum (Fig. S6) indicate the successful hy­
droxylation of BNQDs. From the electrostatic potential (ESP) of SMIP 
and h-BNQDs in Fig. 1a, the C=O in SMIP and the -OH in h-BNQDs 
accumulate the opposite electrostatic potentials, enabling them to form 
self-assembly via O⋅⋅⋅H-O hydrogen bonds, as schematically illustrated 
in Fig. 1b. After mixing h-BNQDs with SMIP, the -OH peak of h-BNQDs is 
shifted to low wavenumber (Fig S7), while the C=O peak of SMIP is 
shifted to high wavenumber (Fig. S8), verifying the formation of 
hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups of h-BNQDs and the 
carbonyl groups of SMIP to achieve self-assembly. Meanwhile, the 
inherent high thermal conductivity of h-BNQDs imparts good thermal 
conductivity to the SMIP/h-BNQDs composite [28,38,39]. Additionally, 
as shown in Fig. 1c, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of 
SMIP is mainly localized near the carboxylate groups, while the highest 
occupied molecular Orbital (HOMO) is mainly localized near the amine 
groups. This distinct separation and partial overlap of HOMO and LUMO 
because of delocalization can reduce the intramolecular reorganization 
energy and increase the electronic coupling between adjacent mole­
cules, thus enabling the SMIP molecules high carrier transport charac­
teristics [40]. After incorporation with h-BNQDs, the HOMO and LUMO 
energy levels of SMIP/h-BNQDs exhibit a shift compared with those of 
SMIP (Fig. S9), indicating molecular orbital hybridization between SMIP 
and h-BNQDs due to the hydrogen bond between them [41]. This hy­
bridization can form an interfacial state with intermediate energy to 
boost charge transfer, enhancing the charge transport capability of the 
SMIP/h-BNQDs composite [42].
The ESP of SMIP/h-BNQDs (Fig. S10) indicates that the electron-rich 
aggregations on C=O in SMIP and N-B in h-BNQDs allow them to act as 
Lewis bases to interact with the undercoordinated cations (Pb²⁺) in the 
perovskite, while the electron-deficient regions of NH⁺ in SMIP and -OH 
in h-BNQDs can form hydrogen bonds (N-H/O-H⋅⋅⋅I⁻/Br⁻) with I⁻ and Br⁻ 
in perovskite, restricting halide ions migration. These interactions can 
maintain the stability of the perovskite lattice structure and passivate 
ionic defects to enable effective improvement in device operational ef­
ficiency and lifespan. To confirm the interaction between SMIP/h- 
BNQDs and the perovskite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) 
measurements were performed. As shown in Figs. 1d, 1e, compared to 
the pristine perovskite film, the Pb 4 f₅/₂ and Pb 4 f₇/₂ peaks of the film 
with SMIP/h-BNQDs are shifted to lower binding energy, while the 
characteristic peaks of the I 3d orbitals are shifted positively, indicating 
a strong interaction between the SMIP/h-BNQDs composite and the 
perovskite. Furthermore, Figs. 1f, 1g show that after mixing with PbI₂, 
the characteristic C=O peak of SMIP and the in-plane stretching vibra­
tion peak of B-N in h-BNQDs are shifted to lower wavenumbers, which 
demonstrates the coordination between the electron-rich C=O of SMIP, 
the lone pair electron containing N of h-BNQDs and Pb²⁺. Besides, the 
series of broadband features associated with NH+ in SMIP also exhibit a 
red shift owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds between NH+ and I-, 
which helps to suppress I- migration. The ¹H NMR spectrum in Fig. S11
shows that after mixing SMIP/h-BNQDs with PbI2, the -OH peak is 
shifted downfield, indicating an interaction between the -OH in SMIP/h- 
BNQDs and the halide ions. The changes in chemical shifts of -OH in

2

text:
SMIP/h-BNQDs (Fig. S12) and N-H in FAI (Fig. 1h) fully demonstrate 
that the hydrogen bonds are formed between the additives and FAI, 
which can anchor FA⁺ and prevent its migration to stabilize the perov­
skite framework. These interactions contribute to comprehensive 
passivation of perovskite defects, consistent with the aforementioned 
XPS and ESP results. Based on the above analyses, the utilization of 
SMIP/h-BNQDs with thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity and 
self-healing properties as additives can effectively regulate the growth, 
charge transport and self-repair characteristics of perovskite film.
To investigate the effect of the SMIP/h-BNQDs additives on the 
crystallization process of perovskite film, the two-dimensional (2D) 
ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra with the variation of 
annealing time of perovskite film were detected. As shown in Fig. 2a-c, 
the crystallization rate of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite film is 
significantly delayed compared with that of the pristine and SMIP- 
treated films, because the strong interactions between SMIP/h-BNQDs 
and perovskite introduces a higher energy barrier for perovskite nucle­
ation and growth [43]. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images 
(Fig. 2d-f) show that after the addition of SMIP/h-BNQDs, the grain size 
of the perovskite film is increased significantly and uniformly, which is 
closely related to its slow crystallization rate and the uniform heat dis­
tribution induced by the highly thermally conductive h-BNQDs. 
Furthermore, the crystallinity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite 
film is improved, as confirmed by the enhanced intensity of the crystal 
plane diffraction peaks (Fig. S13). The positions of the characteristic 
peaks before and after the introduction of additives remain unchanged, 
indicating that the additives do not enter the perovskite lattice but reside 
at the grain boundaries. This facilitates the passivation of defects at the 
grain boundaries and hinders the migration of degrading ions along the 
boundaries, 
effectively 
reducing 
the 
defect 
states 
of 
the 
additives-treated perovskite film [44]. Compared with the pristine film, 
the SMIP and SMIP/h-BNQDs treated perovskite films exhibit an 
enhanced absorption intensity (Fig. S14), which is attributed to their 
improved quality. This is consistent with the SEM and X-ray diffraction 
(XRD) results and contributes to the generation of more photogenerated 
electrons to improve the device short-circuit current density (JSC).
From the atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. 2g-i, the 
surface roughness of the perovskite film with SMIP/h-BNQDs is 
decreased compared with that of the pristine and SMIP-treated ones. 
This indicates that the film with SMIP/h-BNQDs exhibits a more uniform 
and even grain distribution, which is beneficial for enhancing the 
interfacial contact to promote carrier transport and extraction. The 
conductive atomic force microscopy (c-AFM) images in Fig. 2j-l and the 
current distribution in Fig. S15 show that compared with the pristine

text:
and SMIP-treated films, the average current of the SMIP/h-BNQDs- 
treated film is increased, indicating smooth carrier transport 
throughout the film. This is due to the electronic coupling effect of 
SMIP/h-BNQDs forming continuous carrier transfer pathways within the 
perovskite film, which enhances charge transport. Besides, the SMIP/h- 
BNQDs treatment significantly increases the grain size and crystallinity 
of the perovskite film and passivates defect states, which also promotes 
the acceleration of carrier transport. As shown in Fig. S16, the electrical 
conductivity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated film is increased signifi­
cantly, consistent with the c-AFM results, which facilitates charge 
transfer to reduce charge accumulation and improve device efficiency. 
Fig. 2 m summarizes the action mechanism of the SMIP/h-BNQDs ad­
ditives during the growth process of perovskite film. The incorporation 
of the thermally and electrically conductive SMIP/h-BNQDs additives 
effectively prolongs the crystallization process and promotes the uni­
form growth of perovskite film, resulting in film with large grain size and 
low residual strain. Meanwhile, the SMIP/h-BNQDs distributed at grain 
boundaries passivate defect states and accelerate charge transport, their 
flexible molecular chains and reversible ionic interactions impart stress 
release and self-healing properties to the perovskite film. Additionally, 
Fig. 2. 2D UV–vis spectra of (a) pristine, (b) SMIP-treated and (c) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films during the annealing process. SEM images of (d) pristine, 
(e) SMIP-treated and (f) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films. AFM images of (g) pristine, (h) SMIP-treated and (i) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films. c-AFM 
images of (j) pristine, (k) SMIP-treated and (l) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films. (m) Schematic diagram of the action mechanism of SMIP/h-BNQDs additives.
J. Feng et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
Nano Energy 148 (2026) 111696 
4

label: related
text:
the high thermal conductivity of SMIP/h-BNQDs facilitates the dissi­
pation of heat accumulated in the active layer during device operation, 
which is beneficial for enhancing PSCs stability.
The grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) measurements were 
employed to study the effect of SMIP/h-BNQDs addition on the residual 
strain in the perovskite film. As shown in Fig. 3a-c, when the incident 
angle ψ gradually increases from 0◦to 45◦, the diffraction peak position 
of the (100) crystal plane of the pristine perovskite film gradually shifts 
to lower angles, indicating the presence of significant tensile stress 
within the perovskite film. In contrast, after introducing SMIP and 
SMIP/h-BNQDs additives, the shift magnitude of the (100) diffraction 
peak is reduced, especially for SMIP/h-BNQDs additives, indicating 
effective release of residual stress in the perovskite film. Based on the 2θ- 
sin²(ψ) linear relationship (Fig. S17) and the formula: σ =
−
E
2(1+ν)
π
180 cotθ0
∂(2θ)
∂sin2ψ, the residual strain (σ) in the perovskite film can be 
quantitatively calculated, where E and ν represent the Young's modulus 
and Poisson's ratio of the perovskite film, respectively, and θ0 represents 
half the diffraction angle of the stress-free perovskite film [45]. The 
calculations indicate that the residual strain of the pristine film is 
−72.32 MPa, whereas the residual strains of the perovskite films treated 
with SMIP and SMIP/h-BNQDs are decreased to −46.00 and −6.40 MPa 
respectively, which is beneficial for suppressing ion migration and trap 
states formation. This is primarily because the introduction of 
SMIP/h-BNQDs not only allows the flexible polymer chains to act as a 
buffer at the grain boundaries to dissipate the residual stress generated 
during crystal growth through molecular chain conformational adjust­
ments, but also enhances the thermal conductivity of the treated 
perovskite film to reduce thermal stress caused by temperature gradients 
and thus lowers the residual stress. Additionally, the phase segregation 
of WBG perovskite under light and thermal stress is a major factor for 
device performance degradation. As shown in Fig. 3d-f and Fig. S18, 
compared 
to 
the 
pristine 
and 
SMIP-treated 
films, 
the 
SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite film shows significantly reduced 
blue-shift and intensity decrease of the steady-state photoluminescence 
(PL) peak with prolonged continuous irradiation, indicating suppressed 
phase separation and improved film stability. This is mainly attributed 
to the physical barrier effect of the SMIP/h-BNQDs polymer chains and 
their interactions with perovskite ions, as well as the release of residual 
Fig. 3. GIXRD patterns of (a) pristine, (b) SMIP-treated and (c) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films. Time-dependent PL spectra of (d) pristine, (e) SMIP-treated 
and (f) SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films under illumination. (g) Steady-state PL spectra and (h) TRPL spectra of different perovskite films on glass substrates. 
(i) Dark J-V curves of hole-only devices based on different perovskite films.
J. Feng et al.

5

label: related
text:
stress in the treated perovskite film.
From steady-state PL spectra (Fig. 3g), the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated 
perovskite film exhibits a significantly increased and blue-shifted 
emission peak, which implies an effective passivation of the defective 
states and suppression of non-radiative recombination in the perovskite 
film. In the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra (Fig. 3h), 
the average carrier lifetime of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite 
films is significantly extended (Table S1), which crosses with the PL 
spectra results to confirm that the treatment of SMIP/h-BNQDs effec­
tively reduces the non-radiative recombination in the perovskite film, 
thus increasing the lifetime of carriers for efficient charge transport and 
transfer. To quantitatively analyze the trap state density (Nt), the space- 
charge-limited-current (SCLC) characterization was used to test the 
hole-only devices [46,47], as shown in Fig. 3i. After calculation, the Nt 
values of the pristine, SMIP-treated and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perov­
skite films are 7.59 × 10 ¹ ⁵, 6.41 × 10 ¹ ⁵, and 5.56 × 10 ¹ ⁵ cm⁻³ , 
respectively (Table S2). Additionally, according to the J¹/²-V curves 
(Fig. S19), the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated film exhibits a significantly 
improved hole mobility (1.66 ×10⁻² cm² V⁻¹ s⁻¹) compared to the pris­
tine (7.44 ×10⁻³ cm² V⁻¹ s⁻¹) and SMIP-treated (9.87 ×10⁻³ cm² V⁻¹ s⁻¹) 
films. These confirm that the SMIP/h-BNQDs treatment reduces the 
defect states and enhances the carrier transport in the perovskite film, 
which can effectively suppress the non-radiative recombination to 
improve fill factor (FF) and VOC of the corresponding devices.
To investigate the effect of the SMIP/h-BNQDs additives on device 
photovoltaic performance, the carbon-based HTL-free PSCs with a FTO/ 
SnO₂/perovskite/carbon structure were assembled in air, as the illus­
tration shown in Fig. 4a. The current density-voltage (J-V) curves of the 
best devices are shown in Fig. 4a after optimizing the amount of SMIP/h- 
BNQDs additives (Fig. S20). The corresponding photovoltaic parameters 
and their comparison are listed in Table S3 and Fig. 4b, respectively. 
Compared with the original device with a PCE of 12.38 % (VOC =
1.132 V, FF = 60.57 %, JSC = 18.05 mA cm−2) and the SMIP-treated 
device with a PCE of 15.69 % (VOC = 1.184 V, FF = 70.81 %, JSC =
18.71 mA cm−2), the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated device achieves a signifi­
cantly increased PCE of 17.49 % (VOC = 1.206 V, FF = 74.40 %, JSC =
19.49 mA cm−2). This efficiency represents a remarkable improvement 
in comparison with the PCEs of the current air-processed state-of-the-art 
PSCs with the similar structure [48–50], and is also an outstanding value 
among the PCEs of other carbon-based PSCs (Table S4). The phenome­
non fully demonstrates the distinct superiority of the SMIP/h-BNQDs 
additives on enhancing PSCs performance. Apparently, the efficiency 
improvement stems from the increase in VOC, JSC and FF primarily due to 
the reduced defects-dominated non-radiative recombination and rapid 
carrier transport in the device after the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs. 
And these four photovoltaic parameters show a high reproducibility 
(Fig. S21). The integrated JSC values from the external quantum effi­
ciency (EQE) spectra (Fig. 4c) and the current density and efficiency 
from the steady-state output curves (Fig. 4d) are consistent with the J-V 
curves results, verifying the reliability of the performance improvement 
Fig. 4. (a) J-V curves, (b) Radar chart of photovoltaic parameters, (c) EQE spectra, (d) Steady-state output curves, (e) Mott-Schottky curves, (f) Nyquist plots, (g) VOC 
and (h) JSC dependence on light intensity, (i) Dark J-V curves of different PSCs.
J. Feng et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
Nano Energy 148 (2026) 111696 
6

label: related
text:
in the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated device.
To explore in detail the effect of additives on the charge transport 
behavior in devices, a series of electrochemical characterizations were 
conducted. The Mott-Schottky curves in Fig. 4e show that with the 
introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs additives, the built-in potential (Vbi) of 
the PSCs is increased from 0.988 V of the original device and 1.041 V of 
the SMIP-treated device to 1.167 V, indicating a greater driving force in 
the former PSCs to accelerate charge separation and extraction. From 
the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 4f), the incor­
poration of SMIP/h-BNQDs significantly increases the charge recombi­
nation resistance (Rrec) of the device compared with the pristine and 
SMIP-treated PSCs (Table S5), indicating an enhanced resistance to 
charge recombination because of reduced defect states in the former 
device. Meanwhile, the series resistance (Rs) of the PSCs is decreased 
after the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs benefiting from the increased 
conductivity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite film, which in­
dicates a rapid carrier transport throughout the device to improve FF. 
Subsequently, the carrier recombination behavior within the devices 
was studied using the dependence of VOC and JSC on incident light in­
tensity (I) (Figs. 4g, 4h) [51,52]. With the addition of SMIP/h-BNQDs, 
the ideality factor (n) related to monomolecular recombination is 
decreased (Fig. 4g), and the α value related to bimolecular recombina­
tion is increased (Fig. 4h), indicating reduced trap-assisted non-­
radiative recombination and electron-hole radiative recombination. 
This is closely related to the lowest Nt and highest Vbi of the 
SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite film and device, respectively. 
Furthermore, the lower leakage current density (Fig. 4i), slower VOC 
decay 
and 
longer 
electron 
lifetime 
(Fig. 
S22) 
of 
the 
SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated device than that of other PSCs also indicate the 
decreased charge recombination inside the former device, consistent 
with the VOC (JSC)-I results. Evidently, the above characterizations 
demonstrate that the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs additives sup­
presses charge recombination and therefore promotes the enhancement 
of PSCs efficiency.
The excellent thermal conductivity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs additives 
assists in the preparation of high-quality perovskite film by optimizing 
the crystallization process, and also can accelerate heat dissipation of 
the perovskite film to effectively suppress thermal accumulation and 
enhance the long-term operational stability of PSCs. From the temper­
ature variation curves of the functional layers in carbon-based PSCs 
under illumination (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW cm−2) over time (Fig. S23), the 
environmental and functional layers temperatures reach equilibrium 
Fig. 5. (a) Time-dependent temperature evolution of pristine, SMIP-treated and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films in devices under 1-sun illumination (AM 
1.5 G, 100 mW cm⁻²) in air. (b) Real-time temperature tracking of original, SMIP-treated and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated PSCs during cooling test. (c) SPO stability of 
different PSCs operating under bias at maximum power output point at 25 ◦C and 25 % RH in air. (d) Normalized PCE decay of different unencapsulated PSCs stored 
in ambient air at 25 ◦C and 25 % RH. (e) Optical microscope images of pristine, SMIP-treated and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films during self-healing at 
60 ◦C.
J. Feng et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
Nano Energy 148 (2026) 111696 
7

label: related
text:
after approximately 400 s illumination, with the perovskite layer tem­
perature peaking at about 45 ◦C. This indicates that the perovskite layer 
exhibits the most photothermal effect during PSCs operation. Therefore, 
the improvement of the thermal conductivity of the perovskite film is 
beneficial for suppressing heat-induced ion migration and crystal 
decomposition to enhance device stability. As shown in Fig. 5a, 
compared to the pristine (45 ◦C) and SMIP-treated (44 ◦C) perovskite 
films, the operating temperature of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated film is 
reduced to 39 ◦C. This is because the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs 
facilitates the rapid transfer of heat within the perovskite film to the 
adjacent layers, followed by efficient thermal exchange with the envi­
ronment. The higher thermal conductivity of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated 
perovskite film (0.693 W m−1 K−1) than 0.356 W m−1 K−1 of the SMIP- 
treated film and 0.301 W m−1 K−1 of the pristine one (Fig. S24) directly 
indicates an improved thermal conduction of the former film. Addi­
tionally, the infrared thermal images of perovskite films naturally 
cooling from 90 ◦C to room temperature (Fig. S25) show that the SMIP/ 
h-BNQDs-treated film exhibits uniform and rapid color change 
compared to the pristine and SMIP-treated films. This cross-corroborates 
that the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs promotes thermal conduction 
within the perovskite film, thereby accelerating its temperature 
decrease. Correspondingly, the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated device shows a 
faster temperature decline than other PSCs (Fig. 5b), cooling to room 
temperature in about 175 s. These phenomena indicate that improving 
the thermal dissipation of perovskite film helps reduce the device 
operating temperature for high stability. As shown in Fig. 5c, under 
continuous illumination (AM 1.5 G) and steady-state output (SPO) at 
T = 25 ◦C, RH = 25 % in air for 238 h, the unencapsulated carbon-based 
PSCs with SMIP/h-BNQDs retain 94.2 % of the initial efficiency, the 
SMIP-treated device retains 90.4 % of the initial PCE after 137 h of 
illumination, while the PCE of the original device is dropped to 90.2 % 
of the initial value after only 71 h of illumination. This demonstrates a 
significantly improved operational stability of PSCs under continuous 
illumination after the introduction of SMIP/h-BNQDs additives.
Furthermore, the unencapsulated SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated carbon- 
based PSCs also exhibit superior storage stability at T = 25 ◦C, RH 
= 25 %, and demonstrate good self-healing performance after thermal 
treatment at 60 ◦C. As shown in Fig. 5d and Fig. S26, after aging for 
432 h, the photovoltaic parameters (JSC, VOC, FF, PCE) of the device with 
SMIP/h-BNQDs show a less attenuation with an efficiency retention of 
84.8 %, which is much higher than 77.8 % retention for the SMIP- 
treated PSCs and 60.0 % retention for the pristine PSCs. This is not 
only related to the improved quality of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated 
perovskite film, but also benefits from its high-quality induced supe­
rior hydrophobicity (as evidenced by the larger water contact angle, 
Fig. S27), which impedes moisture penetration to inhibit perovskite 
crystal degradation. Notably, after aging for 432 h and subsequent 
thermal annealing at 60 ◦C for 60 min, the PCEs of the SMIP-treated and 
SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated PSCs are recovered to 84.3 % and 93.4 % of 
their initial efficiencies, respectively, while the efficiency of the original 
device is changed slightly. This indicates the outstanding self-healing 
performance of the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated device, attributed to the 
excellent self-healing property of the SMIP/h-BNQDs additives. As 
shown in Fig. S28, compared to SMIP film (150 s), the SMIP/h-BNQDs 
film show complete cracks recovery after 120 s of heat treatment due 
to its high thermal conductivity induced uniform heat distribution 
within the film, which enhances the self-healing characteristics. Corre­
spondingly, the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite film exhibits fast and 
good self-repair capability, as shown in Fig. 5e. To reveal the micro­
scopic repair of perovskite film, the SEM images of the pristine, SMIP- 
treated and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated perovskite films deposited on flex­
ible substrates after bending and healing are characterized and shown in 
Fig. S29. Apparently, the pristine perovskite film shows more grain 
boundary cracks both after bending and healing (Fig. S29a), whereas the 
SMIP-treated (Fig. S29b) and SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated films exhibit fewer 
grain boundary cracks after bending and crack repair after healing, 
especially for the SMIP/h-BNQDs-treated film (Fig. S29c). These ob­
servations intuitively demonstrate that the introduction of self-healing 
SMIP/h-BNQDs additives enables the perovskite film to achieve good 
self-repair. The intrinsic self-repair mechanism is due to the presence of 
dynamic reversible ionic interactions in SMIP, which are formed by the 
aggregation of anions and cations in the polymer through strong elec­
trostatic interactions and exhibit temperature-dependent dynamic 
reversibility. When grain boundary cracks are formed in the perovskite 
film, the SMIP network distributed at these locations is disrupted. The 
mild thermal treatment weakens the electrostatic interaction between 
cations and anions in SMIP, causing ions to dissociate from their original 
ionic interactions and migrate to the crack interface. This process pro­
vides charge compensation and forms new dynamic reversible ionic 
interactions. Subsequently, the ions aggregation regions in SMIP un­
dergo reorganization and relaxation, achieving the repair of grain 
boundary cracks in perovskite film [53–55]. The incorporation of 
h-BNQDs with SMIP promotes heat transfer within the perovskite film, 
accelerating its self-healing process. These results indicate that intro­
ducing SMIP/h-BNQDs additives with excellent self-healing property 
can effectively repair microcracks and defects formed in the perovskite 
film during device operation, achieving long-term maintenance of PSCs 
efficiency.
3. Conclusions
In summary, the self-healing SMIP/h-BNQDs composite additives are 
synthesized to successfully construct a high-quality perovskite film with 
dual enhancement of thermal and electrical conductivity, which effec­
tively improves the performance of carbon-based HTL-free PSCs. The 
results indicate that the incorporation of the highly thermally conduc­
tive SMIP/h-BNQDs additives not only retards the crystallization process 
of the perovskite film, enabling the preparation of high-crystallinity film 
with low defect states, but also accelerates heat transfer and dissipation 
within the film to reduce the device operating temperature. Further­
more, the highly conductive and self-healing SMIP/h-BNQDs additives 
also improve carrier transport, release the residual tensile strain, sup­
press phase segregation within the perovskite film and endow it with 
self-repair capability under mild thermal treatment. Based on these 
synergistic effects, the carbon-based HTL-free WBG PSCs with SMIP/h- 
BNQDs fabricated in air achieve an excellent PCE of 17.49 %, superior 
to the 12.38 % efficiency of the original device, and exhibit outstanding 
operational stability under continuous illumination. The device retains 
84.8 % of the initial efficiency after storage for 432 h in an air envi­
ronment of 25 ◦C and 25 % RH, and the efficiency is recovered to 93.4 % 
of the initial value after thermal treatment at 60 ◦C, fully demonstrating 
the effective enhancement of stability and self-healing performance of 
the PSCs by introducing SMIP/h-BNQDs additives. This work provides 
valuable insights for the selection and design of multifunctional addi­
tives to improve the efficiency and stability of PSCs.

RESEARCH ARTICLE
www.afm-journal.de
Modulating Crystallization of Perovskite Thin Films via
Zero-Dimensional Cs4PbBr6 Nanocrystals
Haodan Shi, Junyi Huang, Tianyu Sun, Haixuan Yu, Yong Hu, Xiongjie Li, Xiaoting Ma,
Wanpeng Yang, Zhirong Liu, Lei Wang, Bing Hu, Yan Shen, and Mingkui Wang*
Formamidinium lead iodide perovskite and its derivatives, such as
FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 (band gap ≈1.56 eV), are promising for use in solar
cells. However, uncontrolled growth and transformation of perovskite (111)
facets during the crystallization process poses a challenge for the production
of high-eﬃciency and long-term stability devices. Here, a zero-dimensional
perovskite decoration method is shown to improve the facet regularity of
FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.053 ﬁlms by suppressing the formation of (111) facets in the
perovskite crystallization process. Due to its unique crystal structure, the
zero-dimensional Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 (a composite of phases Cs4PbBr6 and
CsPbBr3 with Cs4PbBr6 as the major phase) nanocrystal acts as a pinning
particle, i.e., as a spatial obstacle, to prevent the uncontrolled growth of
speciﬁc crystal facets during crystallization. The FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3
perovskite decorated with Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 improves power conversion
eﬃciency to 25.52% in standard test conditions with high open circuit voltage
of 1.177 V. The encapsulated devices maintain more than 93.8% of their
original eﬃciency after exposure to 1500 h of continuous 1-sun illumination
under Maximum Power Point Tracking conditions at 50–55 °C.
1. Introduction
Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have experienced a rapid increase
in power conversion eﬃciency (PCE) over the last decade.[1–5]
Among the diﬀerent perovskite compounds with ABX3 struc-
ture (A: FA+, MA+, Cs+, etc. B: Pb2+, Sn2+, etc. X: Cl–, Br–, I–,
etc.), formamidinium-based lead iodide perovskite is one of the
H. Shi, J. Huang, T. Sun, H. Yu, Y. Hu, X. Li, X. Ma, W. Yang, Z. Liu,
L. Wang, B. Hu, Y. Shen, M. Wang
Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics
School of Optoelectronic Science and Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology
1037 Luoyu Road, Wuhan, Hubei 430074, P. R. China
E-mail: mingkui.wang@mail.hust.edu.cn
L.Wang,B.Hu
Wuhan Hero Optoelectronics Technology Co.,LTD
6 Huanglongshan North Road,East LakeHigh-Tech Development Zone,
Wuhan, Hubei,P.R.China
M.Wang
Optics Valley Laboratory
Wuhan, Hubei 430074,P.R.China
The ORCID identiﬁcation number(s) for the author(s) of this article
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202508064
DOI: 10.1002/adfm.202508064
most promising candidates due to its
appropriate bandgap close to the Shockley-
Queisser
limit
(1.34
eV).[6,7]
However,
the relatively large size of the formami-
dinium cation (FA+) means that these
perovskite ﬁlms are thermodynamically
metastable at room temperature. Further-
more, the (111) facets of cubic 𝛼-phase
FA-based perovskites tend to transform
from corner-sharing octahedral structures
to
face-sharing
octahedral
domains,[8,9]
leading to a higher electrostatic repulsion
among the B-site cations.[10,11] One way
to
alleviate
this
electrostatic
repulsion
is to form anion vacancies within the
face-sharing octahedra. When these cubic
(111) solid solutions, which contain a high
density of iodine vacancies, directly or
indirectly connect to the charge transport
layer via grain boundaries, they can provide
channels for the migration of ions. These
mobile ions accumulate at the heterojunc-
tional interface, preventing the transport of
charge carriers and leading to a pronounced
hysteresis of the J-V characterization.[12–14] The cubic (111) facets
that terminate within the grains can not only contain point de-
fects but also produce stacking faults.[15] This could reduce the
valence of the B-site cations and increase the separation of ad-
jacent B-B cations,[16,17] which leads to spontaneous transforma-
tion from the cubic (𝛼) phase to the hexagonal (2H) phase, i.e., the
yellow inactive perovskite phase.[18] This unstable phase further
transforms into PbI2 during the subsequent high-temperature
annealing (100 °C for 1 h), which reduces the performance
of devices.[19] For the perovskite system with the composition
FAxMAyCs1-x-yPbI3 (x = 0.5–1, y = 0–0.5), the density of (111)
stacking faults increases with FA+ content. In particular, when
x = 0.85–1 and y = 0–0.15, the total surface area of cubic (111)
stacking faults in FAxMAyCs1-x-yPbI3 is ≈2 up to 25 times that
of the grain boundary surface area.[12] The core of a stacking
fault has dangling bonds that can act as centers of nonradia-
tive recombination.[20] Therefore, addressing the defect issues
caused by the (111) facets during the crystallization process and
further improving the stability of FAxMAyCs1-x-yPbI3 (x = 0.5–1,
y = 0–0.5) is of signiﬁcant importance for their practical applica-
tion.
Various strategies have been developed to passivate and
stabilize the FAxMAyCs1-x-yPbI3 (x = 0.5–1, y = 0–0.5) per-
ovskite ﬁlms. Additive engineering stands out among the

text:
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.afm-journal.de
Figure 1. HRTEM images of the (a) Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals (scale bar, 20 nm), and (b) the target thin ﬁlm micro-area (scale bar, 3 nm); In situ
XRD patterns of the (c) control and (d) target thin ﬁlms during the annealing process at 100 °C; Surface SEM images of the (e) control and (f) target
thin ﬁlm (scale bar, 1 μm).
others for its ability to modulate crystallization and growth
kinetics, to reduce defect density, and to control the dynamics
of charge carrier transport behavior.[21–23] Additives include or-
ganic molecules,[24] polymers,[25] and nanocrystals.[26] In partic-
ular, perovskite nanocrystals have a clear advantage in the opti-
mization of PSCs because their component and lattice structures
are similar to those of the bulk perovskite matrix. Introduction
of nanocrystals can modify the perovskite matrix by optimizing
the energy level,[27–32] control of nucleation and growth,[33–40] and
synergistic passivation.[41,42] In solution-processed perovskite
thin ﬁlm, it is noteworthy that stable perovskite nanocrystals
based on cubic or orthorhombic bromides, which are commonly
used as modiﬁers, can experience ion exchange and ion uptake
within matrix ﬁlms with similar structures.[43,44] Undoubtedly, a
high Br content in the active layer widens the bandgap and thus
aﬀects the photocurrent of the device.[45] In order to achieve high-
performance PSCs, it is necessary to identify appropriate per-
ovskite nanocrystal additives that can function as modiﬁers for
ﬁlm growth and defect removal while maintaining the narrow
bandgap of FAxMAyCs1-x-yPbI3.
In this study, we have introduced a new method to mod-
ify and control the crystallization of perovskite thin ﬁlm us-
ing zero-dimensional (0D) nanocrystals. During the crystalliza-
tion of FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 thin ﬁlms, 0D Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals (a composite of phases Cs4PbBr6 and CsPbBr3
with Cs4PbBr6 as the major phase) suppress the formation
of cubic (111) facets and their transformation into the 2H
phase and PbI2, thereby promoting the growth of a regular per-
ovskite crystal structure with large grains. The resulting PSC
using FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 perovskite with Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals can achieve an open-circuit voltage of 1.177 V, which
is a 4.16% increase compared to the unmodiﬁed one. Moreover,
this modiﬁcation method can be easily extended to thin ﬁlms pre-
pared by the anti-solvent spin-coating method on a 5 × 5 cm2
substrate.
2. Results and Discussion
The characterization of optical images, XRD pattern, UV–vis
absorption and emission spectroscopy, and photoluminescence
quantum yield in Figure S1 (Supporting Information) veri-
ﬁes that well-passivated 0D Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals are
successfully synthesized with a microemulsion method.[46] De-
tailed information is provided in the supporting information.
Figure 1a shows a high-resolution transmission electron micro-
scope (HRTEM) image of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals, re-
vealing a monodisperse honeycomb-shaped hexagonal morphol-
ogy with an average particle size of 3.45 nm (Figure S2a, Sup-
porting Information).[47] The selected area electron diﬀraction
(SAED) pattern of individual particles (Figure S2b,c, Support-
ing Information) further conﬁrms the lattice fringe spacing of
d = 2.89 Å, which is very close to (006) plane of the hexagonal
phase Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals. Figure S3 (Supporting Informa-
tion) shows the microstructure of the synthesized monodisperse
nanocrystals with a plane (006).

Figure S4 (Supporting Information) illustrates a schematic di-
agram of the fabrication process of the FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3
perovskite thin ﬁlms with Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals.
Here, we refer to the FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 thin ﬁlm fabri-
cated without the addition of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals
as the control sample, and those fabricated with the addition
of nanocrystals as the target-x samples (where u = 0.7, 0.9,
1.2, 1.8, 5.8 mg mL−1). Among them, the target−0.9 sample
with the optimal modiﬁcation concentration shall be abbrevi-
ated as the target sample. The target sample was characterized
by HRTEM as shown in Figure 1b. The SAED pattern of the
blue region (Figure S5a,b, Supporting Information) reveals d
= 3.7 Å, corresponding to the (111) plane of 𝛼-perovskite. The
SAED patterns of the pink (Figure S5c,d, Supporting Informa-
tion), green (Figure S5e,f, Supporting Information), and yellow
regions (Figure S5g,h, Supporting Information) surrounding the
blue region show d = 2.89 Å, corresponding to the (006) plane of
Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals. This indicates that during the thin-ﬁlm
growth process, the (111) plane of the perovskite matrix was cov-
ered by Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals. Figure S6 (Supporting
Information) also conﬁrms that Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals are pre-
dominantly located at the grain boundaries of the perovskite ﬁlm,
where they contribute to defect passivation and enhanced ﬁlm
stability.
In situ XRD characterization of perovskite ﬁlms was per-
formed during annealing to investigate the evolution of crystal
facets during the crystallization process during the ﬁrst 300 s
(Figure 1c,d). The characteristic peaks at 13.9°, 24.2°, and 28.1°
can be clearly attributed to the (100), (111), and (200) lattice planes
of the cubic phase of FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3. In the control sam-
ple, the (111) plane grows uncontrollably in large quantity dur-
ing the ﬁrst 0–120 s of the annealing process, followed by the
transformation of the (111) facet to (100) facet, to (200) facet, and
ﬁnally to 2H phase (11.6°). The perovskite crystal formation pro-
cess of the target sample shows direct (100) and (200) growth
pathways, where the growth of (111) facets and the associated
transformation are completely suppressed. After 300 s, the XRD
peak positions and the relative intensity of the thin ﬁlm were sta-
ble until the temperature was increased to 140 °C. At this point,
the 2H phase of the perovskite thin ﬁlm is further transformed
into PbI2 (12.6°), as shown in Figure S7 (Supporting Informa-
tion). Here, XRD characterization conﬁrms that the addition of
a suﬃcient amount of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals can ef-
fectively inhibit growth of the (111) crystal plane during the ﬁrst
phase of crystallization (0 to 300 s) of the perovskite thin ﬁlm,
thereby improving the homogeneity of the crystal growth pro-
cess. The nanocrystals, acting as barrier particles (Figure S7a,
Supporting Information), increase the intensity of the main peak
(100) of the target sample, which is 2.4 times higher than that
of the control. Meanwhile, the proportion of PbI2 reduced from
27.5% to 2.5% which were informed by comparing the XRD pat-
tern intensity corresponding to the (100) plane (Figure S7b, Sup-
porting Information). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images re-
veal that the perovskite ﬁlm is the ﬂattest and most compact at a
nanocrystal concentration of 0.9 mg mL−1 (Figure S8, Support-
ing Information). The SEM characterization (Figure 1e,f) reveals
that the average particle size of the control sample is ≈887.8 ±
370.8 nm, and the target sample is ≈1356.3 ± 391.0 nm (Figure
S9a,b, Supporting Information). This eﬀect is attributed to the
orderly crystalline growth process in the preferred (100) plane of
the matrix.[48]
We further calculated the formation energy (ΔEF) of Cs4PbBr6
nanocrystals on the (100), (110), and (111) perovskite surfaces
(Figure S10, Supporting Information). The statistical results are
shown in Figure S11 (Supporting Information). This result in-
dicates that the (006) plane of Cs4PbBr6 can preferentially com-
bine with the (111) plane of the FAPbI3 perovskite matrix dur-
ing the formation process of the perovskite system. Moreover,
the space-charge-limited current (SCLC) measurement (Figure
S12, Supporting Information) and time-resolved photolumines-
cence (TRPL) characterization (Figure S13, Supporting Informa-
tion) reveal ﬁlms with a (100) orientation show a higher mo-
bility (6.29 × 10−3 cm2·V−1·s−1) and a longer carrier lifetime
(1022.2 ns) compared to those dominated by the (111) plane (4.77
× 10−3 cm2·V−1·s−1 and 500.3 ns, respectively). Tables S1 and
S2 (Supporting Information) list the results. Therefore, suppres-
sion of the (111) plane reduces the defect state and improves the
charge transport, which provides a mechanistic explanation for
the increased photovoltaic performance. Figure 2 shows the cor-
responding growth mechanism and XRD phase evolution of the
perovskite ﬁlm during the process from precursor solution to
crystallization after the addition of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrys-
tals. Unlike the crystallization process of the ﬁlm without modiﬁ-
cation (Figure S14, Supporting Information), Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals eﬀectively combine and cover the (111) planes of the
matrix, exerting a role in conﬁned growth (Figure 2a,b). It should
be noted that the eﬀect of modiﬁcation is unique. The suppres-
sion of the (100) plane is negligible in ﬁlms prepared with either
CsPbBr3 alone or with only the ligands (Figures S15, S16, Sup-
porting Information). The ligands on the surface of the nanocrys-
tals also form hydrogen bonds with the matrix after combina-
tion (Figure S17a, Supporting Information). The thin ﬁlm con-
tains only the 𝛼-perovskite phase and the Cs4PbBr6 crystal phase
(marked in blue at 22.8°, Figure 2d) after annealing for 3 s. At this
stage, the intensities of the (100) and (200) peaks are the highest,
and the ﬁlm is free of solvent phase and 2H phase. The study
found that the introduction of nanocrystals makes the composi-
tion of the thin ﬁlm more homogeneous during the crystalliza-
tion process, thereby allowing the main phases (100) and (200)
to grow more orderly, as shown in Figure 2c, the schematic dia-
gram of the crystal arrangement after high-temperature anneal-
ing at 140 °C. Ultimately, a more orderly ﬁlm with well-passivated
non-coordinated Pb2+ ions is formed (Figure S17b,c, Support-
ing Information). Figure 2e shows the XRD diﬀraction pattern
of the thin ﬁlm at this time. It can be seen that the peak in-
tensities of the (111) plane and PbI2 in the ﬁnal prepared thin
ﬁlm are signiﬁcantly reduced, and the diﬀraction peaks of the 2H
phase disappear. Furthermore, the reduction of PbI2 also causes
a red shift in the photoluminescence (PL) of the thin ﬁlm (Figure
S18a, Supporting Information) and increases the lifetime of the
thin ﬁlm (Figure S18b, Supporting Information). Furthermore,
we further conﬁrmed through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy
that the changes in the emission peak of the modiﬁed ﬁlm are
not due to changes in the bandgap (Figure S19, Supporting In-
formation).
Time-of-ﬂight secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS)
was used to study the distribution of nanocrystals in perovskite
ﬁlms. Compared to the control device (Figure S20a, Supporting

Figure 2. Schematic diagram of the crystallization mechanism for the target sample: (a) in the precursor solution, (b) after adding chlorobenzene (CB)
and annealing at 100 °C, (c) the ﬁnal perovskite ﬁlm formed after annealing at 140 °C. XRD patterns measured at the corresponding moments of the
mechanism diagram: (d) after adding CB and annealing at 100 °C for 3 s, (e) after annealing at 140 °C for 10 min, and (f) TOF-SIMS image of the
cross-section of the target PSCs.
Information), the target device (Figure 2f) shows a longitu-
dinally balanced distribution of Br and I elements, which
excludes
accumulation
of
Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrys-
tals
at
either
on
the
surface
or
at
the
bottom
of
the
perovskite
ﬁlm.
Furthermore,
Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals also exhibit uniform horizontal and longitu-
dinal distribution in the center and edges of the thin ﬁlm
(Figures S20b, S21, Supporting Information). Variable-angle
XRD measurements indicate uniform crystal orientation and
structural quality throughout the ﬁlm thickness (Figure S22,
Supporting Information). These results conﬁrm that the ability
of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 hexagonal phase nanocrystals, whose
crystal structure is diﬀerent from the ABX3 cubic phase of
FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 matrix, is not trapped by the matrix dur-
ing the ﬁlm growth process.[33] The introduction of nanocrystals
promises to uniformly modify the perovskite thin ﬁlm and lay
the foundation for the production of large-area perovskite solar
cells.
We further fabricated solar cells with a planar n-i-p struc-
ture made of ITO/SnO2/perovskite/ spiro-OMeTAD/Ag to study
the photovoltaic performance (Figure S23, Supporting Informa-
tion). Figure S24 (Supporting Information) compares the current
density-voltage (J-V) curves of PSC devices (0.04 cm2) by varying
nanocrystals addition concentrations measured under 100 mW
cm−2 AM 1.5G illumination. The corresponding photovoltaic
parameters of the devices are listed in Table S3 (Supporting
Information). The optimal concentration of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals was 0.9 mg mL−1
, which is consistent with the op-
timal amount for modifying the thin ﬁlms. Figure 3a shows the
J-V curves of the control and the target PSCs with photovoltaic
parameters. The increase in performance is mainly due to the ap-
parent improvement in VOC by 47 mV. The hysteresis factor (HI)
for the control and target devices was calculated on the basis of
the J-V scan data in forward and reverse directions. The results
are shown in Table S4 (Supporting Information). HI decreased
from 3.86 to 2.04 after addition of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrys-
tals, due to mitigated iodine vacancy defects in the (111) plane
(Figure S17, Supporting Information). Figure S25 (Supporting
Information) shows statistical plots of the diﬀerent parameters
from the J-V curves of 20 devices, indicating that the increase in
cell eﬃciency of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals is generalized.
The external quantum eﬃciency (EQE) of the control and target
devices (Figure S26, Supporting Information) yields integrated
JSC values of 24.70 mA cm−2 and 24.73 mA cm−2, respectively,
which are consistent with the J-V measurements. We also fabri-
cated 1 cm2 perovskite solar cells using the optimized ﬁlms. As
shown in Figure 3b, a PCE value of 23.64% (with JSC = 25.37 mA
cm−2, VOC = 1.173 V, FF = 80%) was obtained for the PSC de-
vice using the Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals, while the con-
trol device had a PCE of 21.59% (with JSC = 25.35 mA cm−2,
VOC = 1.122 V, FF = 76%). This shows that Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3
nanocrystals play an important role in improving the uniformity
and densiﬁcation of the FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 ﬁlm.
In a nitrogen atmosphere, the operational stability of the en-
capsulated control and target devices was assessed by maximum
power point tracking (MPPT) under continuous illumination
with 1-sun at 50–55 °C (Figure 3c). The target device retained
93.8% of its initial PCE after 1,500 h. In contrast, the control de-
vice maintained only 74.4% of its initial PCE during the same pe-
riod, primarily due to the photo-induced accelerated degradation
of the perovskite layer by PbI2,[49] which introduces defect states
and increases non-radiative recombination, leading to a reduc-
tion in VOC. The stability of PSC devices was then tested under the
condition of 85 °C and 85% relative humidity (RH). As illustrated

Figure 3. Photovoltaic performance of the control and target PSCs. J-V curves of (a) the 0.04 cm2 and (b) the 1 cm2 measured under AM 1.5 G illumination
of 100 mW cm−2 in reverse scan and forward scan. (c) MPP tracking measured of the encapsulated PSCs under full solar illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW
cm−2) at 50–55 °C. (d) Stability test of the encapsulated PSCs under the condition of 85 °C and 85% RH.
in Figure 3d, the encapsulated target device retained 92% of its
initial eﬃciency after 500 h of thermomechanical aging at 85 °C
and 85% RH, whereas the encapsulated control device only main-
tained 48% of its initial eﬃciency. Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrys-
tals, acting as oriented crystallization regulators, eﬀectively im-
prove the performance and operational reliability of PSCs.
Figure 4a shows the cross-sectional SEM images of the two
devices. The control device has perovskite grains with visible
cracks and uneven crystallization, while the grains in the target
device are dense and vertically distributed. Figure 4b shows the
dependence of VOC on light intensity in the range of 5–100 mW
cm−2, from which the ideality factor can be determined to be
1.83 kT q−1 and 1.41 kT q−1 for the control and target devices,
respectively. This indicates that trap-assisted Shockley-Read-Hall
(SRH) recombination is suppressed in the target device.[50,51] In
addition, we evaluated the interfacial recombination lifetime (𝜏re,
Figure 4c; Figure S27, Supporting Information) and carrier trans-
port lifetime (𝜏tr, Figure 4d; Figure S28, Supporting Informa-
tion) of the control and target devices using transient photovolt-
age/photocurrent decay measurements at diﬀerent light inten-
sities. Under 1 sun illumination, the 𝜏re of the target device is
determined to be 28.9 μs, which is longer than that of the control
device (≈9.16 μs). The 𝜏tr of the target device is 1.22 μs, which
is shorter than that of the control device (≈3.36 μs). Note that
herein the lifetime is diﬀerent from that obtained with the time-
resolved photoluminescence technique, which is basically asso-
ciated with the steady-state recombination rate of excess charge
carriers within the perovskite bulk throughout the device. There-
fore, these free charge carriers with longer recombination life-
times are more likely to be collected in photovoltaic devices. The
charge carrier diﬀusion coeﬃcient (Ddiﬀ) of the target device is
calculated to be 8.73 × 10−4 cm2 s−1 using the equation Ddiﬀ= d2
/ (k × 𝜏tr) (where d is the thickness of the absorber layer, k is a con-
stant (≈2.35)),[52] slightly higher than that of the control device
(3.17 × 10−4 cm2 s−1). Additionally, when illuminated with 1 sun
intensity, the charge carrier diﬀusion length (L) increases from
0.539 μm in the control device to 1.589 μm in the target device, as
calculated using L = (Ddiﬀ× 𝜏re)(1/2). Reducing recombination of
interface charges and increasing the diﬀusion length of charge
carriers increases the VOC and FF.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements
were conducted to investigate the charge transport and recom-
bination behavior at the device interface. Figure 4e presents
Nyquist plots of PSCs with and without Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals
under a bias voltage of 1.6 V in the dark, with an oscillation
potential amplitude of 10 mV. One distinct semicircle is ob-
served from the low-frequency regions. The equivalent circuit
used for ﬁtting the EIS data is shown in the inset. The semicir-
cle in the low-frequency region originates from carrier recom-
bination resistance (Rrec), which is inversely related to recombi-
nation rates at interfacial defects or within the perovskite bulk.
Upon incorporating Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals, Rrec increased from
605 Ω to 872 Ω, suggesting suppressed recombination due to
reduced defect density induced by the nanocrystals. Figure 4f

Figure 4. a) Cross-sectional SEM images (scale bar, 500 μm), (b) statistical plots of photovoltaic metrics from light intensity dependence of VOC, (c) the
interfacial recombination lifetimes obtained by exponential ﬁtting of the transient photovoltage decay curves, (d) the charge transport lifetimes obtained
by exponential ﬁtting of the transient photocurrent decay curves, (e) EIS spectra under a bias voltage of 1.6 V in the dark, with an oscillation potential
amplitude of 10 mV, (f) t-DOS curves obtained from C-F measurements of the control and target PSCs.
displays trap density of states (NT) quantitatively calculated by
angular frequency-dependent capacitance measurements on the
two devices (Figure S29, Supporting Information).[53–55] Note that
E𝜔here represents energy (shift relative to the Fermi level). For
the target device, the NT drops to one-third of the value for the
control device at around E𝜔= 0.06 eV (from 5.20 × 1015 cm−3 eV−1
to 1.701 × 1015 cm−3 eV−1). Combined with impedance character-
ization measurements, this further clariﬁes that the incorpora-
tion of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals signiﬁcantly suppresses
trap-assisted SRH recombination, forming a ﬁlm with low defect
density.
Figure S30 (Supporting Information) shows the optical im-
ages of the control and target perovskite ﬁlms kept in air at 25
°C and 50% RH. The fresh control ﬁlm appears bluish-black un-
der yellow light illumination, while the target ﬁlm is darker than
the former, without any blue tint. After being exposed to air for
three months, the control ﬁlm has largely degraded into a yellow
color, whereas the target sample remains intact. To investigate
the degradation mechanisms of perovskite ﬁlms, we monitored
the XRD data of the control and target ﬁlms placed in air at 25
°C and 50% RH for 1035 h. Figure 5a,b shows the results. For
the control ﬁlm, the XRD peaks at 11.6° and 12.6° correspond-
ing to the initial 2H and PbI2 increased rapidly with time. After
1035 h, the control ﬁlm completely decomposed from the orig-
inal 𝛼-phase ﬁlm into the 2H phase. The existing defects in the
ﬁlm are very susceptible to triggering a chain reaction that re-
inforces these defects in air at 25 °C and 50% RH. In contrast,
at the same exposure environment, the target ﬁlm started to de-
compose only after 411 h. Even after 1035 h, the ﬁlm was still pre-
dominantly in its 𝛼phase. The results above show that the envi-
ronmental stability of the nanocrystal-modiﬁed perovskite ﬁlms
is signiﬁcantly improved, arising from a holistic enhancement
in crystallinity, phase purity, and defect passivation within the
modiﬁed ﬁlm, rather than from a reliance on any single facet
orientation.
Finally, we prepared 5 × 5 cm2 perovskite ﬁlms using a
one-step spin-coating method to explore the eﬀectiveness of
Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in large-area perovskite ﬁlms.
Figure 5c shows an optical image of the large-area ﬁlm after the
addition of nanocrystals and nine regions (①–⑨) delineated for
XRD characterization (Figure S31, Supporting Information). The
XRD patterns of the control (Figure S31a, Supporting Informa-
tion) and target (Figure S31b, Supporting Information) ﬁlms in
these regions are consistent with those of the small-area ﬁlms
(1.5 × 1.5 cm2, Figure S10f, Supporting Information; Figure 2f),
and the intensity of the (100) peak in the target samples is sig-
niﬁcantly higher than that in the control samples. We further
extracted and statistically analyzed the ratios of the PbI2 / (100)
peak intensities in nine regions of the control and target ﬁlms,
as shown in Figure 5d, with the scale bar provided below the
image. In the statistical graph, it can be observed that the ratio
of PbI2 / (100) in various regions of the control samples ranges

Figure 5. a) XRD evolution of the control and (b) target ﬁlms after 1035 h in air at 25 °C and 50% RH; (c) Optical photograph under daylight and a
schematic diagram of the segmented areas, and (d) optical photograph under natural light of the 5 × 5 cm2 target ﬁlm, the inset represents the statistical
plot of the intensity of the PbI2/(100) peak for diﬀerent areas of the control and target ﬁlms.
from 25 to 30%, while that of the target samples drops sharply
to 1% to 3%. We found that Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals
can uniformly modify the crystallization of the 5 × 5 cm2 ﬁlm
(prepared by one-step spin-coating) across the entire area, en-
hancing the crystallization strength of the main peak (100) and
signiﬁcantly reducing the content of PbI2 relative to the main
peak (100) over a large area. In addition, we used a 5 × 5 cm2
ﬁlm to fabricate a mini-module (12.6 cm2) composed of six series-
connected sub-cells. As shown in Figure S32a (Supporting In-
formation), the module using perovskite ﬁlm with modiﬁcation
achieved a PCE of 21.85%, which was higher than the control
(19.63%). Furthermore, J-V measurements taken from both the
center and edge regions (Figure S32b, Supporting Information)
reveal a consistent performance with less than 0.5% variation
across the active area, conﬁrming the high uniformity and prac-
tical applicability of this approach to the fabrication of large-area
devices.
3. Conclusion
We introduce zero-dimensional Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrys-
tals to control the crystallization of FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3 per-
ovskites, which face phase instability problems due to the (111)
facets. Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 nanocrystals act as crystalline plane-
blocking particles that can preferentially encapsulate the high-
defect-density (111) crystal planes of the FA0.90MA0.05Cs0.05PbI3
matrix during the initial stage of thin-ﬁlm crystallization. This
action reduces the content of the 2H phase and PbI2, suppress-
ing the trap density of states to 1.701 × 1015 cm−3 eV−1. The re-
sulting PSC achieved an impressive PCE of 25.52% with a VOC
of 1.177 V. After 1,500 h of MPPT measurement under continu-
ous 1-sun illumination at 50–55 °C, the device retained 93.8% of
its initial PCE. This work provides a new method for producing
high-eﬃcient and stable organic-inorganic hybrid PSCs, which
can be widely applied to other perovskite systems and the fabri-
cation of large-area devices.
4. Experimental Section
The details of ﬁlm characterizations, device fabrication, and performance
measurements are described in Supporting Information.

the author.

text:
www.advenergymat.de
1902239  (1 of 10)
© 2019 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
Full Paper
Halogen Engineering for Operationally Stable Perovskite 
Solar Cells via Sequential Deposition
Qi Li, Yao Zhao, Wenke Zhou, Zhengyuan Han, Rui Fu, Fang Lin, Dapeng Yu,  
and Qing Zhao*
DOI: 10.1002/aenm.201902239
1. Introduction
Hybrid organic–inorganic halide perov-
skite materials have drawn intensive 
research attention recently, due to its 
extraordinary 
photoelectric 
properties, 
such as large optical absorption coeffi-
cient,[1–3] tunable bandgap,[4] long and bal-
ance carrier diffusion length,[5] low exciton 
binding energy,[6] and simple preparation 
methods,[7–9] etc. These advantages make 
the power conversion efficiency (PCE) 
of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) boom 
from 3.8%,[1] to today’s record 25.2%.[10] 
Nowadays, one-step antisolvent dripping 
deposition[3,9,11,12] and two-step sequential 
deposition[7,13–16] are widely used to fab-
ricate high-quality perovskite film. Com-
pared to one-step antisolvent dripping  
method, two-step sequential deposition 
could lead to vertical-columnar perovskite 
grains due to the top-down penetration 
during the second step.[13–16] Thus, a 
higher PCE could be achieved by sequen-
tial deposition, especially for the formami-
dinium-lead-iodide-based (FAPbI3-based) 
perovskite material with minimal bromide 
anions and close-to-ideal bandgap below 
1.55  eV, namely (FAPbI3)1−x(MAPbBr3)x, where x is less than 
0.05.[16–19] Although high PCE has been reached, instability 
issue is still a main obstacle in the way of PSCs’ commercializa-
tion, in particular for FAPbI3-based low-bandgap PSCs. When 
operational test at maximum power point (MPP) under 1-sun 
illumination was applied, these PSCs usually exhibit rapid deg-
radation within a few hours,[14,17–19] rarely exceed 100 h.[20–22] 
In contrast, PSCs based on high-bandgap perovskite materials 
fabricated by one-step antisolvent dripping deposition could 
realize operational stability for several hundreds of hours 
rather easily.[3,11,12,23–26] Nowadays, most works in regard to 
the instability issue focused on cation part of the perovskite 
materials and usually enhanced operational stability by incor-
porating inorganic cations, such as Cs,[11,12,23,26] Rb,[3] K,[27]  
etc. For example, Saliba et al. first reported triple cation based 
perovskite with Cs/MA/FA and achieved a stabilized PCE 
at 21.1% and an output at 18% under operational conditions 
after 250 h.[23] After that, they further enhanced the PCE and 
the stability of PSCs by introducing Rb into perovskite film 
forming Rb/Cs/MA/FA quadruple compounds. The quadruple 
The performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) relies on the synthesis 
method and chemical composition of the perovskite materials. So far, PSCs 
that have adopted two-step sequential deposited perovskite with the state-
of-art composition (FAPbI3)1−x(MAPbBr3)x (x < 0.05) have achieved record 
power conversion efficiency (PCE), while their one-step antisolvent dripping 
counterparts with typical composition Cs0.05FA0.81MA0.14Pb(I0.85Br0.15)3 with 
more bromine have exhibited much better long-term operational stability. 
Thus, halogen engineering that aims to elevate bromine content in sequen-
tial deposited perovskite film would push operational stability of PSCs 
toward that of antisolvent dripping deposited perovskite materials. Here, a 
Br-rich seeding growth method is devised and perovskite seed solution with 
high bromine content is introduced into a PbI2 precursor, leading to bromine 
incorporation in the resulting perovskite film. Photovoltaic devices fabricated 
by Br-rich seeding growth method exhibit a PCE of 21.5%, similar to 21.6% 
for PSCs having lower bromine content. Whereas, the operational stability of 
PSCs with higher bromine content is significantly enhanced, with over 80% 
of initial PCE retained after 500 h tracking at maximum power point under 
1-sun illumination. This work highlights the vital importance of halogen 
composition for the operational stability of PSCs, and introduces an effec-
tive way to incorporate bromine into mixed-cation-halide perovskite film via 
sequential deposition method.
Q. Li, Y. Zhao, Dr. W. K. Zhou, Z. Y. Han, Dr. R. Fu, Dr. F. Lin,  
Prof. D. P. Yu, Prof. Q. Zhao
State Key Laboratory for Mesoscopic Physics
School of Physics
Peking University
Beijing 100871, China
E-mail: zhaoqing@pku.edu.cn
Q. Li, Y. Zhao, Dr. W. K. Zhou, Z. Y. Han, Dr. R. Fu, Dr. F. Lin,  
Prof. D. P. Yu, Prof. Q. Zhao
Collaborative Innovation Center of Quantum Matter
Beijing 100084, China
Q. Li, Y. Zhao, Dr. W. K. Zhou, Z. Y. Han, Dr. R. Fu, Dr. F. Lin,  
Prof. D. P. Yu, Prof. Q. Zhao
Frontiers Science Center for Nano-optoelectronics
Peking University
Beijing 100871, China
Q. Li, Prof. D. P. Yu
UCLA-PKU Joint Research Institute in Science and Engineering
Beijing 100871, China
The ORCID identification number(s) for the author(s) of this article 
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/aenm.201902239.

1902239  (2 of 10)
cation cells could achieve a PCE at 21.8% and sustain 95% of 
initial performance after 500 h operational test under 85 °C.[3] 
While, works taking halogen part in perovskite materials into 
consideration are rather rare.[22] Li et al. recently employ fluo-
ride to simultaneously passivate both anion and cation vacan-
cies and acquired a PCE of 21.5% and less than 10% loss after 
1000 h testing under operational condition.[22] The fluoride 
is effective to passivate vacancies on the surface and grain 
boundaries, other approaches are further required to enhance 
the entire bulk perovskite film stability. Table 1 compared the 
PSCs’ operational stability with different composition and fab-
rication method of perovskite materials in some typical reports. 
Notably, perovskite materials deposited by one-step antisolvent 
dripping method usually contain much higher content of bro-
mine (≈17%) than the two-step sequential deposition counter-
part (≈3%) and better operational stability compared to PSCs 
based on sequetial deposition method. These results indicated 
the vital importance of bromine anions for stabilizing mixed-
cation perovskite materials during the long-term operation. 
The incorporation of higher content of bromine would stabi-
lize the perovskite phase of FAPbI3[28] and make the perovskite 
phase a thermodynamically favored phase,[23] resulting in a 
better thermal stability. In addition, because the iodine anions 
have lower ionic migration activation energy, especially under 
light illumination, it would migrate easily under operational 
stability test and cause performance degradation of the PSC 
devices.[29,30] As a result, the replacement of iodine by bromine 
would improve the operational stability of PSC devices.
However, introducing bromine anions efficiently in two-step 
sequential deposition is a tough mission. In traditional two-step 
sequential deposition method, there are two possible ways to 
introduce bromine anions into perovksite film. One is adding 
PbBr2 powder into PbI2 solution and forming PbI2/PbBr2 film, 
then spin coating FAI solution on it (Figure  1a). Unlike the 
layer structure of PbI2,[9,15,20,31] PbBr2 possess a bulk structure, 
which would impede the intercalation of organic amine halide 
during the second step and cause incomplete formation of 
perovskite materials (Figure  1a).[32] Another way is resolving 
FABr and MABr into FAI solution, then depositing on the PbI2 
film (Figure 1b). Compared with I−, Br− anion has higher ionic 
migration activation energy, thus the transportation of Br− in 
PbI2 layer or perovskite film is much harder and slower, which 
would impede the formation of final perovskite materials as 
well (Figure 1b).[25,29,33]
Recently, perovskite seeding growth method[20] and CsCl-
enhanced PbI2 precursor method were developed for better 
perovskite film formation with larger grains.[17] Zhao et  al. 
reported the usage of perovskite precursor as seeds for the first 
time, which was referred as perovskite seeding growth method. 
In this method, submicron-sized crystalline perovskite seeds 
were added in PbI2 solution during the first step in sequential 
deposition. The perovskite seeds could facilitate perovskite crys-
tallization during the reaction between organic amine halide 
and PbI2 in the second step. At the same time, perovskite seeds 
could provide element source, such as Cs, to the final film for 
better operational stability of PSCs.[20] On the basis of perov-
skite seeding growth method, Li et al. developed CsCl-enhanced 
PbI2 precursor method, in which CsCl was introduced into 
PbI2 precursor alongside the perovskite seeds in the first step 
in sequential deposition. CsCl could incorporate more cesium 
into final perovskite film, resulting in better operational sta-
bility of devices. Meanwhile, chlorine could reduce the number 
of nucleation sites and retard the perovskite crystallization 
process, leading to larger grain size and better device perfor-
mance.[17] Based on both methods, here, we devised a Br-rich 
seeding growth method via halogen engineering by altering 
the perovskite seeding composition and seeding concentration. 
Using this method, we elevated bromine concentration in final 
FAPbI3-based perovskite film from ≈3% to ≈6% (with respect to 
iodine) without significant PCE sacrifice of final PSCs (merely
