1902239  (3 of 10)
lower down about 0.1% from 21.6% to 21.5%). Remarkably, the 
operational stability under 1-sun illumination at MPP was sig-
nificantly improved from 150 h to over 500 h by keeping 80% 
of its initial value under operational condition. This work offers 
an effective approach to incorporate bromine anions into the 
state-of-art mixed cation perovskite via sequential deposition for 
better operational stability.
2. Results and Discussion
The schematic of Br-rich seeding growth method was shown in 
Figure 1c and the experimental details could be found in exper-
imental section. PbBr2, FABr, and MABr were preresolved with 
PbI2, FAI, and CsI in DMF/DMSO mixed solvent at stoichio-
metric proportion to obtain Br-rich perovskite seed, which could 
be written as Cs0.2MA0.2FA0.6Pb(I0.22Br0.78)3. And then Br-rich 
perovskite seed was added into PbI2/CsCl solution, forming 
PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed solution, followed by spin coating on 
TiO2 substrate. After annealing, the organic amine salts solu-
tion containing FAI, MABr, and MACl was deposited atop the 
PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed layer. After another annealing process, 
a perovskite layer with high bromine content was formed. By 
using this method, we could preintercalate bromine into PbI2 
layer via Br-rich perovskite seed during the first step, no need to 
increase bromine content of FAI solution in the second step. At 
the same time, the bromine would not impede the intercalation 
of organic amine halide during the second step. There are three 
main parameters to better control the Br-rich perovskite seed 
solution: seed composition, seed concentration, and the seed 
adding amount to PbI2/CsCl solution. To introduce bromine 
anions effectively, the concentration of Br-rich perovskite seed 
solution should be as high as possible. Thus, the best choice 
was seed solution with pure bromine. However, too much bro-
mine would intensively decrease the solubility of the seed solu-
tion with DMF as main solvent, especially for pure bromine 
composition.[20,25,26] For another aspect, as different mixed sol-
vent was used for the perovskite seed solution and PbI2/CsCl 
solution (see solution preparation part), the adding amount of 
Br-rich perovskite seed solution to PbI2/CsCl solution should 
be limited to minimize the mutual interference of the solvent, 
which required high solubility. Therefore, a little iodine should 
be added to compromise the contradiction between bromine 
content and solubility. Here, we chose I/Br proportion around 
1:4, under which a Br-rich perovskite seed solution with about 
1 m concentration could be acquired (see solution preparation 
part), not too far lower than that Br-low perovskite seed solution 
used in CsCl-enhanced PbI2 precursor method (about 1.2 m, 
see solution preparation part). At the same time, the bromine 
content was high enough to introduce bromine into final perov-
skite film. The adding amount of Br-rich perovskite seed solu-
tion to PbI2/CsCl solution would influence bromine content 
in final perovskite film. To obtain the target bromine content 
around 6% (this target number would be discussed later), the 
adding amount as 70  µL per 1  mL PbI2/CsCl mixed solution 
was determined. Finally, to keep the other cations content in 
final perovskite film unchanged, we got the Br-rich seed com-
position as Cs0.2MA0.2FA0.6Pb(I0.22Br0.78)3.
To prove the Br-rich seeding growth method could effectively 
introduce bromine into final perovskite film, we carried out a 
series of characterizations. Hereafter, to narrate simply, we refer 
to the perovskite samples prepared by CsCl-enhanced PbI2 pre-
cursor method with Br-low perovskite seed as “control,”[17] and 
the samples prepared by Br-rich seeding growth method as “Br-
rich,” respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) top-view  
images showed that the perovskite grains were enlarged in the

1902239  (4 of 10)
“Br-rich” sample (Figure  2b) compared with “control” sample 
(Figure 2a), from ≈1 to ≈1.5 µm. The same trend was observed 
in the SEM cross-sectional images on full assembling solar cell 
devices with ITO/TiO2/perovskite/spiro-OMeTAD/Au configu-
ration. Both “control” and “Br-rich” samples exhibited average 
thickness of perovskite layer over 700  nm, which would lead 
to good absorption of light. Moreover, the grains in “Br-rich” 
sample exhibited much more columnar form in cross-section 
SEM images, with grain boundaries aligned vertically from top 
to bottom. Because deep-level defects often distribute at grain 
boundaries and would cause faster nonradiative decay,[34] larger 
grain size, that is fewer grain boundaries, usually means less 
photoelectron loss, thus would be beneficial to device perfor-
mance. The larger grain size in the “Br-rich” sample could be 
allocated to lower perovskite seed concentration using in Br-rich 
seeding growth method (70 µL per 1 mL PbI2/CsCl mixed solu-
tion) compared with control method (130  µL per 1  mL PbI2/
CsCl mixed solution). Less perovskite seeds means a decreased 
number of nucleation sites during the following perovskite 
growth process, thus leading to larger perovskite grain and 
less grain boundaries.[17,20] This result further highlights the 
advantages of using perovskite seed solution in sequential dep-
osition method. Perovskite seed would control the perovskite 
nucleation and facilitate perovskite film growth. By regulating

© 2019 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1902239  (5 of 10)
perovskite seed concentration, larger perovskite grain with 
better vertically columnar alignment could be achieved.[17,20]
To investigate the effectiveness of Br-rich seeding growth 
method for introducing bromine into final perovskite lat-
tice, we performed X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement and 
the results were shown in Figure  3a,b. Compared with “con-
trol” sample, all the perovskite peaks (denoted as asterisks) of 
“Br-rich” sample in XRD measurement showed a same shift 
tendency to higher angle (Figure 3a). To see more clearly, we 
zoomed in a main perovskite peak at around 13.9°. As shown 
in Figure 3b, for “Br-rich” sample, the peak position with max-
imum intensity moved to a higher angle. These phenomena 
indicated that the lattice parameter of the perovskite unit cell 
was reduced, thus, bromine was successfully inserted into the 
perovskite lattice, rather than just modifying at grain bounda-
ries like other halogens, such as chlorine and fluorine.[22,35] 
Moreover, a less pronounced PbI2 peak (at 12.7° and 38.6°, 
denoted as solid triangles) was observed for “Br-rich” sample, 
implying the formation of PbI2 was suppressed.[36]
To further estimate bromine content after applying Br-rich  
seeding growth method, we performed X-ray photo­electron 
spectroscopy (XPS) measurements and did quantitative 
analysis to the XPS spectra. As shown in Figure 3c,d, through  
Br-rich seeding growth method, the content of bromine 
in final perovskite film was obviously elevated (Figure  3c). 
Meanwhile, the content of iodine showed a distinct decrease 
(Figure  3d), which means some of iodine was substituted by 
bromine after using Br-rich seeding growth method, in accord-
ance with XRD results. Quantitative analysis of relative content 
of different elements in perovksite film is achieved from the 
XPS spectra. The XPS spectrum of Pb was used as reference 
(Figure S1a, Supporting Information) and the entire calcula-
tion process could be found in Note S1 (Supporting Informa-
tion). Briefly, after subtracting background signal of spectra, 
Adv. Energy Mater. 2019, 9, 1902239

Br-rich samples and b) zoomed in around 13.9°. Solid triangles denote the PbI2 phase and asterisks denote the perovskite phase. c) X-ray photoelectron 
spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of Br 3d peaks and d) I 3d peaks for control and Br-rich samples. e) The steady-state photoluminescence spectrum and  
f) time-resolved photoluminescence decay spectrum results for control and Br-rich samples.

label: related
text:
www.advenergymat.de
www.advancedsciencenews.com
© 2019 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1902239  (6 of 10)
areas of each peak were acquired by integral calculation. Then 
using atomic sensitivity factors, relative elements content was 
obtained. XPS result showed that using Br-rich seeding growth 
method, bromine concentration in final perovskite film was 
promoted from ≈3% to ≈6% (with respect to iodine). At the 
same time, cesium content remained ≈5% (Figure S1b and 
Note S1, Supporting Information), indicating perovskite com-
position changing from Cs0.05MA0.03FA0.92­Pb(I0.97Br0.03)3 to 
Cs0.05MA0.03FA0.92Pb(I0.94Br0.06)3.
Then optical properties of the perovskite film were investi-
gated. The photoluminescence (PL) peak of “Br-rich” sample 
shifted from 805 to 800  nm (Figure  3e), which is originated 
from a shrunken perovskite unit cell parameter,[23,37] sug-
gesting that bromine anions were integrated into perovskite 
lattice, in accordance with XRD results. Also, time-resolved PL 
(TRPL) decay measurement was carried out to study carrier 
lifetime (Figure  3f). Single exponential function was applied 
to fit measurement results. A carrier lifetime increase from 
1049.4 ns (“control” sample) to 1527.4 ns (“Br-rich” sample) was 
observed, indicating reduced density of defect states by using 
Br-rich seeding growth method, which could be allocated to the 
enlarged grain size and lessened grain boundaries. To confirm 
the carrier lifetime results, we also used biexponential function 
to fit TRPL results. The fitting curve and fitting result param-
eters were given in Figure S2 in the Supporting Information. 
It is worthy noting that when biexponential decay model was 
assigned, carrier lifetime for the relatively fast decay component 
(t1) shown a very similar value compared to that for the slower 
component (t2), which means the TRPL results exhibit a mono-
exponential behavior in fact. In other word, no fast decay could 
be detected. Because the fast decay component usually related 
to nonradiative recombination induced by trap states,[16,38] this 
monoexponential behavior indicated a high perovskite film 
quality with rare defect states at the same time.
After perovskite film characterization, photovoltaic perfor-
mance was examined between PSCs based on “control” and 
“Br-rich” perovskite film in planar structure of ITO/TiO2/
perovskite/Spiro-OMeTAD/Au. Figure 4a gives the current den-
sity–voltage (J–V) curves for the best-performing devices. The 
“control” devices displayed short-circuit current density (JSC) of 
24.2 mA cm−2, open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 1.11 V and fill factor 
(FF) of 80.5%, leading to a PCE of 21.6%. Using Br-rich seeding 
growth method devised in this study, a similar PCE of 21.5% 
was achieved, with JSC of 23.8  mA cm−2, VOC of 1.12  V, and 
FF of 80.8%. The JSC was a little bit lower in “Br-rich” sample, 
derived from a higher bandgap. At the same time, the higher 
bandgap of “Br-rich” sample results in a higher VOC, offsetting 
the short-circuit current loss and causing a similar PCE. Mean-
while, the hysteresis of the devices was measured and the result 
is shown in Figure S3 and Table S1 in the Supporting Informa-
tion. External quantum efficiency (EQE) measurement was also 
performed to confirm the Jsc value in the J–V curves (Figure 4b). 
And the integrated Jsc values are 23.7 and 23.4  mA cm−2  
for the “control” devices and “Br-rich” devices, respectively, 
consisting well with the Jsc values determined by J–V curves. 
The zoomed onset part of EQE spectra around 800  nm  
(Figure S4, Supporting Information) shows a clear shift to 
shorter wavelength in “Br-rich” sample, indicating a larger 
bandgap of perovskite film, in accordance with PL and XRD 
results. The EQE results also pointed out that the short-circuit 
current loss mainly originated from inferior light absorption at 
long wavelength region near the bandgap, in agreement with the 
higher bandgap of “Br-rich” sample. Besides J–V curve, a similar 
performance is observed in steady-state output measurement 
(Figure 4c), both “control” devices and “Br-rich” devices exhibited 
stabilized output efficiency around 20.55% within 500 s.
Then the thermal stability and long-term operational stability 
of the PSCs were studied. Thermal stress test was applied to bare 
perovskite film considering that Spiro-OMeTAD cannot sustain 
high temperature.[3] Both “control” and “Br-rich” samples were 
heated under 150 °C for 30 min in a N2-filled glove box. XRD 
pattern of “control” and “Br-rich” samples (Figure 5a,b) showed 
an intensity growth for PbI2 peak (denoted as solid triangles) 
after heating, but the “Br-rich” sample exhibited a less profound 
PbI2 after heating, implying an enhanced thermal stability. The 
operational stability was performed under AM 1.5G irradiation 
and MPP tracking condition, and the normalized PCE versus 
time was shown in Figure 5c. PCE of “control” device decayed 
below 80% of the initial value after 150 h. Surprisingly, with 
only 3% higher bromine content (from ≈3% to ≈6%), the “Br-
rich” device could maintain 90% of its initial PCE after 200 h of 
continuous working. Even after 500 h of continuous working, 
the “Br-rich” device still retains over 80% of its initial PCE. This 
result stands for an operational stability among the best class of 
PSCs fabricated via sequential deposition method, even com-
parable with that fabricated by one-step antisolvent dripping 
method (Table  1). At the same time, it highlights the signifi-
cance of Br content in improving operational stability of PSCs.

film. The solar cell performance parameters are listed in the inset. b) The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectrum of the devices based on control 
and Br-rich perovskite film. c) The steady-state output of the devices based on control and Br-rich perovskite film.

1902239  (7 of 10)
To confirm that the operational stability originated from 
higher bromine anion content, we performed further control 
experiments by substituting CsCl using in PbI2 solution by 
the same molar weight CsBr to further increase Br content in 
the final perovskite film. XPS spectra of perovskite film sam-
ples fabricated by using PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed mixed solu-
tion (denoted as “CsCl” here) and by using PbI2/CsBr/Br-rich 
seed mixed solution (denoted as “CsBr” here) were shown in 
Figure S5 in the Supporting Information. It is obvious that the 
bromine content was elevated again (Figure S5c, Supporting 
Information). Also, qualitative investigation was applied to 
extract relative content of different elements from XPS spectrum 
(Figure S5, Supporting Information). The entire calculation pro-
cess was similar to that mentioned above and could be found in 
Note S2 in the Supporting Information. XPS result showed that 
after substituting CsCl with CsBr, cesium content remained 
unchanged while bromine concentration in final perovskite 
film was further promoted from ≈6% to ≈8%, indicating a 
perovskite composition of Cs0.05MA0.03FA0.92Pb(I0.92Br0.08)3. 
The excess bromine anion further widened bandgap of perov-
skite material, leading to a higher VOC and lower JSC. The VOC 
increase could not compensate the JSC decrease this time, 
resulting in a slight decay on final PCE from 21.5% to 20.8% 
(Figure S6, Supporting Information). Thus, in Br-rich seeding 
growth method, bromine content should not exceed 6% to 
maintain a basic PCE. To determine the target bromine con-
tent of Br-rich seeding growth method as 6%, which was used 
throughout this work, we fabricated solar cell devices with 
perovskite films having different bromine content ranging 
from 3% to 6%. The different bromine content was reached 
by adding different amount of Br-rich seeding precursor into 
the same amount PbI2 solution. The statistical results showed 
that when bromine content elevated from 3% to 6%, PCE value 
decreased slightly while operational stability was obviously 
enhanced (Figures S8 and S9, Supporting Information). How-
ever, further elevating bromine content to 8% would lead to a 
fast drop of PCE, although the device performed even better 
operational stability (Figures S8 and S9, Supporting Informa-
tion). Finally, we chose 6% as target bromine content to bal-
ance the PCE and operational stability of devices. As depicted 
in Figure S7 in the Supporting Information, the operational 
stability of PSC devices based on perovskite layer with 8% bro-
mine content was further enhanced. After continuous testing 
under AM 1.5G irradiation and MPP tracking for 200 h, the 
device with 8% bromine content (that is “CsBr” here) could 
maintain 95% of its initial PCE, better than 90% for device fab-
ricated by Br-rich seeding growth method (that is “CsCl” here). 
These results further confirm that higher bromine anion con-
tent is beneficial to the operational stability of final photovoltaic 
devices.
For mixed-halide perovskite material with both iodine and 
bromine, previous studies suggested that it would suffer from 
light-induced ion migration and phase segregation under opera-
tional condition, resulting in poor device stability, especially for 
perovskite with bromine content exceeding 15%.[26,39] Several 
experimental approaches were devised to detected ion migra-
tion and phase segregation, such as lateral structure test[26,40] 
and Galvanostatic measurement.[41] To evaluate whether phase 
segregation exist or not in our study, we performed in situ PL 
characterization combined with galvanostatic measurement

1902239  (8 of 10)
Adv. Energy Mater. 2019, 9, 1902239
under light illumination on a confocal laser scanning micro-
scope system (CLSM) (Figure S10, Supporting Information). 
During the test, light illumination and electric field were applied 
together to imitate operational condition of PSCs. If phase seg-
regation actually exist, red and blue shift of PL peak position 
should be detected near the anode and cathode, respectively.[26] 
We tested all “control” sample (with ≈3% bromine content),  
“Br-rich” sample (with ≈6% bromine content) and “CsBr” 
sample (with ≈8% bromine content), based on Au/perovskite/Au  
lateral structure. Surprisingly, after electric poling under light 
illumination for 120 s, no PL peak position shift was detected 
for all samples (Figure S11, the Supporting Information). 
In other word, no phase segregation existed. These results 
strongly suggest the good operational stability of PSCs fabri-
cated by Br-rich seeding growth method in this work.
3. Conclusion
In summary, we reported a Br-rich seeding growth method 
to produce perovskite film with higher bromine con-
tent based on state-of-art perovskite material composition  
Cs0.05MA0.03FA0.92Pb(I0.97Br0.03)3 via halogen engineering. The 
Br-rich seeding growth method led to larger perovskite grain 
size, less grain boundaries and defect states, and longer carrier 
lifetime, leading to a best PCE of 21.5% for PSC devices. More 
importantly, the introducing of more bromine anions from 3% 
to 6% significantly promoted the operational stability of PSC 
device. The “Br-rich” devices could sustain over 80% of its ini-
tial PCE after 500 h of continuous working at MPP bias under 
AM 1.5G illumination, rather than just 100 h for “control” sam-
ples. This work offers an approach to effectively incorporate 
bromine anions into the state-of-art mixed cation perovskite via 
sequential deposition, and at the same time of sustaining high 
PCE, achieving much better operational stability.
4. Experimental Section
Materials: N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%), dimethyl sulfoxide 
(DMSO, 99.9%), chlorobenzene (99.8%), acetonitrile (ACN, 99.9%), 
isopropanol (IPA, 99.7%), lead iodide (PbI2, beads, 99.999%), cesium 
chloride (CsCl, beads, 99.999%), cesium bromide (CsBr, 99.999%) and 
cesium iodide (CsI, 99.999%), were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Lithium 
bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (Li-TFSI, 98%) and lead bromide 
(PbBr2, 99.999%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. 4-tert-butylpyridine 
(4-TBP, 96%) was got from TCI. Methylammonium bromide (MABr), 
methylammonium chloride (MACl, 99.5%), formamidinium bromide 
(FABr), formamidinium iodide (FAI, 99.5%) and 2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis (N,N-
p-dimethoxy-phenylamino)-9,9′-spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD, 99.8%) 
were obtained from Xi’an Polymer Light Technology Corporation. No 
further purification was conducted to all the chemicals and were used 
as received.
Solution Preparation: 625  mg PbI2 beads and 10  mg CsCl beads 
were dissolved in 850 µL DMF and 115 µL DMSO and stirred at 80 °C 
overnight to prepare the PbI2/CsCl solution. the control perovskite seed 
solution was prepared by adding 600 mg PbI2 beads, 36.75 mg PbBr2 
powder, 189.2  mg FAI powder, 16.8  mg MABr powder, and 39  mg 
CsI powder into 1  mL 4:1 volume ratio DMF/DMSO mixed solvent 
and stirring at 60 °C for 30 min. Similarly, the Br-rich perovskite seed 
solution was prepared by solving 34.6 mg PbI2 beads, 357.8 mg PbBr2 
powder, 57.25 mg FAI powder, 37.15 mg FABr powder, 23.5 mg MABr 
powder, and 54.5  mg CsI powder into 1  mL 4:1 volume ratio DMF/
DMSO mixed solvent and stirring at room temperature (≈25  °C) for 
60 min. The PbI2/CsCl/control-seed and PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed mixed 
solution were obtained by adding 130  µL control perovskite seed 
solution and 70 µL Br-rich perovskite seed solution into 1 mL PbI2/CsCl 
solution, respectively. The PbI2/CsBr solution was prepared by dissolving 
3750 mg PbI2 beads and 70 mg CsBr in 5.1 mL DMF and 700 µL DMSO, 
and the solution was stirred at 80 °C overnight. The PbI2/CsBr/Br-rich 
seed mixed solution were obtained by adding 70 µL Br-rich perovskite 
seed solution into 1  mL PbI2/CsBr solution. For the preparation of  
FAI/MABr/MACl solution, 733.3 mg FAI, 73.3 mg MABr, and 76.7 mg 
MACl powder were dissolved in 10 mL IPA and stirred for about 90 min 
under room temperature. For the preparation of Spiro-OMeTAD solution, 
73.4  mg Spiro-OMeTAD, 17.6  µL Li-TFSI solution (520  mg Li-TFSI in 
1 mL ACN), and 28.8 µL 4-TBP were mixed in 1 mL chlorobenzene and 
stirred overnight. The water-based TiO2 colloid precursor was prepared 
according to previous work.[42] Briefly, the TiO2-Cl nanoparticles were 
synthesized following modified nonhydrolytic sol–gel method and the 
nanocrystals sediment were dispersed in ultrapure water to make a 
suspension.
Perovskite Solar Cells Fabrication: Indium tin oxide (ITO) coated 
glasses (≈15 Ω square−1) were prepatterned by retailer. The ITO-
coated glasses were cleaned by ultrasonic cleaner using acetone and 
isopropanol sequentially before use. After cleaning, water-based TiO2 
colloid precursor was spin-coated on the ITO at 4000  rpm for 30 s. 
Finally, the substrates were annealed on a hot plate at 150 °C for 30 min 
in ambient air. After annealing and cooling to room temperature, the 
substrates were transferred into N2-filled glovebox. The perovskite 
films were fabricated via a two-step CsCl-enhanced PbI2 precursor 
method introduced in a previous work.[17] Firstly, the PbI2/CsCl/control-
seed, PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed or PbI2/CsBr/Br-rich seed mixed solution 
was spin-coated on the substrates for 20 s at 1500  rpm and 60 s at 
5000 rpm, a 2 min annealing at 80 °C was followed up. The FAI/MABr/
MACl solution was subsequently spin-coated on the PbI2/CsCl/control-
seed or PbI2/CsCl/Br-rich seed layer after it cooling down to room 
temperature. The spin-coated program was 1700 rpm for 30 s. Then, the 
film was annealed at 40 °C for 2 min in glovebox and 140 °C for 35 min 
in air sequentially. Finally, the perovskite film was washed by isopropanol 
using drop-casting method at 4500 rpm for 30 s after cooling to room 
temperature, a 5 min annealing at 100 °C was followed up to remove 
residual isopropanol. For the hole-transport layer, it was obtained by 
spin coating the Spiro-OMeTAD solution on the top of perovskite film at 
3000 rpm for 30 s. At last, on top of the Spiro-OMeTAD layer, a 80 nm 
Au electrode was thermally evaporated.
Solar Cells Characterization: An Agilent B2900 Series precision 
source/measure unit was used to perform current density–voltage (J–V) 
characterization. During the test, the cells were illuminated under AM 
1.5G irradiation (100 mW cm−2) by a Xeon lamp solar simulator (Solar 
IV-150A, Zolix). A Newport calibrated KG5-filtered Si reference cell was 
used to calibrate the light intensity. The scanning rate of the J–V curves 
was 50 mV s−1 (voltage step of 10 mV and delay time of 200 ms), and 
the scaning scope and direction was from 1.15 to −0.01 V (reverse) or 
from −0.01 to 1.15 V (forward). For the EQE measurement, a solar cell 
QE/IPCE measurement system (Solar Cell Scan100, Zolix) was used, 
and it was also calibrated by a certified silicon solar cells. Meanwhile, 
the scan interval was 2  nm and no bias light was used during the 
measurement. The stability test was performed in a N2-filled glovebox 
under room-temperature and measured under AM 1.5G solar simulator 
(XES-50S1, AAA class, SAN-EI electric co., LTD). A 420  nm cutoff UV 
filter was applied. The stability test was performed by setting the bias 
voltage to the initial max power point and tracing the current density. 
Under all measurement above, the perovskite solar cells’ active area was 
fixed at 4.9 mm2 by a mask.
Perovskite Film Characterization: The top-view SEM images of the 
perovskite films and the cross-sectional SEM images of the PSC 
devices were recorded by a scanning electron microscope (Quanta 
FEG 450) under 5 kV electron beam accelerate voltage. A DMAX-2400 
X-ray diffractometer system using monochromatized Cu Kα radiation

The XPS measurements were conducted on a ThermoFisher Scientific 
ESCALAB 250X system. Al Kα (1486.6 eV) was used as X-ray source and 
the analyzer was put at 54.7° relative to the source. Photoluminescence 
spectrum and time-resolved PL decay spectrum were collected using an 
Edinburgh Instruments Ltd. FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer system. 
The perovskite samples were growing on glass, instead of the subtracts 
using in solar cells. And the exciting light illuminated from the bottom 
side of the perovskite film. For the in situ PL characterization combined 
with galvanostatic measurement under light illumination, an Agilent 
B2901A Series precision source/measure unit was used to applied 80 nA 
static current and an Olympus FV3000 CLSM was used to collect PL 
signal. For the CLSM, a 40× dry objective (LUCPLFLN40X, 0.6 NA, WD 
2.7–4.0) and a 561 nm laser diode were employed. The PL emission was 
collected by the same objective and detected by photomultiplier with 
800 nm detect wavelength upper limit. The scan interval was 5 nm.

file: Advanced Materials - 2024 - Heo - Inorganic‐Derived 0D Perovskite Induced Surface Lattice Arrangement for Efficient and.pdf

text:
RESEARCH ARTICLE
www.advmat.de
Inorganic-Derived 0D Perovskite Induced Surface Lattice
Arrangement for Eﬃcient and Stable All-Inorganic
Perovskite Solar Cells
Jin Hyuck Heo,* Jin Kyoung Park, Hyong Joon Lee, Eun Ha Shin, Seok Yeong Hong,
Ki-Ha Hong,* Fei Zhang,* and Sang Hyuk Im*
The inverted inorganic CsPbI3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are prospective
candidates for next-generation photovoltaics owing to inherent robust
thermal/photo-stability and compatibility for tandems. However,
the performance and stability of the inverted CsPbI3 PSCs fall behind the n-i-p
counterparts due to poor energetic alignment and abundant interfacial defect
states. Here, an inorganic 0D Cs4PbBr6 with a good lattice strain arrangement is
implemented as the surface anchoring capping layer on CsPbI3. The Cs4PbBr6
perovskite induces enhanced electron-selective junction and thus facilitates
eﬃcient charge extraction and eﬀectively inhibits non-radiative recombination.
Consequently, the CsPbI3 PSCs with Cs4PbBr6 demonstrate the highest power
conversion eﬃciency (PCE) of CsPbI3-based inverted PSCs, reaching 21.03%
PCE from a unit cell and 17.39% PCE from a module with a 64 cm2 aperture
area. Furthermore, the resulting devices retain 92.48% after 1000 h under si-
multaneous 1-sun and damp heat (85 °C / 85% relative humidity) environment.
1. Introduction
In the last decade, the organic–inorganic metal halide perovskite
(MHP) has steeply elevated its coordinates in the solar cell ef-
ﬁciency chart and reached a record 26.1% power conversion
J. H. Heo, J. K. Park, H. J. Lee, S. Y. Hong, S. H. Im
Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Korea University
Seoul 02841, Republic of Korea
E-mail: live2000jin@korea.ac.kr;imromy@korea.ac.kr
E.H.Shin,K.-H.Hong
Department of Materials ScienceandEngineering
Hanbat National University
Daejeon 34158,Republicof Korea
E-mail: kiha.hong@hanbat.ac.kr
F.Zhang
School of Chemical Engineering andTechnology
Tianjin University
Tianjin 300072,China
E-mail: fei_zhang@tju.edu.cn
F.Zhang
CollaborativeInnovation Centerof Chemical ScienceandEngineering
(Tianjin)
Tianjin 300072,China
The ORCID identiﬁcation number(s) for the author(s) of this article
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202408387
DOI: 10.1002/adma.202408387
eﬃciency (PCE), leaving only a single
absolute percentage away from par with
the
orthodox
crystalline
Si-based
solar
cells.[1]
However,
despite
the
dazzling
performance of the state-of-the-art per-
ovskite solar cells, long-term operational
challenges of the organic–inorganic hy-
brid MHPs comprising formamidinium
(FA) and methylammonium (MA) leave
their commercial debut at the photovoltaic
market doubtable. Accordingly, to develop
viable MHP photovoltaic technology, signif-
icant interests in perovskites have diverged
to
inorganic
counterparts
(CsPbI3-xBrx,
X = 0–3) exhibiting robust thermal- and
photo-stability because they have relatively
high thermal decomposition temperature
of their constituents and high activation
energy barriers against ion migration.[2–10]
Meanwhile, developing eﬃcient inorganic PSCs with an in-
verted p-i-n architecture[11–16] has been of great interest because
they have suitable energy bandgap (>1.65 eV)[17–19] for top cells in
perovskite/Si tandem solar cells. Moreover, the p-i-n architecture
is more suitable for inorganic perovskites, which are easily dete-
riorated by water ingress because they do not require hygroscopic
dopants. Consequently, intensive studies have been conducted to
develop inverted inorganic PSCs through various strategies, such
as phase stabilization,[20–24] defect-rich surface passivation,[25–30]
and introducing interlayers.[4,31–33] Nonetheless, the inverted p-
i-n inorganic PSCs only recently reached 20% PCE,[28,29,32] and
their eﬃciency is still behind that of hybrid perovskites due to
poor energetic alignment and abundant interfacial defect states
hindering the electron charge extraction.[25,27,34,35]
Recently, bi-layered PSCs with both 3D and low-dimensional
(LD) perovskite stacks using bulky organic ammonium halides
have emerged as a promising solution to the trade-oﬀbetween
photovoltaic performance and operational stability.[36–41] How-
ever, the spontaneously formed LD stacks concomitantly accom-
pany unsettled lattice mismatch in quasi-LD phases, and the ex-
pansion of these complex phases through the bulk under external
stresses limits the eﬀectiveness and reliability of this approach at
the long-term scale.[42–45] Thus, an LD stack with a better lattice
arrangement is desired to exploit the stability of the inorganic
perovskites.
Here, we demonstrated eﬃcient inverted CsPbI3 solar cells
from an eﬀective 3D- 0D all-inorganic heterostructure through

Figure 1. Synthesis and characterization of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. a) Schematic illustration depicting the synthesis process for Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. b) TEM
image of synthesized Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, with an inset showing a high-magniﬁcation TEM image. c) XRD pattern of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. d) UV-absorption
spectrum of the corresponding Cs4PbBr6 PNCs with inset showing optical photographs under lab light and UV light conditions.
spray-coating of Cs4PbBr6 perovskite nanocrystals (PNCs). As
an inorganic 0D perovskite derivative, Cs4PbBr6 with isolated
PbBr6
4−octahedra surrounded by Cs+ ions has similar lattice
spacing, acting as a lattice strain holder with the 3D counter-
parts and exhibits enclosed phase stability and excellent thermal
stability.[46–49] Furthermore, this structure simultaneously real-
izes a robust barrier from environmental stress and ideal per-
ovskite band oﬀset (PBO) heterostructure with the electron trans-
port layer (ETL). As a result, the champion CsPbI3 PSCs with
0D PBO layer showed signiﬁcant improvement from the control
device and remarkable performance, reaching the highest PCE
of 21.03% of CsPbI3-based inverted PSCs from a unit cell and
17.39% PCE from a 64 cm2 module. Finally, the 0D Cs4PbBr6 val-
idated excellent protection, and the device retained 92.48% with
encapsulation under simultaneous 1-sun light and damp heat
(85 °C/85% relative humidity; RH) ambient for 1000 h.
2. Results and Discussion
2.1. Synthesis and Characterization of Pure Phase Cs4PbBr6
PNCs
A novel method has been developed to synthesize pure 0D
Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, showcased in Figure 1a. This approach eﬀec-
tively counters halide deﬁciency during synthesis by utilizing a
stoichiometric reaction mediated by hydrogen bromide (HBr).
The initial step in the synthesis process involves the preparation
of Cs-oleate (Cs-OA) and PbBr2-ligand solutions. When mixed in
toluene, these solutions form the reaction medium, which is then
stirred at room temperature. Introducing aqueous HBr initiates
the rapid formation of cesium bromide (CsBr) at the synthesis
onset, achieving the 4:1 stoichiometric ratio essential for synthe-
sizing phase-pure 0D Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. For detailed experimental
procedures, please refer to the Experimental Section.
To elucidate the synthesis mechanism of phase-pure Cs4PbBr6
PNCs with HBr, the reaction was monitored over time using
UV–vis spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy
(TEM). The evolution observed in the UV–vis spectra (Figure
S1a, Supporting Information) and the morphological changes
observed in TEM (Figure S2a–i, Supporting Information) high-
light the dynamic changes during the reaction. When comparing
the UV–vis spectra of Cs-OA, PbBr2-ligand, and Cs-OA/PbBr2-
ligand complex (Figure S1b, Supporting Information), the initial
spectra for Cs-OA and PbBr2-ligand showed peaks at 282 and
292 nm, respectively. Upon mixing, these peaks disappeared, and
a new peak quickly emerged at 326 nm, indicating the formation
of a new complex between Cs-OA and PbBr2-ligand. Following
the addition of HBr, the peak corresponding to the Cs-OA/PbBr2-
ligand complex vanished within 2 min, with quadrilateral-shaped
particles becoming observable in TEM images (Figure S2a,b,

Detailed
examination
of
the
quadrilateral-shaped particles showed smaller particles re-
siding on the surface, identiﬁed as PbBr2-ligand particles
from prior research (Figure S2d,e, Supporting Information).[50]
Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) image analysis of the quadrilat-
eral NCs revealed d-spacing valued at 0.43 nm/ 0.31 nm and
0.43 nm/0.30 nm, aligning with the {100} and {110} planes
of bulk CsBr powder, as conﬁrmed by X-ray diﬀraction (XRD)
patterns (Figure S3, Supporting Information), suggesting CsBr
formation upon HBr addition. After 3 min, the appearance of a
new peak at 313 nm in the UV–vis spectra signaled the formation
of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, with TEM images indicating a transition to
uniform round-shaped particles (Figure S2c,f, Supporting Infor-
mation), expected to be the initial state of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. The
intensity of the associated peak increased, achieving saturation at
30 min, and TEM images revealed hexagonal-shaped Cs4PbBr6
PNCs (Figure 2Sg–i, Supporting Information), indicating the
completion of the reaction within ≈30 min.
The reaction scheme is summarized as follows:
4Cs −OA + 4HBr →4CsBr + HOA
(1)
PbBr2 −ligands + 4CsBr →Cs4PbBr6PNCs
(2)
The synthesized Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, illustrated in the TEM image
of Figure 1b and its corresponding FFT image (Figure S4a, Sup-
porting Information), exhibit a well-arranged structure without
any byproducts. Analysis of the TEM image with image J deter-
mined the average particle size as 19.83 ± 1.88 nm, highlighting
the uniformity of the synthesis process (Figure S5, Supporting
Information). Additionally, lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.68
and 0.39 nm were revealed in the high-magniﬁcation TEM inset
image and its FFT image (Figure S4b, Supporting Information),
conﬁrming the crystalline nature of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs.[51] The
rhombohedral structure of the PNCs was conﬁrmed by the XRD
pattern in Figure 1c and FFT images, with purity veriﬁed by X-
ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Figure S6, Sup-
porting Information). Investigation into the optical properties of
0D Cs4PbBr6 PNCs showed sharp peaks at 313 nm in the UV–vis
spectrum in Figure 1d, with no visible absorption, indicative of
3D CsPbBr3 PNCs, thus conﬁrming their phase purity. The ab-
sence of luminescence under UV light further emphasized the
distinct phase purity of synthesized NCs. This study clariﬁes the
HBr-driven formation of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs and emphasizes the
signiﬁcance of stoichiometric precision and halide mediation in
synthesizing high-quality PNCs.
2.2. Interfacial Lattice Arrangements and Characterization of
3D/0D Perovskite Films
To better understand the interaction between 0D Cs4PbBr6 PNCs
and 3D CsPbI3 perovskite ﬁlms, we conducted detailed analy-
ses using TEM and grazing incidence X-ray diﬀraction (GIXRD).
We initially deposited layers of CsPbI3 and Cs4PbBr6 PNCs on
an indium tin oxide (ITO) coated substrate, employing the same
spray coating parameters used in device fabrication to replicate
the device structure closely. Subsequently, cross-sectional sam-
ples were prepared using focused ion beam (FIB) milling to ex-
amine the layered structure and interfaces precisely. Scanning
electron microscopy (SEM) depicted the distinct layers. As shown
in Figure 2a, the SEM images demonstrate a well-deﬁned lay-
ered structure with the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer measuring ≈45 nm
thick, conﬁrming successful deposition atop the CsPbI3 layer.
The thickness of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer corresponds to the
stacking of two Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. However, due to the mixing at
the interface with CsPbI3, the actual thickness comprising solely
Cs4PbBr6 is thinner than it appears. This observation, aligned
with time-of-ﬂight mass spectroscopy (ToF-SIMS) results from
the PBO device (Figure S16, Supporting Information), under-
scores the accuracy of our structural replication.
Further microscopic examination via TEM oﬀered deeper in-
sights into the lattice arrangements at the interfaces. As depicted
in Figure 2b, the analysis revealed varied orientations within the
Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer compared to the more uniformly oriented
lattice in the underlying CsPbI3 layer. This variation likely re-
sults from the random stacking of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, evident at
the interface. To further clarify the interfacial lattice conﬁgura-
tion, we conducted an in-depth examination of this region, crit-
ically assessing the alignment of lattice planes. Upon closer in-
spection of the interface in the TEM, the predominant d-spacing
in the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer was measured at 0.34 nm, consistent
with the strongest (024) peak from the XRD analysis depicted in
Figure 1c. Conversely, the CsPbI3 layer displayed a d-spacing of
0.32 nm, corresponding to the (200) plane observed in its XRD re-
sults, as shown in Figure 2d. A continuous lattice structure with
closely matched d-spacing was observed between these layers.
Notably, at the interface, a slight expansion of the CsPbI3 lattice
to 0.33 nm was observed, suggesting subtle lattice strain induced
by the presence of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs lattice.
To further verify these lattice parameter changes, we compared
the XRD results of the PBO and control samples. We conducted a
GIXRD analysis on the PBO sample, as shown in Figure 2d. The
comparison revealed a shift to a lower angle in the PBO sample’s
(200) plane peak, indicating lattice expansion due to the align-
ment with the larger 0.34 nm Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. As the angle of
incidence increases, allowing deeper X-ray penetration, the peak
shifts to a higher angle, suggesting that the CsPbI3 lattice ex-
pands and is ﬁxed by the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs at the interface, with the
eﬀect diminishing farther from the interface. The changes in the
(200) plane lattice parameters of CsPbI3 are shown in Figure 2e.
These results align well with the TEM ﬁndings, conﬁrming that
Cs4PbBr6 PNCs can anchor the CsPbI3 lattice, enhancing phase
stability and ultimately aﬀecting device stability. This thorough
analysis provides critical insights into the lattice dynamics and
stability at the interface between 0D Cs4PbBr6 PNCs and 3D
CsPbI3 perovskite layers, demonstrating the signiﬁcant impact of
interface engineering on the performance and longevity of PSCs.
The well-deﬁned interface structures between 0D and 3D
perovskites can be attributed to structural coherence facilitated
by forming ordered vacancies in 3D structures. Speciﬁcally,
Cs4PbBr6 exhibits a trigonal lattice structure, as depicted in
Figure S7a (Supporting Information), representing its unit cell
structure. The primitive cell of Cs4PbBr6 is triclinic, with an-
gles closely ≈90° (88.85°), as illustrated in Figure S7b (Support-
ing Information). The correlation between 3D and 0D structures
is further demonstrated by transforming 3D perovskite struc-
tures into 0D conﬁgurations. This transformation involves ini-
tially generating CsPbBr3 2 × 2 × 2 supercells, as shown in

Figure S7c (Supporting Information). Subsequently, selecting six
PbBr2 units (indicated by small balls and green diamonds in
Figure S7d, Supporting Information) and removing them (Figure
S7e, Supporting Information) are followed in this process. The
optimized lattice structures resulting from these ordered-vacancy
conﬁgurations were computed using density functional the-
ory (DFT), yielding triclinic structures as presented in Figure
S7f (Supporting Information). These computed lattice constants
(9.81 Å) and angles (88.85°) closely align with the experimental
data (lattice constants of 9.80 Å and similar angles).
2.3. The Properties of 3D/0D Perovskite Films
Having conﬁrmed the synthesis of phase-pure and uniform
Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, the wide bandgap Cs4PbBr6 PNCs are imple-
mented on the surface of the inorganic CsPbI3 perovskite as a
PBO layer. The band energy levels of the PBO samples are de-
rived from the taut plot in Figure S8 (Supporting Information)
and the UV photoelectron spectroscopy in Figure S9 (Support-
ing Information). The resulting band structure is illustrated in
Figure S10 (Supporting Information).[52,53] The Cs4PbBr6 PNCs
exhibit a wide-band structure protruding over the conduction
band of the CsPbI3 layer. Accordingly, a thin enough PBO layer
is expected to suppress undesired radiative recombination that is
especially prevalent at the interface and simultaneously facilitate
charge transfer through a spike-like heterojunction favorable for
electron extraction.[40,54–56]
The SEM image depicts the PBO sample (Figure 3a) and the
control sample (Figure 3b) and reveals conformal and uniform
deposition of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs on the surface. The static pho-
toluminescence (PL) spectra of the PBO and the control samples
are compared in Figure 3c. Upon the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs deposition
on the CsPbI3 ﬁlm, the PL intensity is greatly enhanced, sug-
gesting the passivation role of the Cs4PbBr6 interlayer from sup-
pressed non-radiative recombination. Moreover, while the PBO
sample with C60 pyrrolidine tris-acid (CPTA) ETL exhibits an
abrupt PL diminution similar to the control sample, the larger PL
quenching ratio implicates a promoted charge transfer.[56] There-
fore, despite the wide bandgap property of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs,
the thin enough layer can facilitate extract charge across the PBO
layer through the augmented electron transport interface.
The transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) is conducted for
the PBO and the control samples with CPTA ETL in Figure 3d to
probe the charge carrier kinetics further. The pronounced pho-
tobleaching occurs within the initial ≈1 ps pump-probe delay
time at ≈733 nm, corresponding to the band-edge of CsPbI3, due
to the band ﬁlling by photoexcited carriers from both samples.
Moreover, two positive peaks, indicating photo-induced absorp-
tions, are observed at the high and low energy of the bandgap

Figure 3. Characterization of CsPbI3 perovskite ﬁlms with and without Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer. a,b) Surface SEM images of CsPbI3 perovskite ﬁlms a)
with and b) without Cs4PbBr6 PNCs. c) Static PL spectra of the CsPbI3 perovskite and PBO ﬁlms and their spectra with CPTA ETL. d) TAS spectra of the
CsPbI3/CPTA ﬁlms with and without Cs4PbBr6 PNCs at the interface. e) The normalized bleaching spectra of corresponding TAS spectra. f) SCLC plot
of the CsPbI3 perovskite and PBO ﬁlms under dark.
energy.[57] The photo-induced absorption peak at the high energy
side exhibits instantaneous emersion with the pump and slow
decay alongside the photobleached peaks. It can be ascribed to
the alterations to the refractive indices due to photoexcitation.
Meanwhile, the rapidly decaying peak at the sub-bandgap energy
level could be attributed to the hot carriers generated from sur-
plus energy undergoing the subsequent relaxation process. The
cooling process of the hot carriers is accompanied by the dissi-
pation of the excess energy and is accelerated by trap states.[57–60]
Hence, the prolonged decay lasting up to 5 ps at the sub-bandgap
energy from the PBO sample suggests the suppression of the
non-radiative recombination channels. Furthermore, the charge
transfer rate can be compared by recovering the bleaching spectra
at the bandgap energy accommodated with the extraction of free
carriers by the electron transport layer. The normalized changes
in absorption at 733 nm of the PBO and the control samples are
plotted along the pump-probe delay time, as shown in Figure 3e.
The PBO sample demonstrated faster decay with a 147 ps lifetime
compared to a 347 ps decay lifetime from the control sample, in-
dicating feasible charge transfer over the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs.[61,62]
Thus, the combined charge carrier studies through PL and TAS
analysis conﬁrm the beneﬁcial role of the Cs4PbBr6 band oﬀset
layer in eﬀectively suppressing non-radiative recombination and
promoting charge extraction.
The space-charge limited currents (SCLC) are measured in the
dark from electron-only devices of the PBO and control samples
using SnO2 as the bottom ETL to be substituted for the PTAA
hole transport layer (HTL) and are presented in Figure 3f. The
assumptions made in this technique research may be questioned
when considering the halide perovskite materials. However, it of-
fers a comparative assessment of the trap densities and allows
us to approximate them for comparable perovskite ﬁlms.[63] The
kink-point of the SCLC plot indicates the potential at which ﬁll-
ing of the residing traps occurs, and the trap-ﬁlled voltage (VTFL)
is obtained as 0.48 and 0.72 V for the PBO and the control sample,
respectively. Here, the trap densities (Nt) can be estimated from
the VTFL using the relation Nt = (2ɛɛ0VTFL)/qL2, where q, L, ɛ, and
ɛ0 represent elementary charge, active layer thickness, relative di-
electric constant, and vacuum permittivity, respectively.[64,65] The
calculated trap densities are 3.39 × 1015 cm−3 for the PBO sam-
ple and 5.08 × 1015 cm−3 for the control sample. Electron mobil-
ity measurements, as shown in Figure S11 (Supporting Informa-
tion), indicated no signiﬁcant changes. Accordingly, the smaller
defect densities present from the SCLC analysis imply that the

2.4. Characterization of 0D/3D Perovskite Solar Cells
To evaluate the impact
of the PBO layers on
the de-
vice performance, we prepared the inverted devices with
ITO/PTAA/CsPbI3/ with (PBO) or without (control) Cs4PbBr6
PNCs/CPTA/BCP/Ag structure. The current density–voltage
(J–V) curves of the PBO devices and control devices under 1 Sun
illumination are presented in Figure 4a. Various concentrations
of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs from 10, 20, to 30 wt.% have been employed
for perovskite solar cells to opt for the optimized PBO layer. The
J–V scans of the CsPbI3 solar cells with 10 and 30 wt.% Cs4PbBr6
PNCs are shown in Figure S12 (Supporting Information). The
resulting photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1,
and the corresponding statistical box plots are shown in Figure
S13 (Supporting Information). The introduction of Cs4PbBr6
PNCs follows enhancement in overall photovoltaic parameters
up to 20 wt.% and displays detrimental eﬀect upon excessive
dosage at 30 wt.%, possibly due to an overly thick Cs4PbBr6 layer

impeding charge transport. Consequently, the optimized PBO
layer was ﬁnalized at 20 wt.% and was selected for the following
in-depth assessments.
The best-performing PBO device exhibits 1.23 V open-circuit
voltage (VOC), 20.16 mA cm−2 short-circuit current density (JSC),
84.79% ﬁll factor (FF), and 21.03% PCE from the reverse scan
direction and 1.22 V VOC, 20.16 mA cm−2 JSC, 84.45% FF and
20.77% PCE from the forward scan direction. Such remarkable
performance achieved with PBO layers exceeds the 18.94% PCE
in the reverse scan and 18.80% PCE in the forward scan from the
control device and stands at the zenith among the state-of-the-art
inverted inorganic PSCs. The PBO and the control device’s stabi-
lized power output (SPO) maintain constant eﬃciency at 20.71%
and 18.80%, respectively, as presented in Figure S14 (Supporting
Information), thus validating the performance measured from
J-V scans. In addition, the corresponding devices’ external quan-
tum eﬃciency (EQE) is shown in Figure 4b, and the integrated
JSC are 19.90 and 19.94 mA cm−2 for the PBO and the control
device, respectively, consistent with the JSC measured from the
J-V scans. Such results and prior studies on charge kinetics al-
lude that the PBO layer signiﬁcantly suppresses the recombina-
tion and enhances charge extraction, thereby contributing to the
elevated FF and VOC.
Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was
conducted to probe carrier dynamics from the working devices.
The Nyquist plot, measured under dark at the short-circuit condi-
tion (0 V) and the near-open-circuit condition (1.1 V) for the PBO
and the control samples, is presented in Figure S15a (Supporting
Information). Notably, the low-frequency response is associated
with carrier recombination at the interface, and therefore larger
semicircles represent stronger charge recombination resistance
(Rrec).[52,66] The extracted Rrec were 21.376 and 1.852 kΩ at 0 and
1.1 V, respectively, for the PBO device and 11.325 and 0.854 kΩ at
0 and 1.1 V, respectively, for the control device. Moreover, the ob-
tained Rrec of the PBO and the control devices are plotted through
varying potentials, as shown in Figure S15b (Supporting Infor-
mation). The PBO device consistently maintains twice as high
Rrec over the entire range of applied bias, conﬁrming superior
resistance against interfacial recombination. However, the intro-
duction of the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer resulted in a slight decrease
in conductivity, subsequently causing a modest increase in the se-
ries resistance (Rs) of the PBO device to 18.77 Ω, in comparison
to 13.42 Ω observed in the control device.
Furthermore, the electrical potential distribution throughout
the PBO and control device stacks was examined by scanning
with the Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM). The KPFM mea-
surement was conducted in an open-circuit condition under the
dark and illumination, thus endowing accurate information by
closely simulating the operational state.[67–69] The cross-sectional
SEM image of the PBO and the control devices are presented
in Figure 4c,d, respectively, and the obtained potential distri-
butions were aligned accordingly. As the illumination is shed
upon the solar cells, signiﬁcant potentials are built across the
device stacks, which are recorded in Figure 4e,f. The electrical
ﬁeld was extracted from the derivative of the potential diﬀer-
ence, which allows operand observations of the junctions formed
in photodiodes. Here, establishing a main junction is ideal for
eﬃcient operation, as junctions with inferior quality limit the
performance of the multiple junctions.[4,68,70,71] Interestingly, the
PBO device presents a singular, broad potential drop extend-
ing from the Perovskite–ETL interface across the entire device.
In contrast, the control device exhibits narrow potential drops
at two distinct positions at the HTL-Perovskite and Perovskite–
ETL interface. Consequently, the derived electrical ﬁeld reveals
the formation of a unique dominant junction by introducing
the PBO layer from the competing junctions at the respective
charge extraction interfaces. The PBO layer forms a distinct in-
terface at the Perovskite–ETL, as conﬁrmed by the ToF-SIMS
depth proﬁle shown in Figure S16 (Supporting Information),
and constructs a main junction advantageous for charge ex-
traction from the aggrandized n-type interface, which is in ac-
cordance with the prior charge dynamic studies. Consequently,
the electrical analyses and the KPFM measurement manifest
that the superior performance of the PBO device originated
from the minimized recombination losses and promoted charge
extraction.
In addition to the photovoltaic performance, scale-up and
long-term stability are other critical issues to be resolved to
conﬁrm the reliability of their photovoltaic operation in vari-
ous environments. The PCE comparison of inverted inorganic
CsPbI3 modules with or without PBO modiﬁcation based on
25 and 64 cm2 (Figure 5a) aperture area modules are shown
in Figures S17,S18,S5b and Table S1 (Supporting Information).
The champion PBO modules reach better PCE (18.99% and
17.39% PCE for 25 and 64 cm2 aperture area, respectively)
compared to the control modules, which are the highest PCE
for the reported modules on inorganic perovskites. Moreover,
the SPO measurements in Figure 5c exhibit consistent photo-
voltaic performance with the I–V curve results. Accordingly, the
remarkable performance from PBO modules conﬁrms the ef-
fectiveness of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs capping layers in a large area
scale.
Furthermore, the stability assessments are evaluated for the
PBO and the control devices over 1000 h period with their PCEs
tracked incessantly at their maximum power point under con-
tinuous 1-sun light irradiation in three diﬀerent protocols: (Pro-
tocol 1) mild ambient at 25 °C / 50% RH without encapsula-
tion, (Protocol 2) humid environment at 25 °C / 85% RH with-
out encapsulation, (Protocol 3) damp heat environment at 85 °C
/ 85% RH with encapsulation. The J–V curve evolutions under
Protocol 1–3 are presented in Figure 5d and Figure S19a,b (Sup-
porting Information) for the PBO devices and Figure 5e and
Figure S19d,e (Supporting Information) for the control devices.
Moreover, the SPO measurements throughout the stability eval-
uation under various protocols are displayed in Figure 5f and
Figure S19c (Supporting Information) for the PBO devices and in
Figure 5f and Figure S19f (Supporting Information) for the con-
trol devices, along with the photovoltaic parameters summarized
in Table S2 (Supporting Information). Although both the PBO
and the control devices have exhibited signiﬁcant performance
degeneration without encapsulation, the PBO devices demon-
strated reinforced stability, retaining 76.33% and 66.03% of their
initial PCE under Protocol 1 and Protocol 2, respectively, which
is higher than the 56.38% and 48.18% from the control devices.
Upon encapsulation, both devices demonstrate exceptional sta-
bility in Protocol 3, exhibiting degradation of only 7.52% for the
PBO device and 8.71% for the control device, even under a harsh
damp heat environment. Additionally, we tested the stability of

the encapsulated 64 cm2 PBO module using Protocol 3, and the
results, shown in Figure S20 (Supporting Information), demon-
strated a degradation of ≈14.16% for the 64 cm2 module. Con-
sequently, the thorough stability assessments reﬂect the supe-
rior stability imposed by the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs, contributing sig-
niﬁcantly to the strengthened long-term operation of the PBO
devices.
3. Conclusion
In summary, an inorganic 0D Cs4PbBr6 capping layer is imple-
mented on the CsPbI3 to prepare eﬃcient p-i-n inorganic per-
ovskite solar cells. Our HBr-mediated stoichiometric reaction
procedure conﬁrms the synthesis of the impurity-free Cs4PbBr6
PNCs. The PBO ﬁlms with Cs4PbBr6 demonstrate excellent
charge extraction capability and suppression of the non-radiative
recombination. The PBO devices exhibited a remarkable perfor-
mance of 21.03% PCE, exceeding the 18.83% PCE of the control
devices, from the enhanced recombination resistance and rein-
forced junction formation at the electron-selective interface. Fur-
thermore, the inorganic PBO module demonstrated exceptional
17.39% PCE from an aperture area of 64 cm2 and the PBO devices
validated improvement from long-term stability assessments un-
der various protocols. Therefore, our strategy to prepare the all-
inorganic 3D-0D capping layer proves a viable approach for de-
veloping stable and eﬃcient CsPbI3 photovoltaic cells.
4. Experimental Section
Materials:
Lead(II) bromide (PbBr2, 99.99%), Lead (II) iodide (PbI2,
99.99%) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., LTD, and
Cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3, 99.9%), Cesium iodide (CsI, 99.999%), Hy-
drobromic acid (HBr, 48 wt.% in H2O, ≥99.99%), Dimethyl ammo-
nium iodide (DMAI, 98%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%), N-
methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5%), toluene (TOL, anhydrous, 99.8%),
C60 Pyrrolidine tris-acid (CPTA, 97%) were purchased from Sigma-
Aldrich. PTAA (molecular weight 18 500) was purchased from EMNI Co.,
Ltd. 2,9-dimethyl-4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline (BCP, >99%) was pur-
chased from Lumtec. Patterned tin-doped indium oxide (ITO) substrates
(<10 Ω sq−1.) were purchased from 2H Co., Ltd. All reagents were used
without further puriﬁcation.
Synthesis of Cs4PbBr6 PNCs:
Cs4PbBr6 perovskite nanocrystals were
synthesized by adjusting the temperature and the reacting precursor ratio
based on the previous work. First, two precursor solutions were prepared,
Cs-OA and PbBr2-ligands precursors. To prepare a Cs-OA precursor so-
lution, Cs2CO3 (2 mmol, 0.326 g) and OA (3 mL) were left to react in an
oil-bath set at 80 °C until Cs2CO3 powder was completely dissolved in TOL
(7 mL). Likewise, the PbBr2-ligands precursor solution was prepared in an
80 °C oil-bath. PbBr2 (1 mmol, 0.367 g), OA (1.5 mL), and OAm (4 mL)
were left to react until PbBr2 powder was fully dissolved in TOL (14.5 mL).
In the following step, 1 mL PbBr2–ligands precursor solution was added to
28 mL TOL, respectively, and stirred at room temperature. While stirring,
1 mL Cs-OA precursor solution was dropped, and then 22.6 μL HBr was
dropped. (The precursor preparation process and the nanocrystal synthe-
sis process were carried out in ambient conditions without gas injection or
vacuum state.) After reacting for 30 min, the stirring was stopped. Then, all
solvent in the sample was evaporated using a rotary evaporator to remove

text:
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.advmat.de
water from the sample. After the solvent removal, the collected nanocrys-
tals were redispersed in toluene.
Device Fabrication:
(Standard device) The patterned ITO-coated glass
substrates were washed by ultrasonication with deionized water, acetone,
and isopropanol for 15 min for each. Then, the substrates were dried by
dried air and treated with UV ozone treatment for 15 min. PTAA solu-
tion (5 mg mL−1) was dissolved in the mixed solvent of NMP: toluene
(v:v = 1:1) was spin-coated on ITO substrates at 6000 rpm for 30 s, fol-
lowed by annealing at 100 °C for 10 min. For spray coating, CsPbI3 per-
ovskite, CsI (0.65 g), PbI (1.15 g) and DMAI (0.43 g) were dissolved in
10 mL mixed solvent of DMF: NMP (v:v = 8.5:1.5), vigorously stirred for 1 h
to make it fully dissolved. The CsPbI3 perovskite thin ﬁlm was deposited on
PTAA/ITO substrate by spraying the CsPbI3 perovskite precursor solution
using an ultrasonic spray coater (S80, CERA-TORQ, 80 kHz) combined
with the 3D printer (CREALITA, CR-10S PRO) where the perovskite pre-
cursor solution was delivered by a syringe pump (KD-Scientiﬁc, KDS100).
The Cs4PbBr6 perovskite band oﬀset layer was deposited on top of the
CsPbI3 perovskite thin ﬁlm layer with the solution of Cs4PbBr6 perovskite
nanocrystals dispersed in toluene (20 wt.%) via the same equipment setup
used above. The ultrasonic spray coating process conditions were noz-
zle to substrate distance of 5 cm, a solution ﬂow rate of 0.8 mL min−1
(CsPbI3), and 1.0 mL min−1 (Cs4PbBr6) (ﬂow gas: N2/pressure: 7 psi),
and a deposition temperature of 170 °C. The perovskite-coated substrate
was annealed at 150 °C for 10 min. Afterward, the sample was transferred
to a thermal evaporator for CPTA (30 nm)/BCP (1 nm)/Ag (100 nm) de-
position. The deposition rate of 0.5, 0.2, and 2.0 Å s−1, respectively. The
device area by evaporation was 0.16 cm2.
(Module device) The modules were fabricated following a fabrica-
tion process similar to that used for the standard devices, except for
the P1, P2, and P3 scribing steps, which employed a laser scriber (ND:
YVO4, 355 nm, 6 W). Initially, the large area ITO substrate was patterned
by applying a P1 line to isolate the bottom electrodes. Afterward, the
substrates were thoroughly washed in an ultrasonication bath. Subse-
quently, the SnO2, perovskite, and spiro-OMeTAD layers were sequen-
tially deposited using the earlier coating procedures. To establish a se-
ries connection between the cells and facilitate the deposition of Au elec-
trodes, the prepared stacks were scribed with P2 lines. Finally, P3 scribes
were patterned on the evaporated ﬁlms to isolate the top electrodes.
The width information of P1, P2, and P3 is in Figure S21 (Supporting
Information).
Characterization:
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images
were collected on a high resolution-ﬁeld emission transmission electron
microscope (HR-FE-TEM, Tecnai 20, FEI). UV–vis absorption spectra were
measured by UV–vis–NIR spectrometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu). X-ray
diﬀraction pattern (XRD) patterns were measured by diﬀractometer (D8
Advance, Bruker) with Cu K𝛼radiation at a scan rate of 5° min−1. XPS spec-
tra were measured using a X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (K-Alpha,
Thermo Electron) under 1.0 × 10−8 Torr with a micro-focused mono-
chromated Al-K𝛼X-ray source. UV photoelectron spectroscopy spectra
were obtained using a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi,
Thermo Scientiﬁc) with He I radiation (21.22 eV). PL spectra and time-
resolve PL spectra were collected with a PL measurement system (PC1,
ISS). The J–V curves were measured using a solar simulator (Sun 3000 so-
lar simulator, Abet technologies) with a source meter with a xenon lamp
that was calibrated at 1-sun (AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2) before use with
a certiﬁed silicon reference cell (JIS). The J–V curves were measured by
masking the active area with metal masks having 0.096 and 0.16 cm2 aper-
ture. The J–V measurements were conducted at 50 mV s−1 with a step size
of 10 mV and dwell time of 200 ms, in ambient under controlled temper-
ature and humidity (25 °C / 20% RH). The J–V curves and SPOs were
measured after 15 s light-soaking at 1-sun. The SPOs of the devices were
measured with a source meter (Keithley 2460, Keithley) by monitoring the
photocurrent density output evolution under the biased voltage set near
the maximum power point. The EQE measurements were performed using
a xenon lamp (150 W xenon lamp, 13 014 ABET) with a monochromator
(MonoRA-500i, DONGWOO OPTRON Co., Ltd.) and potentiostat (IVium-
Stat, IVIUM). The KPFM was measured with an atomic force microscope
(Autoshot, Nanofocus).
Statistical Analysis:
Mathematical statistical methods were used to an-
alyze various photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs and PSMs with and
without the Cs4PbBr6 PNCs layer. Statistical analyses were performed with
Origin 9.5. The data obtained from J–V measurements were the original
data without normalization. Statistical analysis for PSCs was conducted
using 50 samples each for the control (without Cs4PbBr6 PNCs) and for
samples with Cs4PbBr6 PNCs at varying concentrations of 10, 20, and
30 wt.% (PBO). For PSM, statistical analysis was performed using 100
samples each for the control (without Cs4PbBr6 PNCs) and for samples
with 20 wt.% Cs4PbBr6 PNCs (PBO). All results were presented as mean
(averaged value) ± standard deviation (SD), which referred to the statisti-
cal parameters for PSCs and PSMs.

Conﬂict of Interest
The authors declare no conﬂict of interest.
Author Contributions
J.H.H. and J.K.P. contributed equally to this work. J.H.H., J.K.P., and S.H.I.
conceived this work. J.H.H. and J.K.P. synthesized nanocrystals and car-
ried out material characterizations. J.H.H. fabricated devices and J.H.H.
and H.J.L. performed ﬁlm and device characterizations. E.H.S. and K.H.H.
conducted DFT calculations. J.H.H., J.K.P., K.H.H., F.Z., and S.H.I. wrote
the manuscript. J.H.H., K.H.H., F.Z., and S.H.I. supervised the project. All
authors discussed the results and commented on the article.
Data Availability Statement
The data that support the ﬁndings of this study are available from the
corresponding author upon reasonable request.

file: Advanced Materials - 2025 - He - Stopping Phase Separation Enables Durable Wide‐Bandgap Photovoltaic Perovskites.pdf
label: related
text:
RESEARCH ARTICLE
www.advmat.de
Stopping Phase Separation Enables Durable Wide-Bandgap
Photovoltaic Perovskites
Xiao-Ying He, Bin Song, Kai-Li Wang,* Nan Li, Lei Huang, Rui-Hao Qin, Jing Chen,
Chun-Hao Chen, Yu Xia, Ilhan Yavuz, Yan-Hui Lou,* and Zhao-Kui Wang*
Light-induced halide segregation presents a fundamental barrier to the
longevity of wide-bandgap (WBG) mixed-halide perovskites. Herein, a
multifunctional ionic polymer, polyquaternium-37, is reported as an eﬀective
grain boundary passivator to inhibit the degradation pathway. This
dual-interaction mechanism concertedly arrests halide migration at grain
boundaries, thereby suppressing local electric-ﬁeld and ultimately curbing
light-induced phase separation. Consequently, the modiﬁed WBG perovskites
demonstrate robust photostability under light stress. The champion inverted
perovskite photovoltaic device delivers a power conversion eﬃciency (PCE) of
22.86% under AM 1.5G illumination and an outstanding indoor PCE of
43.19% under 1000 lux. Remarkably, the modiﬁed device exhibits a projected
T90 lifetime exceeding 10 000 h under continuous indoor light cycling. This
work pioneers a facile solution to halide segregation via grain boundary
engineering, paving the way for operationally stable WBG perovskite
photovoltaics.
1. Introduction
Metal-halide perovskite photovoltaics (PVs) have advanced
rapidly over the past decade, enabled by low-temperature process-
ability and highly tunable optoelectronic properties. With increas-
ing demand for indoor energy harvesting, particularly in applica-
tions such as the Internet of Things (IoT) and building-integrated
photovoltaics, wide-bandgap (WBG) perovskites have emerged as
promising candidates.[1–3] The absorption spectra of WBG per-
ovskites align well with common indoor light sources, such as
light-emitting diodes (LEDs). The strong potential of WBG per-
ovskites for indoor applications has been highlighted by recent
X.-Y. He, B. Song, K.-L. Wang, N. Li, L. Huang, R.-H. Qin, J. Chen,
C.-H. Chen, Y. Xia, Y.-H. Lou, Z.-K. Wang
State Key Laboratory of Bioinspired Interfacial Materials Science
Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Soochow University
Suzhou 215123, China
E-mail: klwang@suda.edu.cn;yhlou@suda.edu.cn;
zkwang@suda.edu.cn
I.Yavuz
Department of Physics
Marmara University
Ziverbey,Istanbul 34722,Turkiye
The ORCID identiﬁcation number(s) for the author(s) of this article
can be found under https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202518492
DOI: 10.1002/adma.202518492
rapid advancements, with a certiﬁed power
conversion
eﬃciency
of
44.72%
being
achieved under 1000 lux illumination.[4]
However, long-term operational stability
remains the primary obstacle to achieving
large-scale commercialization.[5,6]
Mixed-halide WBG perovskites exhibit
intrinsic instabilities that critically limit
long-term
operational
performance.
Il-
lumination triggers photoinduced halide
phase segregation in initially homogeneous
ﬁlms, resulting in the formation of distinct
iodide-rich (lower-bandgap) and bromide-
rich (higher-bandgap) domains.[7–9] Arising
from both photonic and thermal activa-
tion, this reversible process is governed by
multiple factors, including thermodynamic
miscibility gaps, polaron-induced localized
lattice strain, and the migration of ionic
defects.[8]
Halide
phase
segregation
is
detrimental to device performance because
the resulting iodide-rich domains act as energetic sinks that
funnel charge carriers. This process markedly increases ra-
diative recombination and ultimately causes a substantial
open-circuit voltage (VOC) deﬁcit.[10] Moreover, when electri-
cal bias is applied, photo-driven segregation often couples
with perovskite amorphization at interfaces, initiating a dis-
tinct degradation pathway that severely hampers charge trans-
port and collection.[11] Together, these degradation mecha-
nisms give rise to localized electric ﬁelds and nonradia-
tive recombination centers,[9] particularly at grain boundaries,
which ultimately diminish device eﬃciency and long-term
stability.[12]
Consequently, extensive research has been dedicated to mit-
igating these degradation pathways, primarily through compo-
sitional engineering, crystallization control, and the integration
of functional additives. Compositional strategies have demon-
strated that increasing lattice distortion by incorporating smaller
A-site cations such as rubidium (Rb+) can elevate the energy bar-
rier for ion migration, thereby suppressing phase segregation.[13]
Additive engineering, where functional molecules are utilized
to passivate defects and modulate ﬁlm properties, has shown
promise. Pseudo-halogen ions such as thiocyanate (SCN−) have
been employed to occupy iodine vacancies and sterically im-
pede halide migration within the bulk lattice.[14] Similarly, mul-
tifunctional organic additives like coumarin serve to passivate
various defect types and alleviate residual stress in the ﬁlm,
which is another known trigger for instability.[15] Nevertheless,

Figure 1. Local electric ﬁeld suppression mechanism with interaction between ATK and the perovskite. a) Schematic of ionic migration-induced local
electric ﬁeld at grain boundaries and suppression of LEF by ATK. b) XPS spectra of Pb 4f and I 3d for perovskite ﬁlm and ATK treated perovskite ﬁlm.
c) FTIR spectra of ATK and ATK/perovskite. d) LF-NMR proﬁles of ATK and ATK/perovskite.
although these strategies primarily mitigate ion migration and
passivate defects, the control of local electric ﬁelds at grain
boundaries (GBs), intrinsically linked to phase instability, re-
mains a critical and unresolved challenge for achieving long-term
stability.
Here, we introduce a dual-locking crosslinking strategy em-
ploying polyquaternium molecules preferentially distributed
at grain boundaries to suppress local electric ﬁelds and in-
hibit light-induced phase segregation in WBG perovskite pho-
tovoltaics (PVs). These quaternary ammonium groups anchor
halide ions, while carbonyl functionalities bridge Pb2+, form-
ing a robust molecular network that elevates the ion migra-
tion barrier and neutralizes localized charge accumulation. Such
molecular-level regulation mitigates light-induced halide redis-
tribution and associated nonradiative losses, leading to advance-
ments in both eﬃciency and operational durability. Our mod-
iﬁed devices demonstrate power conversion eﬃciency (PCE)
of 22.86% under standard solar illumination and 43.19% un-
der indoor light, while projected T90 lifetime exceeding 10
000 h under continuous indoor light cycling. These ﬁndings
highlight a promising pathway for realizing intrinsically sta-
ble WBG PVs tailored for high-performance indoor energy
harvesting.
2. Results and Discussion
In this work, we propose a local electric ﬁeld suppression (LEFS)
strategy to simultaneously mitigate phase segregation and nonra-
diative recombination in WBG perovskite. This is achieved by in-
corporating the multifunctional ionic polymer polyquaternium-
37 (ATK, Figure S1, Supporting Information) into the precursor,
where it preferentially assembles at grain boundaries to form a
dual-locking network. The quaternary ammonium groups of ATK
electrostatically interact with halide anions, while the carbonyl
groups chelate undercoordinated Pb2+ centers. As illustrated in
Figure S2 (Supporting Information), this cooperative binding
immobilizes mobile ionic species and homogenizes interfacial
electric ﬁelds, thereby suppressing the formation of iodide-rich
and bromide-rich domains that drive photoinduced phase seg-
regation and the associated VOC losses. In pristine perovskite
(Figure 1a), a density of halide vacancies (VX) at GBs act as
nonradiative recombination centers and serve as channels for
halide migration under illumination. The resulting ionic redis-
tribution generates inhomogeneous local electric ﬁelds (Figure
S3, Supporting Information), which accelerate structural disor-
der and trap formation. Upon ATK incorporation, these detri-
mental processes are eﬀectively mitigated by the chemically
