label: related
text:
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.advmat.de
anchored and physically crosslinked network, which homoge-
nizes the local electric ﬁeld at grain boundaries and suppresses
halide phase segregation.
To elucidate the chemical interactions underpinning this stabi-
lization eﬀect, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was con-
ducted (Figure 1b). In untreated perovskite ﬁlms, the Pb 4f bind-
ing energy was centered at 137.7 eV, whereas ATK-treated ﬁlms
exhibited a positive shift to 138.1 eV, indicating reduced electron
density around Pb2+. Concomitantly, the I 3d peak shifted from
619.0 to 618.7 eV, reﬂecting increased electron density at halide
sites.[16] These complementary shifts conﬁrm the formation of
coordination bonds between ATK carbonyl groups and Pb2+ cen-
ters, together with probably the electrostatic immobilization of
halide anions by quaternary ammonium groups. Such interac-
tions would eﬀectively passivate ionic defects and neutralize in-
terfacial charge accumulation.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy further cor-
roborated these interactions (Figure 1c). The characteristic car-
bonyl stretching vibration (─C═O) of ATK, initially located at
1728 cm−1, downshifted to 1711 cm−1 upon mixing with per-
ovskite, revealing coordination with positively charged Pb2+
sites.[17] Simultaneously, the quaternary ammonium group
(─NR4
+) exhibited an upshift from 1339 to 1345 cm−1. This shift
is indicative of strong electrostatic interactions between the posi-
tively charged quaternary ammonium groups and the negatively
charged halogen ions on the perovskite. These interactions dis-
rupt and weaken the internal chemical bonds.[18]
In addition to chemical passivation, ATK establishes a phys-
ical conﬁnement eﬀect attributed to its high molecular weight
and extended chain architecture. The polymer initially forms a
viscous network within the precursor solution (Figure S4, Sup-
porting Information). During crystallization, the polymer likely
preferentially localizes at the GB regions, where it imposes steric
hindrance that inhibits deleterious ionic migration. Low-ﬁeld nu-
clear magnetic resonance (LF-NMR) measurements provided ev-
idence of this (Figure 1d). The transverse relaxation time (T2), a
sensitive probe of molecular mobility, decreased from 42.83 μs in
pristine ATK to 38.34 μs in the ATK/perovskite composite. This
reduction signiﬁes constrained polymer chain dynamics,[19] con-
sistent with the hypothesized entanglement of ATK within the
perovskite. Such restriction imposes steric hindrance at the grain
boundaries, forming a dense and mechanically robust conﬁne-
ment network that complements chemical anchoring and con-
tributes to increasing the ion migration energy barrier.[20]
Collectively, these ﬁndings indicate that the interaction be-
tween the polyquaternium molecules and the perovskite lattice
can eﬀectively modulate perovskite properties, leading to sup-
pressed phase segregation. However, the direct role of local elec-
tric ﬁelds at grain boundaries in driving this instability remains
to be unequivocally veriﬁed. To consolidate these mechanistic in-
sights, we further sought to provide direct evidence for the pres-
ence and modulation of these local ﬁelds. While LEFS has been
validated as the primary mechanism, it is worth noting that ATK
may also exert beneﬁcial eﬀects through improved ﬁlm morphol-
ogy (Figure S8, Supporting Information) or other reasons. These
potential synergies cannot be excluded. Nevertheless, the con-
verging evidence from both experimental and theoretical anal-
yses conﬁrms that LEFS serves as the dominant pathway under-
lying the observed stability enhancement.
We ﬁrst conducted a series of theoretical calculations and com-
plementary experiments to visualize the local electric ﬁeld distri-
bution and elucidate how the ATK-induced electrostatic network
governs their suppression. The distribution of local electric ﬁelds
in mixed-halide perovskites is intrinsically governed by the halide
composition, with iodide-rich (I-rich) regions known to induce
more pronounced ﬁeld ﬂuctuations than bromide-rich (Br-rich)
domains. To elucidate this eﬀect, I-rich and Br-rich surface termi-
nations were constructed and structurally optimized (Figure S5a,
Supporting Information). As a simpliﬁed model for the micro-
scopic grain boundary interfaces, we utilized I-rich and Br-rich
surface terminations to analysis. Distinct termination geometries
were observed, each modulating the electrostatic landscape dif-
ferently. Electric ﬁeld distributions were obtained from the elec-
trostatic potential gradient along the z-axis. For the I-rich surface,
signiﬁcant ﬁeld oscillations were detected (Figure S5b,c, Support-
ing Information), whereas the Br-rich surface exhibited compar-
atively smoother variations (Figure S5d,e, Supporting Informa-
tion). Planar-averaged 1D ﬁeld proﬁles (Figure S5c,e, Supporting
Information) revealed that the enhanced localized ﬁelds at I-rich
terminations are likely to facilitate nonradiative recombination,
underscoring halide compositional uniformity as a critical fac-
tor for improving perovskite stability and optoelectronic perfor-
mance.
To suppress such detrimental ﬁelds, ATK molecules were in-
troduced at the perovskite interface. For computational simplic-
ity, FAPb(I0.75Br0.25)3 was selected as a model system to iso-
late the intrinsic halide-dependent eﬀects, instead of employing
the full Cs0.05(FA0.75MA0.25)0.95Pb(I0.75Br0.25)3 composition. The
optimized ATK/FAPb(I0.75Br0.25)3 conﬁguration (Figure 2a) re-
vealed the formation of a stable crosslinked layer at the inter-
face. Electrostatic potential (ESP) mapping (Figure 2b) demon-
strated a substantially homogenized potential distribution upon
ATK incorporation, while charge density diﬀerence (CDD) anal-
ysis (Figure S6b, Supporting Information) conﬁrmed electron
redistribution between ATK and the perovskite surface.[21] The
CDD proﬁles further indicated the formation of non-bonded Cl-
Pb and Cl-I interactions, with a strong binding energy of -1.58 eV
at the interface.
To quantitatively assess the modulation of local electric ﬁelds,
the ﬁeld proﬁle was calculated from the potential gradient along
the z-axis (Figure 2c). After ATK incorporation, ﬁeld ﬂuctuations
were markedly attenuated, and the average ﬁeld strength at the
ATK/perovskite interface was reduced to approximately 8 × 106 V
cm−1 (Figure 2d). The corresponding planar-averaged 1D ﬁeld
proﬁle corroborated this suppression. Electron localization func-
tion (ELF) analysis (Figure S7, Supporting Information) further
revealed enriched electron density localized around nitrogen- and
oxygen-containing moieties of ATK in proximity to undercoor-
dinated Pb and I sites, indicative of coordinative or electrostatic
interactions. These interactions facilitate interfacial charge redis-
tribution, eﬀectively screening and neutralizing localized electric
ﬁelds at grain boundaries.
To experimentally validate the theoretical prediction of ATK-
induced ﬁeld homogenization, kelvin probe force microscopy
(KPFM) was employed to obtain high-resolution surface potential
mappings to directly assess the impact of the LEFS strategy on the
local electronic landscape at GBs, complemented by atomic force
microscopy (AFM) measurements of the corresponding surface

Figure 2. Suppression of local electric ﬁelds and structural stabilization via LEFS. a) Optimized conﬁguration of ATK/perovskite interface. b) ESP mapping
of optimized ATK/perovskite interface. c) Calculated local electric ﬁeld proﬁle along the z-axis after ATK incorporation. d) Planar-averaged 1D electric
ﬁeld distribution at the ATK/perovskite interface. e) KPFM maps of pristine and ATK modiﬁed perovskite. Line-scan CPD across grain boundaries of
treated versus untreated perovskites. f) HR-TEM images of ATK treated and untreated perovskite.
topography (Figure S8, Supporting Information). KPFM quanti-
ﬁes the contact potential diﬀerence (CPD), providing a spatially
resolved measure of the local electrostatic potential and thus
enabling the visualization of grain-boundary-associated electric
ﬁelds.
As shown in Figure 2e, the control perovskite ﬁlm exhibited
pronounced surface potential depressions at the GB regions com-
pared to grain interiors, with a CPD diﬀerence of approximately
15.10 mV. This marked potential gradient indicates the presence
of a deleterious localized electric ﬁeld, likely arising from the ac-
cumulation of negatively charged mobile species (e.g., halide va-
cancies or free halide ions). Such localized ﬁelds facilitate halide
redistribution and act as electrostatic sinks, thereby enhancing
nonradiative recombination and contributing to the VOC deﬁcit
commonly observed in WBG PVs.
Upon incorporation of ATK through the LEFS strategy, the
surface potential distribution became markedly more homoge-
neous. The CPD contrast was substantially reduced to 2.34 mV,
yielding a nearly ﬂattened electrostatic proﬁle. This signiﬁcant
suppression of the potential gradient suggests a synergistic, dual-
action mechanism: the polymer’s quaternary ammonium moi-
eties eﬀectively neutralize localized negative charges at GBs,[22,23]
while its carbonyl groups simultaneously enhance defect pas-
sivation. Together, these interactions diminish the intensity of
interfacial electric ﬁelds and mitigate ﬁeld-driven ion migration.
Synergistically, these interactions diminish the intensity of in-

Figure 3. Suppression of halide migration and light-induced phase segregation in perovskite ﬁlms. a) Halogen ion content distribution statistics at the
grain boundaries of treated and untreated perovskite ﬁlms. EDX results of b) Br and c) I distribution at the grain boundaries of treated and untreated
perovskite ﬁlms. Temperature-dependent conductivity of d) pristine and e) ATK treated perovskite lateral devices. In situ PL spectra of the f) pristine and
g) ATK treated perovskite ﬁlm under illumination. In situ UV–vis spectra of the h) pristine and i) ATK treated perovskite ﬁlm after illumination.
terfacial electric ﬁelds and mitigate ﬁeld-driven ion migration.
This interfacial screening exerted only a minor eﬀect on the p-
i-n band alignment (conﬁrmed by UPS in Figure S9 and Table
S1, Supporting Information). However, its critical function was
to suppress the formation of light-driven ion-rich local electric
ﬁeld, which are known to promote halide redistribution.
To further substantiate this conclusion, High-resolution trans-
mission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was conducted to exam-
ine the structural evolution of the WBG perovskite. The lattice pa-
rameter for the iodine-rich phase is deﬁned as a = 6.67 Å, while
that for the bromine-rich phase is a = 6.45 Å.[24] The lattice pa-
rameter a is calculated using the formula d =
a
√
h2+k2+l2 . In un-
treated ﬁlms, Br-rich/I-rich heterojunctions led to detectable lat-
tice expansion and ﬂuctuations in interplanar spacing, indicative
of enhanced ion migration and phase segregation. In contrast,
the ATK-modiﬁed samples exhibited nearly invariant lattice con-
stants of 6.53 and 6.55 Å (Figure 2f). The sharp, well-deﬁned spots
in the fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis of the ATK-treated
sample, which stand in stark contrast to the blurred and diﬀuse
spots of the control sample, conﬁrm that the LEFS strategy stabi-
lizes the perovskite lattice and eﬀectively suppresses phase seg-
regation.
To further determine the ATK arrangement on the perovskite
grain boundaries, Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX)
tests were performed. In EDX mapping (Figure S10, Support-
ing Information), Pb element represented perovskite and Cl ele-
ment represented ATK. Cl element was observed throughout the
Pb-rich area, suggesting that ATK is broadly distributed within
the perovskite matrix in a network-like manner. This observa-
tion is consistent with theoretical calculations (Figure S6, Sup-
porting Information), which predict that ATK molecules pref-
erentially assemble at grain boundaries to stabilize the local
environment.[25,26]
Building on the KPFM results that conﬁrmed suppressed
local electric ﬁelds at grain boundaries, we next examined
how the LEFS strategy inﬂuences halide distribution, ion mi-
gration kinetics, and phase segregation in perovskite ﬁlms.
Line-scanning EDX (Figure 3a) and scanning electron mi-
croscopy (SEM) images (Figure S11) revealed that ATK-treated
ﬁlms maintained a uniform halide distribution with an I:Br
ratio of ≈3:1, consistent with the nominal stoichiometry of

label: related
text:
www.advancedsciencenews.com
www.advmat.de
Cs0.05(FA0.75MA0.25)0.95Pb(I0.75Br0.25)3. In contrast, control ﬁlms
exhibited signiﬁcant compositional ﬂuctuations at grain bound-
aries (Figure 3b,c), with Br enrichment (28.89%) and I depletion
(71.11%), indicative of light-induced phase segregation.[27]
To probe the kinetic origin of this stability, we performed
temperature-dependent conductivity measurements on lateral
Au/perovskite/Au devices to determine the activation energy (Ea)
for ion migration (Figure 3d,e). The activation energy was ex-
tracted by a linear ﬁtting of the data based on the Nernst-Einstein
equation, 𝜎T = 𝜎0exp(-Ea/kBT). In this equation, 𝜎is the ionic
conductivity, 𝜎0 is a pre-exponential factor, kB is the Boltzmann
constant, and T is the temperature.[5,28–31] The pristine sample
showed a low activation energy of 0.49 eV, whereas ATK incorpo-
ration raised it to 0.83 eV. This enhancement highlights the syn-
ergistic eﬀect of chemical anchoring and polymeric conﬁnement
in immobilizing halide ions and suppressing their drift under
bias or illumination. The two slope regions in the Arrhenius plots
correspond to ionic and carrier conduction regimes, respectively.
The strong increase in Ea for ionic conduction after ATK incor-
poration arises from electrostatic immobilization of halide ions
and Pb-carboxyl coordination, while the negligible change in car-
rier conduction Ea reﬂects that ATK does not alter the perovskite
band transport network.
To further examine whether the suppressed phase segregation
translates into improved spectral stability, we conducted in situ
photoluminescence (PL) and in situ UV–vis measurements by
subjecting the perovskite ﬁlms to continuous illumination, fol-
lowed by a dark recovery period. After 1 h of continuous AM
1.5G illumination, the PL emission peak of the control sam-
ple exhibited a 13.25 nm redshift, indicative of the formation
of iodide-rich, low-bandgap domains (Figure 3f).[32,33] In con-
trast, the treated ﬁlm exhibited only a minor shift of 4.84 nm
(Figure 3g), indicating that phase segregation was signiﬁcantly
suppressed. Following 1 h of illumination, dark-state UV–vis ab-
sorption spectroscopy revealed that the control ﬁlm underwent
notable spectral relaxation (Figure 3h), whereas the treated ﬁlm
remained highly stable, exhibiting minimal change from its light-
induced state (Figure 3i). This representative result demonstrates
that the LEFS strategy eﬀectively mitigates the light-induced
halide redistribution responsible for phase segregation, leaving
minimal residual driving force for relaxation.
To directly elucidate the mechanisms underlying light-induced
defect dynamics and probe the suppression of local electric ﬁelds,
KPFM was conducted on control and ATK-treated perovskite
ﬁlms under dark, 1000 lux (Figures S12 and S13, Supporting In-
formation), and AM 1.5G illumination. As shown in Figure 4a,
the control ﬁlm displayed noticeable surface potential ﬂuctua-
tions after 10 min of weak (1000 lux) illumination compared to
the dark state, indicating that even indoor light can induce lo-
cal ﬁelds that drive halide redistribution and phase separation.[7]
The CPD response exhibited a clear intensity dependence. Un-
der short-term weak light illumination, a slight increase was ob-
served because minor electron accumulation occurred at sur-
face or trap states.[34] In contrast, under AM 1.5G illumination,
the CPD markedly decreased. This is because substantial photo-
induced halide migration created a negatively charged surface
layer. This layer increased the work function and consequently
reduced the CPD.[35,36] At the same time, the ATK-treated ﬁlms
maintained a uniform surface potential under all illumination
conditions (Figure 4b), conﬁrming the robustness of the LEFS
strategy across a broad range of light intensities.
To further quantify the impact of weak light on phase stability,
we performed in situ PL measurements (Figure S14, Supporting
Information) and compared them with the results under stan-
dard illumination (AM 1.5G, Figure 3f,g). Figure 4c summarizes
the PL peak shifts, with the relative magnitude normalized to the
total shift of both ﬁlms under each condition. This metric reﬂects
the extent of halide segregation. Under standard illumination,
the control ﬁlm accounted for 73.2% of the total PL redshift, a
value that increased to 87.8% under weak light, indicating that
phase separation remains a signiﬁcant issue even at low inten-
sities. Conversely, the ATK-treated ﬁlm showed a substantially
smaller shift under standard light (26.8%), which further dimin-
ished to just 12.2% under weak light. This demonstrates that the
LEFS strategy eﬀectively mitigates light-induced phase segrega-
tion, with a more pronounced stabilizing eﬀect under low-light
conditions.
Complementary in situ UV–vis spectra (Figure 3h,i, and
Figure S15, Supporting Information) were used to monitor the
Urbach energy (Eu) relaxation dynamics after diﬀerent light il-
lumination, providing insight into defect state evolution.[37] As
shown in Figure 4d, the pristine ﬁlm accumulated more defects
under 1 h standard illumination than under weak light. Inter-
estingly, recovery after weak-light exposure was faster than after
standard illumination, consistent with the greater defect density
generated under higher photon ﬂux. In contrast, the ATK-treated
ﬁlms exhibited negligible changes in Eu during dark recovery,
regardless of the prior illumination history. This indicates that
the LEFS strategy not only suppresses photo-induced defect for-
mation but also enhances the structural reversibility of the per-
ovskite.
Finally, we investigated the inﬂuence of the LEFS strat-
egy on trap states using trap density of states (tDOS) and
deep-level capacitance proﬁling (DLCP) on complete devices
(ITO/4PADCB/Perovskite/PCBM/BCP/Ag). The tDOS results
(Figure 4e,f) reveal a marked reduction in deep-level traps in the
ATK-treated device across all light conditions compared to un-
treated perovskite device. The apparent ﬁlling of shallow traps
under illumination is likely due to compensation by photogen-
erated carriers, a transient eﬀect that does not resolve the un-
derlying instability.[38,39] The DLCP analysis (Figure 4g,h) further
conﬁrmed a substantial reduction in the bulk trap density for
ATK-treated device.[3,40] Notably, under weak light, the trap den-
sity in these device remained nearly identical to the dark state,
underscoring the robust defect tolerance conferred by the LEFS
approach. These combined results conﬁrm that the enhanced
stability stems from the ATK-based LEFS strategy synergistically
passivating pre-existing defects with its functional groups while
suppressing the local electric ﬁelds at grain boundaries that drive
ion migration and defect formation.
Inverted WBG PVs were fabricated using the LEFS strategy
to evaluate its impact on device performance under both stan-
dard illumination and indoor weak light conditions. Figure 5a
and Figure S16 (Supporting Information) presents the reverse
and forward scan champion PCEs of the ATK-treated and un-
treated devices under standard light, with the corresponding pho-
tovoltaic parameters summarized in Table S2 (Supporting Infor-
mation). While statistical data from 10 independent devices is

Figure 4. Light intensity dependent suppression of local electric ﬁeld, phase segregation, and trap states by LEFS strategy. Line-scan CPD across grain
boundaries of a) untreated versus b) treated perovskites after diﬀerent light illumination. c) Normalized PL peak shift under standard and weak illumi-
nation of treated and untreated perovskite ﬁlms. d) In situ Urbach energy after illumination of pristine and ATK treated perovskite ﬁlm. tDOS curves of
e) the pristine and f) target devices under diﬀerent light illumination. DLCP curves of g) the pristine and h) target devices under diﬀerent light illumina-

shown in Table S3 and Figure S18 (Supporting Information), the
untreated devices exhibit a PCE of 21.20% with a VOC of 1.23 V.
By contrast, the LEFS-treated devices achieve a signiﬁcantly im-
proved VOC of 1.26 V and an enhanced PCE of 22.86%, conﬁrm-
ing that the LEFS strategy eﬀectively suppresses nonradiative re-
combination losses and reduces interfacial trap-assisted recom-
bination. The short-circuit current density (JSC) of the treated de-
vices reached 22.46 mA cm−2, with an integrated current den-
sity of 21.51 mA cm−2 (Figure S17, Supporting Information).
We applied the same rigorous validation to our indoor measure-
ments. The experimentally measured JSC for the champion de-
vice under 1000 lux was 0.133 mA cm−2 (Table S3, Supporting
Information). To check for consistency, we calculated the theo-
retical JSC by integrating the 1 sun EQE (External Quantum Eﬃ-
ciency) spectrum (Figure S17, Supporting Information) with the
1000 lux LED spectrum (Figure S12, Supporting Information),
which yielded a value of 0.121 mA cm−2 (Figure S19, Support-
ing Information). This 9.9% mismatch is an expected physical
phenomenon, as the standard 1-sun EQE is known to underesti-
mate a device’s spectral response under low-light conditions due
to intensity-dependent recombination mechanisms. This analy-
sis, which is supported by consensus guidelines,[41–43] conﬁrms
that the experimentally measured JSC is the correct and physi-
cally relevant value for indoor PCE calculation. Anyway, under
indoor weak light, the LEFS strategy demonstrated the same level
of eﬀectiveness. Indoor photovoltaic performance was measured
using a customized Enlitech (IL-30 and HS-IL) indoor light sys-
tem integrated with a spectrometer, enclosed in a matte black
chamber to eliminate ambient light interference and ensure uni-
form illumination (Note S6, Supporting Information). As shown
in Figure 5b, the LEFS-treated indoor devices achieved a max-
imum PCE of 43.19%, with a VOC of 1.08 V and a ﬁll factor
(FF) of 85.29%, while the forward scan was shown in Figure S20
(Supporting Information) and the detailed parameters are sum-
marized in Table S4 (Supporting Information). The champion
device in present study achieved an indoor PCE of 43.19% un-
der 1000 lux white-LED illumination, which places it in the top
tier among the reported eﬃciencies for WBG perovskite indoor
photovoltaics. This high performance was highly reproducible,
with an average PCE of 42.07 ± 0.64% calculated from 10 devices

Figure 5. Performance and stability of inverted WBG PVs with and without LEFS treatment under standard and weak light conditions. Reverse-scan J–V
curves of pristine and ATK-treated devices under a) AM1.5G and b) indoor light illumination. c) Comparison of PCE and VOC gains induced by LEFS under
both illumination regimes. d) Comparison of representative perovskite and non-perovskite indoor photovoltaic eﬃciencies under 1000 lux white-LED
illumination. LHP, lead halide perovskite; THP, tin-halide perovskite; OPV, organic photovoltaic; DSSC, dye-sensitized solar cell; a-Si:H, hydrogenated
amorphous silicon. e) Summary of VOC × FF of the published perovskite indoor photovoltaics (PIPVs) in recent years. f) ISOS-L stability test of the
pristine and target inverted devices. g) ISOS-LC stability test of the pristine and target PIPVs.
(Table S5 and Figure S22, Supporting Information). The parame-
ter gains attributed to the LEFS strategy under both illumination
regimes were compared in Figure 5c. Noticeably, variations in
illumination spectra, calibration standards, and active-area def-
initions across diﬀerent reports can inﬂuence the derived pho-
tocurrent values. Here, all indoor measurements were performed
under a spectrally calibrated 1000 lux white-LED source with an
accurately deﬁned aperture mask to ensure reproducibility and
comparability.
To further elucidate the origins of the performance enhance-
ment, charge carrier dynamics were investigated, determining
the transient photovoltage (TPV) and transient photocurrent
(TPC) measurements, respectively. Upon ATK incorporation, the
photogenerated current decay time decreased markedly from
0.469 to 0.163 μs (Figure S23, Supporting Information), indi-
cating more eﬃcient carrier extraction owing to improved ﬁlm
quality and suppressed trap-assisted recombination.[44] In con-
trast, TPV measurements (Figure S24, Supporting Information)
revealed prolonged photovoltage decay in the treated devices, re-
ﬂecting suppressed grain-boundary electric ﬁelds and reduced
nonradiative recombination losses.[17] These results are consis-
tent with the signiﬁcant increase in VOC under both illumina-
tion conditions. Beyond VOC, a quantitative analysis of the FF
components reveals that the optimized device exhibits a smaller
gap between FFmax and FFSQ-limit compared to the pristine de-
vice (Figure S21 and Note S5, Supporting Information). This re-
duced gap further evidences suppressed nonradiative recombi-
nation and charge transport losses, which enhance carrier trans-
port and directly improve the FF of the ﬁnal device.[45] The re-
duced diﬀerence between FFmax and FFmeasured in the ATK-treated
device correlates well with the shorter TPC decay time, suggest-
ing more eﬃcient carrier extraction. The accelerated charge col-
lection suppresses interfacial charge buildup and voltage losses,
thereby reducing transport-related FF loss. Consistent with these
observations, the illumination ideal factor was calculated using
the reported formula,[46] nid =
q
KT × dVOC
d ln(L). After ATK modiﬁca-
tion, nid decreased from 1.51 to 1.20 (Figure S25, Supporting In-
formation), indicating a pronounced suppression of nonradiative
recombination and defect states.
To evaluate the color temperature dependence of our ATK-
treated wide-bandgap mixed-halide perovskite devices under

www.advmat.de
indoor conditions, we measured performance using white light
emitting diode sources at 3000, 4000 and 5500 K, all at 1000 lux.
Spectral irradiance proﬁles show a blue-shift with rising tem-
perature (Figure S28, Supporting Information). The experimen-
tally measured JSC for the champion device under diﬀerent spec-
tra were shown in Figure S29 (Supporting Information), indicat-
ing low spectral mismatch. J–V results reveal robust invariance
(Table S7, Supporting Information). The PCE drop from 3000 to
5500 K arises from the Pin increase exceeding JSC/VOC gains, sig-
nifying eﬃcient broadband harvesting. This stability stems from
polyquaternium-37 passivation suppressing halide segregation,
epically induced by blue-photon, at grain boundaries. Further-
more, to evaluate the device’s performance under more common
and weaker indoor light conditions, we characterized the opti-
mized ATK-treated device at 500 lux, 200 lux, and 100 lux. The
device demonstrated excellent performance retention, maintain-
ing high PCEs of 42.16% (500 lux), 40.91% (200 lux), and 38.45%
(100 lux). Notably, the VOC and FF remained high even at 100
lux (1.02 V and 80.78%, respectively), which conﬁrms that our
LEFS strategy is highly eﬀective at suppressing nonradiative re-
combination losses at low light levels. The full J–V curves and
detailed parameters for this intensity-dependent study were pro-
vided in Figure S26 and Table S6 (Supporting Information). To
contextualize our performance within the broader landscape of
indoor photovoltaics, we benchmarked our device against other
state-of-the-art technologies (Figure 5d). This comparison plots
the performance of our LEFS-treated device against other leading
perovskite, organic (OPV), dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), tin
halide perovskite (THP) and hydrogenated amorphous silicon (a-
Si:H) under 1000 lux illumination. This chart highlights that our
device, with a PCE of 43.19%, is highly competitive and at the
forefront of the ﬁeld. We also note that direct cross-technology
comparisons must be made with caution, as variations in re-
ported illumination spectra, color temperatures, and testing stan-
dards can signiﬁcantly inﬂuence outcomes. In contrast to previ-
ous results, our LEFS-treated device achieved a VOC × FF prod-
uct of 0.921 V. As shown in the comparison in Figure 5e, this
speciﬁc value was markedly high for devices with a comparable
Eg. Such an improvement demonstrated that the LEFS approach
eﬀectively suppresses non-radiative recombination, minimizes
VOC loss, and drove the device performance closer to the theo-
retical limit.
The environmental stability of the inverted WBG perovskite
photovoltaics was further evaluated under both standard and
weak light conditions. Under standard light (ISOS-L, 25 °C, N2),
the untreated devices retained only 50% of their initial PCE after
663 h, whereas the LEFS-treated devices maintained 90% after
1447 h (Figure 5f). This enhanced stability is attributed to the
suppression of light-induced ion migration and the reduction of
defect formation by the LEFS strategy. To better approximate real-
world operating conditions, we assessed device stability using the
ISOS-LC protocol, which alternates between light (2000 lux) and
dark phases (Figure 5g). After 12 cycles, LEFS-treated devices re-
tained over 98.3% of their initial indoor PCE, whereas untreated
devices degraded to 95.4%. Linear extrapolation of the decay pro-
ﬁle yielded a projected T90 lifetime of 10,247 h for ATK-treated de-
vices, compared with 3787 h for untreated counterparts. It should
be noted, however, that linear projection does not fully capture
the complexity of real degradation pathways. In practice, aging
often accelerates at later stages, meaning the actual operational
stability may be lower than predicted.[47] The superior durability
arises from the LEFS strategy, which stabilizes grain boundaries,
suppresses local electric ﬁeld induced trap formation, and facili-
tates defect relaxation during the dark phase, thereby improving
the reversibility of degradation and prolonging device lifetime.
Furthermore, this dual-locking mechanism targets ion migration
and local ﬁeld accumulation, which are universal instability fac-
tors in mixed-halide perovskites. This suggests that the LEFS de-
sign principle is not bandgap-speciﬁc and should be broadly ap-
plicable for stabilizing other I/Br-mixed perovskites where phase
segregation remains a critical challenge.
3. Conclusion
In summary, we have demonstrated a LEFS strategy that es-
tablishes a molecular level dual locking network at perovskite
grain boundaries to eﬀectively suppress local electric ﬁeld ac-
cumulation, thereby preventing light induced phase segrega-
tion in WBG perovskite photovoltaics. Through synergistic car-
boxyl Pb2+ bridging and quaternary ammonium halide coordi-
nation, this approach elevates ion migration barriers, neutral-
izes grain boundary charge accumulation that drives local electric
ﬁeld formation, and eﬀectively suppresses light induced phase
segregation along with nonradiative losses. Consequently, the de-
vices achieved a PCE of 22.86% under standard illumination and
43.19% under 1000 lux indoor light, with an estimated opera-
tional lifetime corresponding to over 10 000 h under day and
night cycling conditions, based on extrapolated aging tests. This
work unveils a generalizable strategy for stabilizing mixed halide
WBG perovskites and provides a robust design principle for sta-
ble WBG perovskites for indoor energy harvesting.
4. Experimental Section
Materials:
Lead (II) iodide (99.999%) and lead (II) bromide (99.99%)
were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. CsI (99.99%) was purchased from
Yingkou Libra New Energy Technology Co., Ltd. MACl (99.99%), FAI
(99.99%) and MABr (99.99%) were purchased from Greatcell Solar.
DMF (>99.8%), DMSO (>99.8%), chlorobenzene (CB, >99.8%), IPA and
ethanol (EtOH, >99.8%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. PCBM was
purchased from Funano Co., Ltd. 4PADCB (98%) and C60 (99%) were
purchased from Xi’an Yuri Solar Co., Ltd. Polyquaternium-37 was pur-
chased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Solar cell substrates
were pre-patterned ITO glass obtained from Yingkou Advanced Election
Technology Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were used as received without
puriﬁcation.
Solution
Preparation:
The
4PADCB
SAM
solution
was
pre-
pared
at
a
concentration
of
1
mmol
L−1
in
ethanol.
For
Cs0.05(FA0.75MA0.25)0.95Pb(I0.75Br0.25)3-based photovoltaics, a 1.4 mol
L−1 solution was prepared in mixed solvents of DMF and DMSO (v/v =
8:1) by dissolving FAI, MABr, PbI2, PbBr2, CsI. Polyquaternium-37 was
dissolved in a DMSO solution at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 than
stirred for 2 h. During perovskite solution preparation, 25 μL of this
solution was added to 1 mL of the perovskite solution. 20 mg PCBM
was dissolved in 1 mL CB. The solutions were stirred overnight at room
temperature (approx. 25 °C) on a magnetic stirrer before usage.
Device Fabrication:
The PVs were fabricated with a structure of
ITO/SAM/perovskite/PCBM/BCP/Ag. The ITO-coated glass substrates
underwent a cleaning protocol involving detergent and 10 min rinses in
deionized water, acetone, and ethanol. After being dried in an oven at 70 °C
for over 6 h, they were treated with ultraviolet ozone for 20 min. All solution

processing and spin-coating steps for the perovskite and transport layers
were conducted inside a nitrogen-ﬁlled glovebox with H2O and O2 levels
maintained below 1 ppm. The SAM layer was deposited by spin-coating
at 3000 rpm for 40 s and then annealed at 100 °C for 10 min. Perovskite
ﬁlms were fabricated through a two-step spin-coating process, conducted
at 1000 rpm for 10 s and 6000 rpm for 30 s, with the addition of CB anti-
solvent at 25 s, and subsequently annealed inside the nitrogen-ﬁlled glove-
box at 100 °C for 30 min. PCBM layer was deposited at 2000 rpm for 30 s.
The substrates were then introduced into a thermal evaporation chamber,
where BCP (8 nm) and Ag (80 nm) layers were deposited in sequence via
vacuum evaporation at a pressure of 3 × 10−4 Pa, with respective deposi-
tion rates of 0.2 and 2 Å s−1.
Characterization:
XPS and UPS spectra were acquired using a Kratos
AXIS Ultra-DLD ultrahigh vacuum surface analysis system. The FTIR spec-
tra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 Infrared Fourier transform micro-
scope by Thermo Fisher Scientiﬁc. Low-ﬁeld nuclear magnetic resonance
was collected on NMI20-030V-I produced by Niumag. The ﬁlms’ morphol-
ogy and electronic structure were characterized by AFM in Peak Force
KPFM (PF-KPFM) mode using a Bruker Dimensional Icon with MESP-
RC probes. The TEM images were collected by using an FEI Talos F200X
microscope. Morphological properties and EDX results of the perovskite
ﬁlms before and after modiﬁcation were characterized by scanning elec-
tron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta 250 FEG, USA). PL spectra were mea-
sured at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm using a Horiba Jobin-Yvon
Lab RAM HR800. Absorption spectra of perovskite ﬁlms were recorded
with a UV/vis/near-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 750). A Keith-
ley 2400 source meter was used to measure the J–V characteristics of
PVs under AM 1.5G, 1000 lux and 2000 lux. The current density–voltage
characteristics were measured using a Keithley 2400 source meter un-
der two distinct setups. Standard solar illumination measurements were
performed under an AM 1.5G solar simulator (Enli Technology Co., Ltd).
For this AM 1.5G testing, a ﬁxed area holder was used without an aper-
ture mask, deﬁning the device area as 0.0725 cm2. In contrast, all indoor
photovoltaic (IPV) characterizations were performed under a 1000 lux or
2000 lux white LED (3000K). For these crucial indoor measurements, a
metal aperture mask with a precisely deﬁned area of 0.0669 cm2 was
placed in direct contact with the device surface. This was done to accu-
rately deﬁne the illumination area and eliminate edge or stray light eﬀects,
in accordance with recommended IPV characterization guidelines.[41–43]
For EQE measurements, a system including a xenon lamp, monochro-
mator, chopper, lock-in ampliﬁer, and a calibrated silicon photodetector
was employed. No pre-conditioning, such as light-soaking or bias volt-
age, was applied to any devices. The IM6e Electrochemical Workstation
(ZAHNER, Germany) facilitated standard C–V, DLCP, and TAS measure-
ments, while C–f analysis was carried out with an Agilent 4294A precision
impedance analyzer. TPV and TPC spectra were measured by STPC and
TPVC-1.

Vapor phase deposition of perovskite
photovoltaics: short track to commercialization?†
Tobias Abzieher,
*a David T. Moore,
*a Marcel Roß,
b Steve Albrecht,
b
Jared Silvia,c Hairen Tan,
d Quentin Jeangros,e Christophe Ballif,ef
Maximilian T. Hoerantner,g Beom-Soo Kim,
h Henk J. Bolink,
i Paul Pistor,
jk
Jan Christoph Goldschmidt,
l Yu-Hsien Chiang,mn Samuel D. Stranks,
mn
Juliane Borchert,op Michael D. McGehee,
q Monica Morales-Masis,
r
Jay B. Patel,
s Annalisa Bruno
t and Ulrich W. Paetzold
*u
While perovskite-based photovoltaics (PV) is progressing toward commercialization, it remains an open
question which fabrication technology – solution-based, vapor-based, or combinations – will pave the
way to faster economic breakthrough. The vast majority of research studies make use of solution-
processed perovskite thin films, which benefit from a rapid optimization feedback and inexpensive to
procure tools in modern research laboratories, but vapor phase deposition processes dominate today’s
established thin-film manufacturing. As research and development of vapor phase processed perovskite
thin films are still strongly underrepresented in literature, their full potential is yet to be identified. In this
collaborative perspective of academic influenced by industrial views, we convey a balanced viewpoint
on the prospects of vapor-based processing of perovskite PV at an industrial scale. Our perspective
highlights
the
conceptual
advantages
of
vapor
phase
deposition,
discusses
the
most
crucial
process parameters in a technology assessment, contains an overview about relevant global industry
clusters, and provides an outlook on the commercialization perspectives of the perovskite technology
in general.

PERSPECTIVE

label: related
text:
Energy Environ. Sci.
This journal is © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2024
Broader context
Worldwide, perovskite PV is progressing rapidly toward commercialization, with the first solar module manufacturers expected to enter the market by 2024/
2025. Despite this rapid progress, it is yet unknown which fabrication technology – solution-based, vapour-based, or combinations – will eventually pave the
way toward the economic breakthrough at the terawatts scale. Although vapor phase deposition processes dominate today’s established thin-film
manufacturing, this processing route is heavily underrepresented in the majority of research studies on perovskite photovoltaics that employ solution-
processed perovskite thin films. For this reason, the authors of this perspective feel urged to reach out to the community with our perspective on vapour phase
deposition of perovskite PV.
Introduction
Photovoltaics (PV) is one of the cornerstones for a resilient and
sustainable energy supply in the 21st century and at the same
time an integral element of the solution for the most pressing
challenge of mankind: global climate change. At this juncture,
photovoltaic energy conversion vastly relies on mono-crystalline
silicon-based PV. While ongoing technological improvements of
silicon-based PV will steadily decrease their costs, significant leaps
ahead in cost reduction and performance are unlikely as the
technology approaches its practical as well as theoretical limit in
power conversion eﬃciency (PCE). Among the various candidates
for next-generation PV technologies, organic–inorganic hybrid
perovskite thin-film PV have positioned themselves a front runner
in less than 15 years of development, achieving PCEs of 26%
for single-junction solar cells and PCEs approaching 34% for
multi-junction solar cells, already outpacing well established and
longer researched PV technologies including organic photovol-
taics (OPV, PCE up to 19.2%), cadmium telluride (CdTe, PCE up to
22.3%), copper indium gallium diselenide (CIGS, PCE up to
23.6%), and even multi-crystalline silicon solar cells (PCE up to
23.3%).1 This tremendous improvement within an unprecedented
short development period is based on excellent optoelectronic
material properties – including high absorption coefficients,2 high
initial defect tolerance,3 high charge carrier mobility and long
lifetimes,4–6 as well as tunability of its bandgap.7 Moreover, on a
laboratory scale significant advances are demonstrated with
regard to the weak spots of perovskite-based PV, such as the yet
unproven durability of perovskite semiconductors thin films
under operational conditions as well as understanding how to
safely manufacture and deploy their potentially hazardous mate-
rials (mostly related to the use of lead and commonly employed
solvents).8–11 To date, perovskite-based PV seems at the edge of
commercialization, as evidenced by the considerable number of
start-up companies as well as the wide interest in the technology
from established PV manufacturers, equipment suppliers, and
materials companies (see Discussion below). Nevertheless, it
remains an open question if the technology will also be successful
in the highly competitive PV mass market and which fabrication
technology – solution-based, vapor-based, or combinations – will
pave the way to faster economic breakthrough for the various
perovskite-based PV technologies, e.g., perovskite-based single-
and multi-junction PV, or light-weight and flexible perovskite PV.
High-quality perovskite semiconductors are commonly pro-
cessed via two distinct routes: solution-based or vapor-based
approaches. Solution-based approaches apply inks that contain
the precursor materials and suitable solvent systems that
are
spin-coated,12–14
blade-coated,15–17
slot-die-coated,18–20
spray-coated,21–23 or inkjet-printed on a substrate.24–26 In con-
trast, vapor-based processing employs dry and solvent-free meth-
ods that transfer the precursor materials into their vapor state by
evaporating or subliming them under vacuum conditions, fol-
lowing a condensation of the vapor on a substrate and the
formation of a highly uniform perovskite thin film. Both deposi-
tion routes can also be combined in different ways, e.g., in two-
step fabrication processes.27–30 Further information about the
concept of vapor phase deposition of hybrid perovskite semi-
conductors, including a recapitulation of its known challenges
and current research status is found in Box 1.
Despite the dominance of vapor phase deposition processes
in the manufacturing of other commercial thin-film PV tech-
nologies and optoelectronic applications in general, research
on vapor phase processed hybrid perovskite semiconductors –
although an internationally prevalent research topic (see Fig. S1
in the ESI†) – remains heavily underrepresented compared to the
solution-based counterparts. In 2021, only one research article
was published on vapor-based deposition approaches for each
200 research articles on solution-based methods according to
the Perovskite Database Project.31 Solution-based approaches
still dominate the advances of the field, given their facile and
fast process optimization as well as straight forward integration
in research laboratory environments. Moreover, research on
solution-based approaches is often driven by the vision of
realizing low-cost and high-throughput roll-to-roll fabrication,
similar to newspaper printing or by spraying functional layers on
desired surfaces inspired by a simple painting of a wall. While
the success of solution-based approaches for perovskite-based
PV on the laboratory scale is undeniable, its implementation
into industrial processes is complex.32,33 In that regard, solution-
based approaches have encountered crucial limitations in other
areas of opto-electronic device manufacturing before – especially
during the initial commercialization stage where a fast and
investment-efficient market entry is indispensable. A prominent
example is the fabrication of organic light-emitting diodes
(OLEDs) for the fabrication of consumer electronics. While
research of OLEDs on the laboratory scale was extensively driven
by solution-based approaches, commercialization of the technol-
ogy and the formation of a mass market were enabled by vapor-
processed devices.34–36 Moreover, thin-film PV markets (includ-
ing
disappeared
technologies)
are
also
nearly
exclusively
Perspective

label: related
text:
This journal is © The Royal Society of Chemistry 2024
Energy Environ. Sci.
dominated by vapor-based fabrication techniques (e.g., for tech-
nologies like CdTe (First Solar, Inc., closed-space sublimation),
CIGS (Solar Frontier, sputtering and selenization), or OPV
(Heliatek GmbH, thermal evaporation)). Reasons for the challen-
ging implementation of solution-based approaches into indus-
trial large-scale fabrication are mostly related to the complex
interaction between precursor materials, solvents, and proces-
sing environment, resulting in a broad pool of process para-
meters that need to be optimized, controlled, and monitored
during fabrication.32 A high level of control of all these steps
becomes particularly important, and at the same time more
challenging, when transferring processes to the large areas
required for PV applications. In contrast, vapor-based approaches
rely on a low number of process parameters limited to the
deposition rates of the individual precursor materials, the exact
process conditions (e.g., process pressure and system design), and
the substrate conditions (e.g., temperature and surface condi-
tions), enabling a process that is comparably simple to monitor,
control, and reproduce. The latter is not only a major asset in
high-throughput and large-area processing, but also explains the
great success of these techniques in developing a mass market for
previous technologies. In that regard, promising results for larger-
area vapor-processed perovskite solar modules have been reported
recently.37–39
Following these preliminary remarks, current developments
in research and commercialization of perovskite-based PV are
characterized by a surprising discrepancy: the strong focus on
solution-based deposition techniques significantly departing
from historically successful approaches for the commercializa-
tion of opto-electronic technologies. In the present perspective,
we convey a balanced viewpoint on this apparent discrepancy
and provide an outlook on the prospects of vapor-based proces-
sing of hybrid perovskite semiconductors on an industrial
scale. First, we present a techno-economic considerations of
vapor- and solution-based deposition techniques that allow for
the determination of strategic opportunities and challenges of
vapor-based approaches. Second, we present an overview about
essential steps required for a fast transition of vapor-based
approaches from laboratory to industry, together with a discus-
sion of how academia can assist to facilitate this transition.
Third, by involving and collecting the input of a worldwide
network of researchers with proven track records in the field of
vapor phase deposition of hybrid perovskite semiconductors,
unique and self-contained viewpoints on the topic are made
accessible. In addition, converse with early-stage companies in
the field of perovskite-based PV as well as companies with long-
established experience in the commercialization of opto-
electronic technologies has fed into our assessment and is
reflected in our discussion, facilitating novel insights into
current developments. Finally, to support our viewpoint on
the urgent need for strengthening the development of vapor-
based deposition techniques and a closer cooperation between
research and industry, we provide unique key insights into
today’s perovskite industry viewpoints on the perspective of
vapor-processed perovskite-based PV. The latter will stimulate
further discussions about the prospects of this technology, the
perspective of vapor-based approaches, and help answer the
pressing question whether industry will first commercialize via
vapor or solution processing, in order to enable high-quality
opto-electronic devices and their successful commercialization
into the mass market.
Key challenges of perovskite PV
commercialization: a basic technology
assessment
When evaluating the chances for economic success of perovskite
PV and associated production technologies, various parameters
need to be considered that go beyond the performance of solar
cells on the laboratory scale. In fact, for a successful entry into
the PV mass market, the overall costs (e.g., levelized costs of
electricity, LCOE, in US$ per kWh) that encompass all steps
from materials supply to decommissioning or recycling of a solar
module, need to be considered (cradle-to-grave approach). While
unique selling points can justify slightly higher overall costs
compared to established technologies when employed in niche
markets, on the long run, perovskite-based PV is expected to
have its main application in the mass market.8,40 Thus,
perovskite-based PV need to outperform established PV technol-
ogies (i.e., silicon and CdTe) with regard to LCOE that are
expected to achieve values of US$0.05 per kWh for residential
PV and US$0.03 per kWh for large-scale utility PV by 2030
(according to the goals set by policy makers such as the SunShot
2030 initiative of the U.S. Department of Energy).41 When
comparing solution- and vapor-processed perovskite PV, the
overall LCOE are expected to be only slightly aﬀected by the
life-cycle chains before and after the actual fabrication process
given their similar raw materials and device architectures. In
order to gain further insights into the perspectives of vapor-
processed perovskite PV, a basic techno-economic analysis (TEA)
for vapor-processed perovskite solar modules is performed and
compared to a TEA for solution-processed perovskite solar
modules recently published by the U.S. National Renewable
Energy Laboratory (NREL).42 This will allow identifying the
principal diﬀerences between both approaches and highlight
necessary directions of research and development. Details,
assumptions, and limitations of the basic TEA methodology
are described in the ESI.†
When assessing cost diﬀerences between solution- and
vapor-deposited devices, both the general production costs of
the absorber layer as well as the capital expenditures (CAPEX) of
the employed deposition equipment must be considered. NREL
recently published full TEA data for several PV technologies,
including perovskite single-junction solar modules,42 which are
used here as the starting point for the following discussion and
serve to substantiate this perspective. This report breaks down
both the CAPEX and production costs for only the absorber
deposition (gate-to-gate approach) and the costs given below,
for both vapor and solution processing, are only inclusive of the
absorber layers. For solution-based absorbers processed in a
sheet-to-sheet production line with an annual production

capacity of 2 GW and a target power conversion eﬃciency for
the solar modules of 16%, production costs of US$0.023 per
Watt and a CAPEX of US$0.30–0.33 per Watt of annual capacity
have been estimated, which are used as the benchmark for vapor
processing. In order to prevent distortion due to speculation, the
performance metric is chosen in line with the current certified
PCEs for perovskite solar modules o19%. Generally, if higher
PCEs are enabled via non-substantial changes in the absorber
chemistry, solvent systems, deposition methods, or choice of
contact layers that do not change raw costs dramatically, then
the difference in costs that are discussed in the following will not
change either. In contrast, if the deposition process changes for
example as a result of more complex multi-layer deposition
approaches (e.g., by adding a two-dimensional capping layer)
adjustments will be required that are too speculative in their
exact impact to be implemented here. As in any TEA of non-
established technologies, there are several assumptions for
unknowns made in both the original report by NREL for solution
Box 1: Vapor phase deposition of halide perovskite semiconductors in a nutshell
Vapor phase deposition belongs to the most advanced and successful industrial fabrication techniques for opto-electronic thin films. It is not surprising that
these techniques also play a crucial role for the development of halide perovskite semiconductors and their application particularly in solar cells.125–127 In fact,
vapor phase deposition of halide perovskite thin films by multi- and single-source approaches were already employed by Era et al. and Mitzi et al., respectively,
in the late 1990s.128,129 Even though these studies did not identify the semiconducting characteristic of halide perovskite materials, they evaporated these layers
a decade before the demonstration of the first solar cell employing a halide perovskite material by Kojima et al. in 2006.130 Since these early attempts, the
concept of vapor phase deposition has not changed significantly and can be categorized into three approaches: (1) the deposition of pre-prepared powder or
films from a single source (single-source approach), (2) the simultaneous deposition of several individual precursor materials (co-deposition approach), and (3)
the sequential deposition of individual precursor materials (layer-by-layer approach). To date, co-deposition is by far the most researched approach as it enables
simple tuning of the thin-film composition with evaporation equipment commonly employed in research.
In vapor-based approaches, one or more precursor materials (e.g., one organic and one inorganic halide salt) are vaporized simultaneously or sequentially by
increasing their vapor pressure above the surrounding pressure inside a vacuum chamber kept under high-vacuum conditions (B105 mbar range). While the
energy for transferring the precursor materials into their vapor state is in most cases supplied by radiation energy from electrically powered heating wires
wrapped around an IR-transparent crucible or by direct contact of the precursor materials with a high-temperature surface in special evaporation boats, other
energy sources including high-energy electron beams, laser radiation, or electric arcs are similarly suitable. Following the transfer of the vaporized molecules
through the vacuum chamber, condensation of the molecules on a cold (compared to the thermal energy of the molecules) substrate surface takes place that is
accompanied by the nucleation and crystallization of a high-purity thin film. The composition of the resulting thin film, which is tuned by the ratio of organic
and inorganic salts reaching the substrate, can be readily monitored by quartz crystal monitors (QCMs) in real time.81,131 The crystallization kinetics of vapor-
processed perovskite films diﬀers fundamentally from solution-processed films and is subject to ongoing research and development.
Liu et al. demonstrated for the first time the suitability of the co-evaporation approach for the fabrication of perovskite solar cell absorbers in 2013 by simultaneously
depositing lead chloride (PbCl2) and methylammonium iodide (CH3NH3I) resulting in a first solar cell with a power conversion eﬃciency (PCE) of 15.4%.132 In
addition, it was shown that next to complex mesoporous device architectures mostly employed in solution-processed solar cells, planar layer stack sequences are
similarly suitable for the fabrication of eﬃcient perovskite-based solar cells, simplifying the fabrication process significantly. Having paved the way for the general
applicability of vapor-based deposition techniques, a fast rise in research activity on vapor-processed perovskite absorbers was observed that ultimately resulted in the
demonstration of solar cells with PCEs 424%,46,47 only slightly lower than for their solution-based counterparts with record PCEs of 26%.1 In addition, good long-
term stability for up to 3,600 h at 85 1C has been proven for unencapsulated perovskite solar cells employing co-deposited absorbers, which might be even higher than
for comparable solution-processed devices.133 This progress is particularly remarkable considering the overall rather small community (with a share of perovskite
related publications of less than 1%) researching that field compared to the broad community investigating solution-based approaches.31 Reasons for the fast
improvement in performance are related to (1) a better understanding of the chemical processes taking place during co-deposition of organic and inorganic
salts,59,60,65,81,94,97 (2) a better understanding of the growth mechanism during the co-deposition and the influence of substrate surface properties on it,52,54,97,98 (3) as
well as the use of alternative absorber composition next to methylammonium lead iodide (CH3NH3PbI3).68,75,134–136 A particularity of the simultaneous co-deposition
approaches, already observed in early studies,56–58 is the omnidirectional sublimation characteristic of the organic halide salts given their low enthalpy of evaporation
and vulnerability to decomposition.59,60,137 Depending on the design of the vacuum chamber, this particularity can complicate the monitoring of the individual
deposition rates and influences the composition of the deposited thin films. In response, some research groups employed cold surfaces inside the vacuum chamber
to adsorb the omnidirectional evaporation component39,54,134 as well as monitoring approaches via multiple QCMs.81,94 In addition, alternative deposition
approaches are researched.43,44,138,139 Important progress was made by gaining insights into the film formation mechanisms during co-deposition. For example, a
strong influence of the substrate material’s surface properties on the crystallographic and morphological properties of the resulting thin film was found,96–98 insights
that enabled a better screening of suitable materials the absorber is deposited onto.52
Of particular interest for an industrial application of vapor-processed perovskite solar cells are fully evaporated layer stack sequences, in which all functional
layers of the solar cell are prepared by vapor-based approaches. The concept was pioneered by Polander et al. in 2014, who achieved fully evaporated perovskite
solar cells with PCEs as high as 10.9%,140 and applied and refined in the meantime by others.52,141–145 Particularly, these concepts lower the complexity of the
fabrication process compared to mixed approaches also employing solution-based approaches and most importantly enable a simple upscaling of the
deposition process toward industrially relevant device areas. First promising prototype solar modules have been demonstrated,37,38,143,146 exhibiting low
upscaling losses comparable to other much longer researched thin-film technologies.39 Finally, applying vapor-processed perovskite solar cells in tandem
applications – for example in combination with textured silicon bottom solar cells – open promising applications that can significantly enhance the
performance of established PV technologies.147 As a result of the high conformity of vapor-deposited layers, high-quality film formation can be achieved even
on highly textured silicon bottom solar cells,85,148–150 which is usually tricky to access by solution-based approaches.151 First fully textured perovskite-silicon
tandem solar cells employing vapor-processed deposition techniques for the deposition of the top perovskite solar cell have been demonstrated recently
achieving PCEs exceeding 30%.28,86,135,152 Furthermore, solvent incompatibilities make vapor processing also a highly relevant deposition technique for
monolithic all-perovskite tandem solar cells.75,137 Given the outstanding scientific progress achieved in shortest time as well as their beneficial properties,
vapor-processed perovskite solar cells are indeed considered to play an important role in the industrialization of the perovskite PV technology.

processing and the present TEA for vapor processing. Specifi-
cally, the following discussion is based on very conservative
assumptions with regard to key factors such as production yield
(90% for both, solution and vapor processing), material usage
(same for both techniques), and post-treatment or annealing
(disregarded but critical for solution processing) that are more
likely to be favorable for solution processing in these calcula-
tions, thus, more likely to result in a rather conservative outcome
for the case of vapor processing. A detailed discussion about
these assumptions is found in the ESI.†
In terms of overall costs, the principal diﬀerences between
vapor and solution processing are the production costs, mostly
aﬀected by throughput (accurately expressed in industrial
deposition equipment by the dynamic deposition rate in
nm m min1, which is the thickness of the deposited film
multiplied by the substrate speed) and yield, and the CAPEX
associated with the vacuum equipment. Under the assumption
that all other layers in the device stack are the same for both
absorber deposition routes, these diﬀerences only impact the
deposition of the absorber layer. Both at the research and
industrial scales, high production yields are easier to achieve
via vapor-based deposition methods, resulting in a decisive cost
advantage of these methods over solution processing. On the
other hand, production throughput, which impacts both the
total production costs and the CAPEX, is typically lower for
vapor processing given the generally lower deposition rates of
common vapor processing methods. However, production
throughput in vapor processing can be readily manipulated
by placing multiple deposition sources in series, by applying
deposition sources with larger evaporation zones, or by increas-
ing the deposition rate for each individual source (as long as
the employed materials feature suﬃciently high temperature
resistances). In fact, there have been demonstrations, at the
laboratory scale, of deposition rates above 100 nm min1 by
sputtering, flash sublimation, and vapor transport techniques,
all methods that operate between 1  104 and 1  105
Torr.43–45
Therefore,
the
herein employed
CAPEX
model
refrains from considering ultra-high-vacuum equipment and
only considers the use of less expensive high-vacuum equip-
ment. Given these preliminary notes, deposition rates and the
number of sources are obviously the most important para-
meters that need to be optimized in order to bring production
costs and CAPEX to a competitive level. It is highlighted that
combinations of deposition rates and number of linear sources
are found here that enable competitive values for both produc-
tion costs and CAPEX (see Fig. 1), despite the often-claimed
unbeatable cost advantage of solution-based methods. The
annual production capacity is fixed here at 2 GW which can be

achieved by including multiple, parallel production lines in the
factory. The number of linear sources in series is per production
line. As the number of sources is reduced, the number of
complete lines in the factory increases to maintain the total
capacity which increases the total CAPEX. Deposition rates of
480 nm min1 or greater, with six or more deposition sources in
series enable production costs and CAPEX as low as US$0.011
per watt and US$0.03 per watt, respectively, equivalent to
solution deposition. In addition to the general process of vapor
deposition being cost competitive, it needs to be highlighted
again that additional cost-beneficial factors of vapor processing
have not been included in the above discussion (see discussion
in the ESI†). These include the lower production of hazardous
waste burden as well as safety management requirements due to
the omission of hazardous solvents, the availability of deposition
equipment and long-established process know-how, potential
benefits of a lack of annealing procedures in vapor-processed
films, as well as the expected higher yield and reproducibility of
vapor-based methods as demonstrated by established thin-film
technologies (e.g., the OLED technology).
Status and outlook for vapor phase
fabrication methods: how to become a
game changer for the
commercialization of perovskite
photovoltaics?
Having discussed that from an economic point of view vapor-
processed perovskite PV is generally able to compete with
solution-based
approaches,
a
comprehensive
discussion
covering key technological challenges of vapor-based techni-
ques along with potential solutions are presented in the follow-
ing. In agreement with the discussion before, it is highlighted
that – to date – the slow throughput fabrication along with high
CAPEX costs are the most critical aspects that hamper the
implementation of vapor phase fabrication of perovskite PV
on an industrial scale. Therefore, the first and foremost goal
needs to be the development of competitive vapor deposition
strategies capable of realizing (1) highly efficient absorber
materials at (2) industrially viable throughput rates (dynamic
deposition rates in industrial terminology) with (3) the lowest
possible complexity of the deposition process, enabling a
reliable process environment for a reproducible high-yield
mass production with minimum demands on monitoring and
process control. A major outcome of this discussion is the
suggestion for more research and development on innovative
deposition strategies besides the established co-evaporation
approaches, such as sequential as well as closed-space or
single-source deposition approaches.
Performance
When it comes to the performance of vapor-processed perovs-
kite absorbers, remarkable progress has been made recently by
the extensive academic network (see Fig. S1 in the ESI†) –
particularly for intensely investigated material compositions
like single-cation materials (e.g., MAPbI3) and double-cation
materials (e.g., CsFAPbI3). Not only are wide ranges of vapor-
processed perovskite compositions steadily approaching thin-
film qualities of their solution-based counterparts, but in some
cases such as methylammonium-free compositions they out-
perform
already
today
solution-processed
absorbers
(see
Fig. 2).46,47 For this reason, it must be highlighted that the

often-claimed preeminence in thin-film quality of solution-
based approaches is most likely not founded on fundamental
scientific limitations of vapor-based approaches, but rather just
a result of the personnel and monetary disparity in research
and development in the field, which resulted in a significant
backlog for vapor processing over the years. In fact, one could
likewise expect equal or even better thin-film qualities for
vapor-processed
perovskite
materials
given
their
simpler
nucleation and thin-film formation processes as well as the
absence of residual solvents and pinholes in the final thin film.
It should be noted here that the vapor-processed CsFAPbI3
absorber employs PbCl2 as a crystallization agent while the
solution-based reference does not. As PbCl2 in vapor processing
acts similarly on the crystallization dynamics as the for example
the exact choice of solvent systems or anti-solvent treatments in
solution processing, the comparison chosen here is believed to
be still reasonable.
When comparing champion solar cells prepared with
solution- or vapor-based perovskite absorbers (see Fig. 2 and
Fig. S2 as well as Table S2 in the ESI†), the latter in many cases
outperform their solution-based counterparts in terms of fill
factor, given the improved uniformity and conformality of
vapor-based deposition – characteristics that are of particular
importance for the prevention of losses via shunt and series
resistances at larger scales. However, lower open-circuit vol-
tages and lower short-circuit current densities is often observed
for vapor-based absorbers devices (see Tables S3 and S4 in the
ESI†). Deficits in open-circuit voltage in vapor-processed per-
ovskite solar cells are attributed to a generally lower opto-
electronic absorber quality accompanied by the presence of
non-passivated defect states inside the absorber as well as at its
interfaces with adjacent charge transport layers, resulting in
non-radiative recombination and in turn a limited maximum
extractable open-circuit voltage of the solar cell.48–50 In fact,
both photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and charge
carrier lifetimes of vapor-processed perovskite absorbers –
especially for co-evaporated hybrid perovskite materials – have
been shown to be up to one order of magnitude lower com-
pared to their solution-processed counterparts,51 indicating a
particular need for improvements of the bulk (and interface)
quality of vapor-processed absorbers. In that regard, small
grain sizes that are likely to come along with a significant
number of detrimental grain boundary defects have been
reported
as
an
ongoing
issue
for
the
dominating
co-
evaporation processes. While methods to manipulate the ver-
tical grain structure via adjustments of the employed substrate
material have been reported,52 efforts to enlarge the lateral
grain
structure
(e.g.,
via
a
fine-tuning
of
the
substrate

temperature) typically do not result in a similar improvement of
the opto-electronic quality of the absorber.53,54 Therefore, a
deeper understanding of how process parameters influence the
morphological properties of vapor-processed perovskite absor-
bers and what currently limits the formation of higher quality
absorbers is key for the further advancement of vapor-
processed perovskite materials – also evident from existing
learnings of already commercialized vapor-processed thin-
film technologies like organic PV and organic LEDs.55 In that
regard, deepening the understanding of crystallization kinetics
for vapor-processed perovskite films in general is pivotal and
needs to be further advanced by the research community –
similarly to the progress that has been made for solution-
processed films. Generally, it is believed that the complex
evaporation characteristics in these co-evaporation approaches
accompanied by a complex set of chemical interactions
between the inorganic and organic compounds as well as
potential decomposition products of the chemically fragile
organic halides (see Discussion below),52,56–60 strain related
effects,61,62 and the limited thermal stability of perovskite
materials that restricts the use of high-temperature, diffusion-
assisted deposition processes are the main reasons for the non-
ideal crystallization dynamics and in turn small grain sizes.63–65
Furthermore,
considering
that
significant
morphological
improvements in solution-processed approaches have been
achieved by adding additives that slow down reaction rates
(e.g., DMSO) or control crystal growth (e.g., alkylammonium
chloride), it can be inferred that the lack of similar studies on
these approaches for vapor-based processes is another reason
for
smaller
grain
sizes
compared
to
the
solution-based
counterparts.66,67 Recently, alternative deposition strategies, par-
ticularly sequential deposition methods, have been shown to
result in improved absorber morphologies with notable improve-
ments in open-circuit voltages as a result of the changing film
formation
mechanism.46,47,68
Dynamic
co-evaporation
approaches including seed layer depositions have also shown
promising
improvements in film morphology and device
performance.69,70 In addition, the formation and incorporation
of decomposition products into the absorber can be circum-
vented by employing less harsh deposition procedures for the
organic compound like hybrid methods (i.e., the vapor proces-
sing of the inorganic compounds and solution processing of the
organic compounds).27–29 Nevertheless, the suitability of these
two-step processes for the fabrication of high-quality perovskite
absorbers particularly in an industrial setting still requires
further confirmation and investigation.
While the concept of defect passivation of the perovskite
absorber and its interfaces has been proven to be crucial for the
realization of high-eﬃciency perovskite solar cells and is now a
well-established strategy in solution-based approaches,71–73 the
adoption of similar passivation approaches has been woefully
neglected for vapor-based perovskite solar cells. Only a handful
of studies investigated the influence of interface passivation for
vapor-processed perovskite solar cells so far, however, always
with a beneficial effect on the open-circuit voltage when inte-
grated into the layer stack sequence.46,74,75 Most importantly,
recent vapor-processed champion solar cells achieving power
conversion efficiencies 424% employed a highly specialized
passivation approach at the backside interface of the vapor-
processed absorber, enabling an open-circuit voltage deficit of
only 10%abs compared to its detailed balance limit, being on
par with the solution-based counterparts with similar absorber
composition.46,47 A stronger focus on the development and
implementation of advanced passivation strategies is therefore
a logical next step toward more competitive vapor processing of
perovskite solar cells. Not only is the development of multi-
interface passivation approaches that vastly reduces recombi-
nation at the interfaces between the absorber and charge
transport layers crucial, but also considerations about how
these approaches can be reasonably implemented in an indus-
trial setting. The development of vapor-based passivation stra-
tegies that can be integrated directly into the absorber
fabrication process are considered exceptionally suitable in
that regard and should be particularly targeted by research and
development.74 For the selection of potential passivation stra-
tegies, the extensive research that has already been performed
on passivation strategies in solution-based approaches should
be considered a helpful resource.
While losses in open-circuit voltage are mostly related to
electrical losses in the device that aﬀect vapor and solution-
based absorbers alike, the typical reduction in short-circuit
current density appears to be a particular issue for the vapor
phase deposition of perovskite thin films. When comparing
both fabrication approaches (see Fig. 2 and Fig. S2 in the ESI†),
short-circuit current density deficits of solar cells employing
vapor-processed perovskite absorbers are often up to 10%abs
larger than those of their solution-based counterparts (see
Tables S3 and S4 in the ESI†). First and foremost, the lack of
suitable passivation strategies for vapor-processed perovskite
absorbers does not only limit the open-circuit voltage but also
indirectly influences the charge carrier extraction given the
trade-oﬀbetween increasing the absorber thickness to max-
imize current generation and decreasing thickness to keep
recombination at an acceptable level. As a consequence, most
vapor-processed perovskite solar cells still employ absorber
thicknesses
not
exceeding
500
nm
while
high-eﬃcient
solution-based approaches with optimized passivation utilize
absorbers as thick as 900 nm, enabling a close to perfect
absorption of the incident sunlight. This emphasizes one more
time the preeminent role the development of passivation
strategies should play in the development of highly eﬃcient
vapor-processed perovskite solar cells. Moreover, lower short-
circuit current densities can also be attributed to very planar
vapor-processed perovskite thin films that provide neither light
scattering nor a texture-induced facilitated light incoupling.
Throughput
When developing highly eﬃcient vapor processing approaches,
deposition rates need to be considered as the most important
side constraint. Industrially viable deposition rates for the
perovskite
absorber
should
ideally
be
in
the
range
of
1000 nm min1 and above (synonymous with deposition times

label: related
text:
