Empirical 
dielectric 
function 
of amorphous 
materials 
for spectroscopic 
ellipsometry 
T. Yamaguchi, 
Y. Kaneko, A. H. Jayatissa, and M. Aoyama 
Research Institute of Electronics, Shizuoka University, Hamamatsu 432, Japan 
A. V. Zotov and V. G. Lifshits 
Institute for Automation and Control Processes, Far-Eastern Branch of the Academy of Russia, 690041 
Vladivostok, Radio Street S, Russia 
(Received 9 November 1994; accepted for publication 17 January 1995) 
An empirical dielectric function (EDF) is proposed for optical characterization of amorphous 
materials. The EDF consists of the sum of damped harmonic oscillator terms whose square root 
amplitudes are distributed according to a hyperbolic function of photon energy connected to an 
exponential function. The usefulness of EDF is demonstrated by fitting it to the table values of 
dielectric constants for a-Si and a-Si3N4 and by applying it to the spectra-ellipsometric analysis of 
a-Si films deposited by vacuum evaporation and rf glow discharge methods. 
Q 199.5 American 
Institute of Physics. 
I. INTRODUCTION 
Ellipsometry usually measure two variables p and A in 
the formula tan 1I’ exp(iA) = rp lr, . Here rp and r, are, re- 
spectively, p and s components of complex amplitude reflec- 
tion coefficients of a sample. They are functions of an angle 
of incidence, complex dielectric constants E = E, - iez of 
the sample, and other geometrical parameters, e.g., thickness 
d if the sample is a plane parallel film, or volume fraction 4 
if the sample is composed of several components.’ 
Principally 
single wavelength ellipsometric 
measure- 
ments at a single angle of incidence cannot determine more 
than two unknown parameters. To overcome this limitation 
spectroscopic ellipsometry (SE) has been developed. The ad- 
vantage of SE comes from the following three boundary con- 
ditions: 
(a) 
geometrical parameters do not change with X. 
Cb9 er and E? must change continuously with A. 
(c) 
it and E? must obey Kramers-Kronig 
(KK) relations 
which are obtained from the principle of causality.2 
One way to look for a solution which satisfy KK rela- 
tions is to use an appropriate function which satisfy the KK 
relations automatically. Adachi et al. proposed model dielec- 
tric functions for semiconductor single crystals.3 Dielectric 
functions of a single crystal are complicated because those 
spectra consist of several critical points and include more 
than ten parameters. Forouhi and Bloomer’ proposed empiri- 
cal optical constants for amorphous materials as a function of 
photon energy E as follows: 
k=a(E-d)‘/(E2-bE+c). 
The KK relations of Eq. (1) yield 
(1) 
n=f+(gE+h)l(E2-bE+cj, 
(2) 
where

and 
h=(a/j) 
X[0.5b(d2+c)-2cd] 
withj=0.5J(4c-b”). 
Independent 
unknown parameters in the above formula are a, b, c, d, 
and f. g, h, and j are functions of these parameters. For a 
certain range of the spectra these formula fit well to experi- 
mental curves. However for a wider spectral range the fitting 
is not satisfactory. 
In this paper we propose a new empirical dielectric func- 
tion (EDF) which can be used for a wide spectral range. We 
demonstrate the usefulness of the EDF. by fitting it to the 
table values of dielectric constants for a-Si and a-S&N, and 
applying it to the SE analysis of a-Si films deposited by 
vacuum evaporation and rf glow discharge methods. 
II. EMPIRICAL DIELECTRIC FUNCTION 
EDF is given by the following function of photon energy 
E, 
E(E)=E,+J 
E;/--:iErd& 
The second term is due to damped harmonic oscillators 
(DHO) with resonance energy Ea and damping factor F. The 
distribution function of the amplitude if (E,) 
is shown in 
Fig. 1. It consists of a hyperbola 1 and an exponential func- 
tion 4 smoothly connected to 1 at the point 3 on the slope of 
energy side exceed the peak energy 2. 
Equation (3) is uniquely determined by the following 
seven unknown parameters: the position of the origin 5of the 
hyperbola, X0 and Ya, the intercept of the asymptoticline 6, 
Xo, the difference between 2 and 5, dY the energy differ- 
ence between 2 and 3, UrX and two constants ~00 and I’. Here 
the mean square fittings is done using SIMPLEX method. 
Ill. FITTING TO THE TABLE VALUES 
Dotted lines in e1 and e2 in Fig. 2 show the table values,5 
and full lines are the best tit EDFs. The best-fit distribution 
functions ,/f are shown in the lowest figure. Despite our se- 
lected materials whose absorption peak energy is quite dif- 
ferent from each other, the EDF fits well to the table values 
in the wide spectral range. The best choice of the seven 
parameters is listed at left two columns in Table I.

B. Spectroscopic 
ellipsometer 
4 computer controlled four zone null spectroscopic el- 
Iipsometer is constructed and used for measurement. A 75 W 
Hg-Xe I amp (Hamamatsu Photonics K. K., Japan’) is em- 
ployed as a light source. A 10 cm double spectrometer (DH- 
10, Jobin Yvon, France) connected with a photomultiplier is 
used as a null detector. The wavelength is corrected during 
measurement using Hg spectral lines. 
A four reflection-type Fresnel rhomb made of fused 
quartz rods is used as an achromatic quarter wave plate, 
which has two pinholes with 1 mm diameter for inlet and 
1

It takes 20-40 s for four zone null measurement at a 
single wavelength depending on the intensity of the light 
beam. Usually, 60 points in the spectral range 230-800 nm 
are measured in 30-40 mm. Our measurements were per- 
formed in air. Angles of incidence were 65” for sample A and 
70” for sample B.

The left and right side figures in Fig. 4 show the best-fit 
EDF for samples A and B, respectively. The amplitude in- 
creases if the water adsorption layer is assumed in the 
analysis. 
V. DISCUSSION 
It is interesting that E of natural materials can be ap- 
proximated well by an artificial combination of a hyperbolic 
function and an exponential function. When measured P and 
A with small systematic errors can be explained well by a 
reasonable model of constant thickness, those values of E are 
regarded as correct. This is the measurement principle. 
Thicknesses of a-Si and water absorption layer are also quite 
reasonable. At present it is convenient to use EDF for the 
analysis of SE. 
For comparison, three EDF results for a-Si are drawn in 
Fig. 5. Full, dotted, and dashed lines denote, respectively, 
J. Appt. Phys., Vol. 77, No. 9, 1 May 1995 
Yamaguchi et a/. 
4675 
table values, vacuum evaporation, and rf glow discharge 
deposition. The peak energy and amplitude of e2 or Jf are 
different with each other. It seems difficult to establish E of 
a-Si uniquely because they depend on the deposition 
conditions. 
Films of a-Si are extensively studied by SE. Usually the 
effective medium approximation (EMA) is used in their 
analysis.8 EMA gives a kind of mean value of E weighted 
with volume fraction of components. Those components are 
a-Si, crystal Si, and voids. In fact, it is necessary to explain 
smaller amplitude of the observed E by introducing voids. 
The volume fraction of the voids as high as several 10% has 
been reported. Those values suggest that those films are quite 
polous in spite of tightly deposited films. 
According to private communications, EMA analysis 
seems to be not successful sometimes. 
This must be because

compiles Y? and A into a real function, and does not depend

VI. SUMMARY 
4 novel EDF for amorphous materials is proposed. The 
EDF is successfully applied to the table values of E of a-Si 
and a-Si3N4. The usefulness of EDF is also demonstrated by 
applying it to the SE analysis-of a-Si layers deposited by 
vacuum evaporation and rf glow discharge methods. 
It is suggested that the present EDF is useful to analyze 
the thermal annealing and recrystallization process of a-Si 
films. We are applying it successfully to those process of 
amorphous Si and Ge films, which will be published later. It 
is desirable to apply the EDF to various amorphous 
materials.

file: LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR

M. Dahlinger, S. J. Eisele, J. R. Köhler, and J. H. Werner 
Institut für Photovoltaik (ipv) 
Pfaffenwaldring 47, 70569 Stuttgart, Germany 
morris.dahlinger@ipv.uni-stuttgart.de

ABSTRACT: We present a novel laser doping process with sputtered boron precursor layers. The sub nanometer thin 
boron layers act as a finite dopant source during the laser doping process. By adjusting the boron precursor thickness 
and the laser pulse energy density, the presented process provides an elegant way to tailor the emitter achieving a 
very wide range of boron surface concentrations from 2×1019 cm-3 to 2×1020 cm-3 and emitter depths from 100 nm to 
500 nm. By varying the precursor layer thickness and pulse energy density, the emitter profiles, measured by 
secondary ion mass spectrometry are conveniently adjustable. The Combination of the presented laser doping process 
with the high spatial resolution of the laser beam provides an elegant way to create boron emitters, selective emitters 
and (local) back surface field applications. 
Keywords: Boron, Doping, Laser Processing, n-type

1 
INTRODUCTION

file: LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
The basic structure of industrial crystalline silicon 
solar cells has not changed for decades. The p-type doped 
bulk silicon has a furnace diffused phosphorous emitter, a 
silicon nitride anti-reflection coating, and a screen printed 
metallization grid at the front side. A full area screen 
printed aluminum metallization forms a back surface 
field and provides the back contact. 
Recently the selective emitter is introduced as an 
optimization step of the solar cell front side in a growing 
number of industrial fabrication lines [1,2]. A huge part 
of the limitation of the solar cell efficiency is caused by 
the bulk material quality and recombination at the 
aluminum back contact. These limitations could be 
overcome by using n-type doped bulk material or a 
passivated backside. 
The minority carrier diffusion length in n-type doped 
base silicon has been proven to be less sensitive to 
common transition metal impurities compared with p-
type doped silicon [3,4]. Furthermore n-type doped 
silicon does not degrade due to the absence of boron 
oxygen complexes [5]. Consequently n-type doped bulk 
silicon allows for higher solar cell efficiency. A 
passivated back side with locally diffused contacts 
reduces the rear surface recombination and hence 
increases the solar cell efficiency. Unfortunately, both 
changes in the solar cell structure require high quality p-
type diffusion.  
In this paper we present a laser doping process to 
obtain boron emitters or back surface fields. This laser 
doping process uses sputtered sub nanometer thick boron 
layers as a precursor and allows easy tailoring of the 
doping profile in depth and boron surface concentration. 
Combined with the inherent advantages of laser doping, 
such as high selectivity and spatial resolution, the 
proposed process provides an elegant way to produce 
advanced solar cell structures.

2 
EXPERIMANTAL

2.1 Boron deposition 
To evaluate the emitter sheet resistances ρs and the 
doping profiles, we use polished n-type doped CZ wafers 
with a specific resistivity ρ ≥ 10 Ωcm. Hydrofluoric acid 
removes the native oxide from the silicon surface before 
sputtering. A sputtering machine deposits the boron from 
an eight inch boron target using argon plasma. A constant 
sputtering pressure of pSp = 3.5×10-2 mbar and varied 
sputtering 
powers 
100 W ≤ PSp ≤ 200 W 
are 
used. 
Spectral ellipsometry measures the layer thickness dB. 
The deposition rates r at different sputtering powers PSp 
are determined by linearly fitting the measured layer 
thickness dB with regard to the different sputtering 
durations tSp.

file: LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
2.2 Laser doping

The ipv laser doping process is based on laser 
induced liquid phase diffusion of dopant atoms from a 
sub nanometer thin, predeposited dopant film into a 
shallow molten surface layer of crystalline silicon 
samples. A radiofrequency sputtering process deposits 
the precursor layer on to polished silicon substrates. After 
boron deposition, a pulsed neodymium doped yttrium 
aluminum garnet laser with a wavelength λ = 532 nm and 
a pulse duration Tp = 65 ns subsequently irradiates the 
sample surfaces under ambient air conditions. In the laser 
induced melt the predeposited precursor diffuses into the 
molten silicon. After the laser pulse, the melt 
recrystallizes epitaxially and thus creating high quality 
boron doped emitters as well as back surface fields.

The use of a line focused laser beam with a width of 
less than 10 µm FWHM in the short axis prevents defect 
formation during recrystallization [6]. 
To obtain 
homogeneous doped emitters the laser scans areas of 
1.3×1.3 cm² on the wafer surface with pulse repetition 
rate fRep = 20 kHz, scanning speed ν = 50 mm/s and 
defined pulse energy density H.

3 
RESULTS

3.1 Boron deposition rates 
Figure 1 shows the measured boron layer thicknesses 
dB deposited at varied sputtering parameters. The 
deposition rates r decrease from about r = 1 nm/min to 
r = 0.3 nm/min with decreasing sputtering power from 
PSp = 200W to PSp = 100W.  
By 
extrapolating 
the 
deposition 
curves 
the 
thicknesses dB of the boron precursor layers in the sub 
nanometer region are determined. With varied sputtering 
power PSp, the boron layer thicknesses deposited at a 
constant sputtering duration tSp = 1 min range from 
0.3 nm ≤ dB ≤ 1 nm. 
26th European Photovoltaic Solar Energy Conference and Exhibition
1152

file: LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
Figure 1: Boron layer thicknesses dB deposited at a 
constant sputtering pressure pSp = 3.5×10-2 mbar. The 
sputtering power PSp varies from PSp = 100 W to 
PSp = 200 W and sputtering durations up to tSp = 120 min. 
The boron layer thickness dB measured by spectral 
ellipsometry increases linearly with sputtering duration 
tSp. The deposition rate r decreases with decreasing 
sputtering power PSp.

3.2 Emitter sheet resistance 
The sheet resistance ρs of each sample is determined 
by four point probe measurement and the doping profiles 
are measured by secondary ion mass spectrometry 
(SIMS). SIMS allows us to evaluate the influence of the 
boron layer thickness dB and the influence of the pulse 
energy density H on the emitter sheet resistance ρs and 
doping profile separately.

Figure 2 shows the measured sheet resistances ρs of 
samples 
with 
boron 
precursor 
layer 
thicknesses 
dB = 1 nm, 0.6 nm, 0.5 nm, and 0.3 nm after irradiation 
with 
pulse 
energy 
densities 
between 
0.88 J/cm2 ≤ H ≤ 1.79 J/cm2. The sheet resistance ρs 
ranges from ρs ≈ 42 Ω/□ to ρs > 400 Ω/□. For all 
precursor layer thicknesses dB, the sheet resistance ρs 
decreases with increasing pulse energy density H. 
However, the minimal achievable sheet resistance ρs 
increases with decreasing precursor layer thickness dB. 
Hence the precursor layer acts as a finite dopant source.

3.3 Influence of laser pulse energy density

Figure 3 shows the measured emitter profiles 
corresponding to the emphasized points from the dashed 
curve in figure 2. The boron precursor layer thickness dB 
is dB ≈ 0.6 nm and the pulse energy densities H are 
H = 0.88 J/cm2, 1.1 J/cm2, 1.39 J/cm2, and 1.79 J/cm2. 
The emitter depth zE increases from zE ≈ 100 nm to 
zE ≈ 500 nm with increasing pulse energy density H. At 
the same time the maximum boron concentration cB,max as 
well as the sheet resistance ρs decrease.

Figure 2: Sheet resistances ρs measured by four point 
probe measurement of samples with different boron 
precursor layer thicknesses dB after irradiation with 
varying pulse energy densities. The precursor layer 
thickness dB limits the minimum achievable sheet 
resistance ρs. With increasing pulse energy density H the 
sheet resistance ρs decreases for all precursor layer 
thicknesses dB. The emitter profiles of the emphasized 
measurement points are shown in figure 3 and figure 4.

file: LASER DOPED BORON EMITTERS WITH SPUTTERED PRECURSOR.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
Figure 3: SIMS measures the boron emitter profiles, 
using a constant precursor layer thickness dB ≈ 0.6 nm. 
The emitter depth zE increases with pulse energy density 
H, while the maximum boron concentration cB,max as well 
as the sheet resistance ρs decrease.

We explain the laser doping process as follows: 
When the laser pulse energy density H exceeds the 
threshold pulse energy density Hth ≈ 0.8 Jcm-2, the silicon 
surface melts and boron diffuses from the precursor layer 
into the liquid silicon. A higher pulse energy density H 
leads to a deeper melt and the silicon remains longer in 
the liquid state. In a deeper melt the doping atoms diffuse 
deeper into the silicon and form a deeper emitter after 
solidification. The longer the molten region is in the 
liquid state the more dopant atoms diffuse into the melt, 
though limited by the finite dopant source, which results 
26th European Photovoltaic Solar Energy Conference and Exhibition
1153

label: unrelated
text:
in a lower dopant concentration cB,max at the surface. The 
emitter depth zE is mainly determined by the pulse energy 
density H.

3.4 Influence of precursor layer thickness 
Figure 4 shows SIMS profiles of the emphasized 
points in figure 2 at a constant pulse energy density of 
H = 1.79 Jcm-2 but different boron precursor layers 
thicknesses dB ≈ 1 nm, 0.6 nm, 0.5 nm, and 0.3 nm. The 
maximum boron concentration cB,max decreases from 
cB,max ≈ 1×1020 cm-3 to cB,max ≈ 2×1019 cm-3 with 
decreasing precursor layer thickness from dB ≈ 1 nm to 
dB ≈ 0.3 nm.

Figure 4: SIMS profiles of laser doped emitters, 
irradiated with a constant pulse energy density of 
H = 1.79 Jcm-2 but different boron precursor layers. The 
emitter depth zE is about zE ≈ 500 nm for all samples. 
Decreasing the precursor layer thickness dB from 
dB = 1 nm to dB = 0.3 nm, the maximum boron 
concentration cB,max decreases from cB,max ≈ 1×1020 cm-3 
to cB,max ≈ 2×1019 cm-3 while the sheet resistance ρs 
increases from ρs = 42 Ω/□ to ρs = 210 Ω/□.

A constant pulse energy density H induces a constant 
melt depth of the silicon and therefore a constant emitter 
depth of zE ≈ 500 nm. Thinner precursor layers shift the 
maximum 
incorporable 
dopant 
density 
to 
lower 
concentrations 
cB. 
The 
reduced 
boron 
doping 
concentration cB at constant emitter depth thus leads to a 
rise in the emitter sheet resistance from ρs = 42 Ω/□ to 
ρs = 210 Ω/□.

4 
CONCLUSION

We presented a novel laser doping process with 
sputtered boron precursor layers. With boron layer 
thicknesses in the sub nanometer region, followed by the 
ipv patented laser doping process creates boron doped 
emitters with sheet resistances between 40 Ω/□ and 
500 Ω/□. The emitters show a maximum boron surface 
concentration in a range from 2×1019 cm-3 to 2×1020 cm-3 
and emitter depths from 100 nm to 500 nm. Combined 
with the high spatial resolution of the laser beam, this 
process provides an elegant way to create boron emitters, 
selective emitters or local back surface field applications.

file: Modeling of Cu gettering in p- and n-type silicon in poly-silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
Appl. Phys. A 75, 525–534 (2002) / Digital Object Identiﬁer (DOI) 10.1007/s003390101021
Applied Physics A
Materials
Science & Processing
Modeling of Cu gettering in p- and n-type silicon and in poly-silicon
R. Hoelzl1,2,∗, K.-J. Range2, L. Fabry1
1 Wacker Siltronic AG, P.O. Box 1140, 84479 Burghausen, Germany
2 Institute of Inorganic Chemistry, University of Regensburg, 83053 Regensburg, Germany
Received: 3 September 2001/Accepted: 4 September 2001/Published online: 20 December 2001 – Springer-Verlag 2001
Abstract. Based on experimental ﬁndings we set up calcu-
lations of numerical modeling of gettering efﬁciencies for
Cu in various silicon wafers. Gettering efﬁciencies for Cu
were measured by applying a reproducible spin-on contam-
ination in the 1012 atoms/cm2 range, followed by a thermal
treatment to redistribute the metallic impurity. Subsequently,
the wafers were analyzed by a novel wet chemical layer-
by-layer etching technique in combination with inductively
coupled plasma mass spectrometry. We investigated p/p+
and n/n+ epitaxial wafers with different doping levels and
different substrate-doping species. We have also investigated
gettering efﬁciencies of phosphorus-diffused p- and n-type
wafers. Heavily boron doped silicon exhibited a gettering ef-
ﬁciency of ∼100%, while gettering by n+ silicon occurred
for doping levels > 3×1019 atoms/cm3 only. In another set
of experiments we investigated the dependence of the getter-
ing efﬁciency of p-type wafers with poly-silicon back sides
for different cooling rates and Cu spiking levels. A strong
dependence on both parameters was found. Cu gettering in
p/p+ epitaxial wafers was modeled by calculating the in-
creased solubility of Cu in p+ silicon compared to non-doped
silicon taking into account the Fermi-level effect, which sta-
bilizes donors in p+ silicon, and the pairing reaction between
Cu and B. Calculated gettering efﬁciencies were in very good
agreement with experimental results. Gettering in n+ silicon
was similarly modeled in terms of pairing reactions and the
Fermi-level effect. But, for n-type silicon, many experimen-
tal uncertainties existed; thus, we applied our expressions to
solubility data of Hall and Racette to obtain the unknown pa-
rameters. The empirical calculations were in good agreement
even with results on n/n+ wafers. For phosphorus-diffused
wafers we had to consider an excess vacancy concentration of
1.2–5.5 times the equilibrium concentration to explain the ex-
perimental ﬁndings by the model. Gettering by poly-silicon
back sides was simulated by solving the time-dependent dif-
fusion equation with boundary conditions that take into ac-
count different surface reaction rates of silicon point defects.
Using this advanced model, the experimentally measured get-
∗Corresponding author.
(Fax: +49/8677-83-7303, E-mail: robert.hoelzl@wacker.com)
tering efﬁciencies were reproduced within the uncertainty of
the measurement.
PACS: 81.65.Tx; 61.72.Yx; 82.20.Wt
Fe, Ni and Cu are detrimental metallic impurities for mi-
croelectronic devices, like microprocessors or DRAMs [1, 2].
They decrease the gate oxide integrity [3–5], decrease minor-
ity carrier lifetimes [6–10], increase leakage currents, lower
refresh cycle times and can even result in short-circuits. Get-
tering of metallic impurities in regions of a silicon wafer
that are not used by the microelectronic devices is a cheap
and very effective way to increase yields [2]. Feature sizes
reaching nowadays the sub-1/4-µm area (0.13, 0.11 and
even 0.09 µm are already in development) demand a con-
trolled and reproducible gettering. For this purpose, getter-
ing efﬁciencies of different gettering techniques must be
well known to enable defect engineering. In order to under-
stand the underlying physicochemical reactions, simulation
of gettering is the natural step further [11–26]. Gettering of
Cu is a ﬁeld of intense research in the past few years be-
cause of potential contamination issues in the new Cu in-
terconnect technology for producing high-performance GHz
microprocessors [27–35].
In the past, the evaluation of gettering mechanisms and
gettering efﬁciencies was hindered by the lack of any suit-
able analytical tools to measure Cu-concentration proﬁles
in silicon wafers at realistic contamination levels. For ex-
ample, deep-level transient spectroscopy without limitations
can only be applied to iron in Czochralski-grown (CZ)
silicon [36]. The idea to use a step etching technique in
combination with chemical analysis can be found already
in the literature over twenty years ago [37], but Shabani
et al. [38–41] have revitalized this method. We have de-
veloped a new chemical analytical tool, which allowed us
to measure concentration depth proﬁles of metallic impu-
rities in silicon wafers with a detection limit of (5−10)×
1012 Cu atoms/cm3. The depth-proﬁling technique was real-
ized by a wet chemical layer-by-layer etching technique we
called ultra-trace-proﬁling (UTP) [42,43]. Afterwards, these

file: Modeling of Cu gettering in p- and n-type silicon in poly-silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
526
‘stratigraphical’ etching solutions were measured by induc-
tively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [44,45],
one of the most sensitive element-speciﬁc ultra-trace analyt-
ical methods. The main differences between the method of
Shabani et al. [38–41] and our method is that we were able to
etch surface layers of a very low thickness variation (< 10%
of total etching removal) and the method is optimized for low
blank levels resulting in low detection limits.
We have investigated three different gettering reactions,
namely gettering of Cu by p+ silicon, by n+ silicon and by
poly-silicon. In Sect. 2 we describe the experimental meas-
urements and the investigated parameter dependences. In
Sect. 3 we model the gettering reactions and compare the cal-
culated results with the experimental results.
1 Experimental
1.1 Wafers
The speciﬁcations of the investigated silicon wafers are listed
in Table 1. 125-mm, 150-mm and 200-mm wafers have a sub-
strate thickness of 250 µm, 675 µm and 725 µm, respectively.
All wafers used were ⟨100⟩-oriented. Growth conditions are
either Czochralski (CZ) or ﬂoating zone (FZ). The numbers
in brackets in the specimens’ names denote the substrate re-
sistivity in mΩcm. Spreading resistance proﬁles were inde-
pendently measured on all wafers in order to characterize the
substrate resistivity and the resistivity and thickness of the
epitaxial layer. The bevel angle was 11.32◦and the probe load
was 10 g. We used an SSM 150 (Solid State Measurements,
Inc., Pittsburgh, PA, USA). All specimens were cleaned by
a modiﬁed RCA clean with modiﬁed cleaning solutions [46]
just before the spiking procedure.
1.2 Chemicals
For the spiking procedure we prepared solutions by diluting
standard atomic absorption stock solutions (Merck, Darm-

stadt, Germany) with 0.5% nitric acid. We applied the Cu ni-
trate spiking solutions in concentrations of 7.5 and 0.5 mg/L.
For analysis, we used Tamapure-100 chemicals of hydroﬂu-
oric acid (38%) and nitric acid (55%) (WAKO Chemicals,
Neuss, Germany) and nitric acid (65%) of sub-boiling quality.
The ultra-pure water contained less than 1 ng/L trace metals
as inspected routinely by ICP-MS (HP 4500, Agilent, Wald-
bronn, Germany).
1.3 Spiking procedure
For a description of the procedure the reader is referred to [3].
The aqueous solution, containing the metallic impurity, was
spun on the front side of the wafer. The most crucial parame-
ter of the spin-on spiking was the rotation speed of 3500 rpm.
0.5 and 7.5 mg/L Cu spiking solutions led to areal densities
of 0.5 and 7.5 atoms/cm2 Cu, respectively, as conﬁrmed by
total-reﬂection X-ray ﬂuorescence analysis.
1.4 Metal drive-in
To introduce the metal impurity from the surface into the bulk
of the wafer, the spiked wafers were annealed at 800 ◦C for
30 min (heavily doped n-type samples: 1000 ◦C/20 h) under
argon atmosphere in a horizontal furnace. The sequence and
the temperatures of the treatments were adjusted such that the
theoretical diffusion distance of the metals would exceed the
wafer thickness, and the metal impurity would therefore dis-
tribute homogeneously throughout the wafer bulk. The cool-
ing rate at the end of the process was adjusted by changing the
number of wafers in a boat (‘thermal mass’) and the pulling
velocity out of the furnace. Two different cooling rates with
50 ◦C/min and 200 ◦C/min were applied in this procedure.
1.5 UTP/ICP-MS measurements
For the optimized procedure the reader is referred to [42,
43]. Wafers were not additionally cleaned before the analy-
sis. The wafers were etched step-by-step from the front side

file: Modeling of Cu gettering in p- and n-type silicon in poly-silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
527
and the back side with a mixture of nitric and hydroﬂuo-
ric acids. The stratigraphical resolution of the etching layers
was 0.5–10 µm/step. The etching solutions were then evap-
orated to dryness and redissolved in 200 µl nitric acid (55%)
and 4.8 mL ultra-pure water. Finally, the samples were ana-
lyzed by ICP-MS under cold-plasma conditions. We used an
ICP-MS 4500 from Agilent (Waldbronn, Germany). Typical
uncertainties of the ICP-MS measurement are of the order of
20% as controlled by the difference between Cu measured at
mass/charge ratios 63 and 65. Detection limits were calcu-
lated from 3σ of four individual etching solutions not applied
to a wafer. Typically after 25-µm total etching removal from
the front and back sides of the wafer by the UTP analysis, we
analyzed the residual bulk contamination by dissolving a 2×
2-cm2 piece of the remaining wafer bulk. The further steps of
this bulk analysis were identical to the UTP method. The total
recovery of metals was > 75% in each case.
2 Experimental results and discussion
2.1 Gettering in p-type silicon
The Cu distribution after the metal drive-in of the ‘p/p +
(20)’ wafer is presented in Fig. 1. The solid line denotes
the Cu concentration; the dashed line (- - -) represents the
level of a hypothetical homogeneous Cu distribution in the
wafer. Crosses (×) denote the detection limit calculated from
3σ of four individual samples not applied to a wafer. The
p- epitaxial layer exhibited a strong Cu depletion, while al-
most 100% of the total spiked Cu amount was measured in
the p+ substrate wafer. The Cu was homogeneously dis-
tributed in the p+ substrate wafer. A very low Cu level of
1.75×1010 atoms/cm2 was detected on the front side of the
wafer. In Fig. 2 the results of the wafer ‘p +(17)/p−’ are
plotted. The p+ epitaxial layer exhibited a very strong get-
tering effect, while the p- substrate wafer was depleted of
Cu. Upon these ﬁndings we can conﬁrm a very high gettering
efﬁciency of heavily boron doped silicon for Cu. Gettering
efﬁciencies of p/p+ wafers for different substrate wafers
were determined and summarized in Fig. 3. All p/p+ wafers

different substrate wafer doping levels. For speciﬁcations see Table 1
exhibited a gettering efﬁciency of ∼100% up to substrate re-
sistivities of 0.060 Ωcm. Contamination levels on the front
side and the back side of the wafers were near the detection
limit of the UTP/ICP-MS method.
2.2 Gettering in n-type silicon
Figure 4 exhibits a typical concentration proﬁle of Cu after
the gettering test for n/n+ epitaxial wafers with substrate

> 3×1019 atoms/cm3 was gettering of Cu by heavily arsenic

tion of 3×1019 atoms/cm3 As led to a gettered Cu concen-
tration of (5.70 ± 0.07) × 1013atoms/cm3 Cu. In the other

The Cu proﬁle of the phosphorus-diffused n-type wafer is
presented in Fig. 6. At a doping level < 6×1019 atoms/cm3
P the Cu concentration dropped to very low levels. On the
other hand, a doping level of 6×1019 atoms/cm3 P was
able to capture 2–4×1015 atoms/cm3 Cu. The Cu concen-

7.5×1016 atoms/cm3. In this layer the P concentration was
1.3×1020 atoms/cm3 P. The results for n-type wafers are
summarized in Fig. 7.
Generally, gettering of Cu occurred for doping levels
> 3×1019 atoms/cm3. Below this doping level, gettering of
Cu by n-type silicon did not occur and Cu completely dif-
fused out onto the surfaces of the wafer.
2.3 Gettering in poly-silicon
A representative Cu proﬁle after the gettering test in p–/poly
wafers is presented in Fig. 8. On the front side of the wafers,
a Cu level was detected, which depended on cooling rate and
spiking level. A speciﬁc level of Cu is measured on the front
side of the wafer. A lower concentration was measured in the
bulk of the wafers, while Cu was detected with a high con-
centration level in the poly-silicon. Figure 9 presents these
ﬁndings in a quantitative manner. Obviously, fast cooling con-
ditions and low contamination levels led to low gettering
efﬁciencies of poly-silicon for Cu and vice versa.

3 Modeling of Cu gettering
3.1 Gettering in p-type silicon
Gettering of Cu by heavily boron doped silicon can be treated
by thermodynamic principles. Because of the very high dif-
fusivity of Cu in p-type silicon [47–49], which leads ap-
proximately to equilibrium conditions for Cu, it is not ne-
cessary to adopt a kinetic model. We have already presented
a detailed description of how to model Cu gettering in p-
type silicon [43]. In order to be able to compare gettering
in p-type silicon and in n-type silicon, we apply the model
from [43]: it is well known that Cu in p-type silicon is
a single positively charged interstitial Cu ion [50, 51]. We
linked the gettering ability of heavily boron doped silicon
to an increased solubility of Cu in heavily boron doped sil-
icon. The increased solubility was calculated taking into ac-
count the pairing reaction of Cu and boron (1) [52–55]. The
second effect controlling the solubility is called the Fermi-
level effect, which stabilizes donor species in p-type silicon
(2) [56].

The redistributionof Cu in the wafer was calculated by solving
the time-dependent diffusion equation applying the Crank–

full thickness of the wafer. Boundary conditions for the front
surface and the poly-silicon back side were chosen differ-

in a way that Cu relaxes to the equilibrium concentration
and the amount larger than the equilibrium Cu concentration
immediately precipitates (diffusion-controlled precipitation).
After [83–85] the Cu precipitation reaction can be formulated

The frontsides of the silicon wafers were polishedand covered

Similar to the poly-siliconbackside, Cu relaxes to this equilib-

tion) on the front side. We have determined the excess enthalpy
(0.7 eV) and entropy values (28.5 k) by ﬁtting to the experi-
mental results.
Figure 15 shows a typical development of the interstitial
Cu concentration at different time steps during cooling of the
wafer. In the beginning a homogeneous distribution of Cu in
the wafer is found. Reaching the equilibrium concentration
at a speciﬁc temperature, Cu starts to precipitate as Cu3Si at
the wafer back side. At even lower temperatures the satura-
tion at the front side is reached and precipitation starts at this
side, too. Now both sides compete in Cu precipitation. As re-
cently found by µ-PCD measurements [81], Cu precipitation
in silicon takes place at above 150 ◦C. Thus, if we take the
remaining Cu concentration at 145–155 ◦C for the bulk Cu
concentration we can compare the simulation results with the
experimentalresults fromSect. 2.3. This is depictedin Fig. 16.
Here the modeled Cu distributions are summarized for the ex-
perimental conditions applied in Sect. 2.3. We can see that the
Fig. 15. Simulated Cu distribution in a p–/poly wafer during cooling to room
temperature at the end of the metal-drive-in sequence for different time steps
and temperatures
Fig. 16. Comparison of the calculated gettering effects of poly-silicon in

main effects are well captured by the simulation: slow cool-
ing conditions result in high gettering efﬁciencies, fast cooling
conditions result in low gettering efﬁciencies. A high Cu con-
centration (> 1012 atoms/cm2) is to be gettered more easily
than a low Cuconcentration (< 1011 atoms/cm2), because pre-
cipitation starts at higher temperatures and thus for a higher
diffusivity of interstitial Cu. Using this model we were able to
reproduce all the experimental results of Sect. 2.3 within the
uncertainty of the measurement.
4 Conclusion
Segregation gettering in heavily boron doped silicon can
be quantitatively modeled by a thermodynamic equilibrium
model taking into account the pairingreaction of Cu and boron
and the Fermi-level effect. A similar approach is viable for
segregation gettering in n-type silicon. Solubility calculations
taking into account the three species Cu+
i , Cu3−
s
and (CuP)2−
s
result in quantitative agreements between measurement and
simulation. On the contrary, poly-silicon back-side getter-

file: Modeling of Cu gettering in p- and n-type silicon in poly-silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
533
ing is a diffusion-limited process. It has thus to be modeled
by a kinetic model. An appropriate approach for the equi-
librium concentration at the wafer surfaces led to a model
that allowed quantitative assessments of experimentally ob-
tained gettering efﬁciencies within the experimental range of
conﬁdence.

file: Mössbauer study of the proximity gettering of ionimplanted 57Co impurities by B-Si precipitates in Si.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
EUROPHYSICS LETTERS
15 December 1998
Europhys. Lett., 44 (6), pp. 707-713 (1998)
M¨ossbauer study of the proximity gettering of ion-implanted
57Co impurities by B-Si precipitates in Si
W. Deweerd1(∗), G. Koops1, H. Pattyn1, S. M. Myers2, T. L. Aselage2
T. J. Headley2 and G. A. Petersen2
1 Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Instituut voor Kern- en Stralingfysika
Celestijnenlaan 200D, B-3001 Leuven, Belgium
2 Sandia National Laboratories - Albuquerque, NM 87185-1056, USA
(received 22 April 1998; accepted in ﬁnal form 26 October 1998)
PACS. 33.45+x – M¨ossbauer spectra.
PACS. 61.72Yx – Interaction between diﬀerent crystal defects; gettering eﬀect.
Abstract. – We have studied the gettering of ion-implanted 57Co impurities to amorphous B-Si
precipitates in B-implanted c-Si using M¨ossbauer spectroscopy in conjunction with Secondary
Ion Mass Spectroscopy and Transmission Electron Microscopy. Under high-temperature an-
nealing, the Co atoms migrate from their as-implanted state to a gettered state at the B-Si
precipitates in the B-implanted layer, while no association between the Co impurities and
unprecipitated B is observed. The gettered Co atoms clearly occupy a non-metal-silicide site.
The M¨ossbauer spectrum displays a pronounced quadrupole splitting, closely conforming to
the spectrum observed for bulk crystalline B3Si implanted with Co. We therefore tentatively
argue that the gettered atoms reside at solution sites within the precipitates and not in their
periphery, in agreement with previous indications.
Introduction. –
The identiﬁcation and understanding of new and eﬀective mechanisms
of transition metal impurity gettering is necessary for future down-scaling of ICs because
of the stringent impurity tolerances [1, 2]. As atomic impurities, these elements are known
to cause deep levels, acting as recombination centers for the minority carriers, while as
metallic silicide precipitates, they cause short circuits.
It is expected that contamination
levels below 1010 atoms/cm2 have to be reached as the IC feature size approaches 0.1 µm.
Proximity gettering by segregation of the metal impurities to pre-existing low-energy sinks has
important advantages over conventional gettering by silicide formation at SiO2 precipitates or
relaxation-induced gettering, since the latter processes only decrease the impurity levels to the
solid solubility and involve macroscopic diﬀusion lengths [3]. Gettering processes of the ﬁrst
(∗) Research Assistant for the Belgian National Fund for Scientiﬁc Research (NFWO). E-mail:
wim.deweerd@fys.kuleuven.ac.be
c⃝EDP Sciences

file: Mössbauer study of the proximity gettering of ionimplanted 57Co impurities by B-Si precipitates in Si.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
708
EUROPHYSICS LETTERS
type remain active to arbitrarily low concentrations and can be implemented at small diﬀusion
distances, reducing the required thermal budget [4]. One eﬀective way of achieving this is by
gettering the impurities to the inner walls of nanocavities (see, e.g., refs. [4-6] and citations
therein). It has been shown that this process can prevent as well as reverse silicide formation.
In this paper we investigate another mechanism of proximity segregation gettering: trapping of
the impurities at B-Si precipitates in c-Si, which are formed by high-dose ion implantation of B
atoms to supersaturation and subsequent annealing. Previously, strong gettering of Fe, Co, Cu
and Au by this process has been reported [7] with binding free energies relative to interstitial
solution in the host matrix ranging from 1.2 eV for Cu to 2.2 eV for Au. For all metals,
this value is lower than the binding energy of the corresponding silicide phase, which can be
accounted for by an additional entropy-controlled driving force for the gettering process [4].
The observed characteristics of the gettering combined with high-resolution analytical electron
microscopy prompted the proposal that the metal atoms go into solution within the bulk of
the precipitated B-Si phase. Heretofore lacking, however, is any direct indication of the atomic
nature of the gettered state.
The purpose of the present work is to illuminate the microscopic state of the atoms gettered
to the B-Si precipitates by employing M¨ossbauer spectroscopy on 57Co probes. In this way Co
is not only the measuring nuclear probe but it also forms an intrinsic part of the investigated
system. We compare the M¨ossbauer spectra of trapped Co with the corresponding data for
1) Co in CoSi2, 2) implanted Co in Si with B-Si precipitates before and after annealing,
and 3) Co in bulk crystalline B3Si before and after annealing. From these comparisons it
is suggested that the gettered Co resides at a relatively well-deﬁned, non-cubic solution site
within the matrix of the amorphous B-Si precipitates and that the site is most deﬁnitely not
associated with local CoSi2 precipitation.
Experimental.
–
In performing the experiments, we employed three specimens.
One
sample (#1) was conﬁgured to exhibit, through Secondary Ion Mass Spectroscopy (SIMS)
proﬁling, the gettering of Co to the region containing the B-Si precipitates. The FZ Si (111)
sample was ion-implanted at 350 ◦C with 5 × 1016 59Co/cm2 with an energy of 100 keV,
followed by vacuum annealing at 600 ◦C for 1 h and 1000 ◦C for 2 h to develop large CoSi2
precipitates [8]. Subsequently, 1017 B/cm2 were implanted at 170 keV on the opposite side
of the wafer, followed by annealing at 1000 ◦C for 2 h.
This leads to (a) the formation
of a shallow layer of B-Si precipitates and (b) to cross-wafer gettering of the impurities to
these particles, as will be shown later.
The B-Si precipitates are situated at a depth of
0.5 µm beneath the polished surface and have an average diameter of ±10 nm.
Figure 1
shows a [110] phase-contrast High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy picture of a
single B-Si particle. The irregular granularity is characteristic of the absence of long-range
order, indicating that the particles are amorphous. Both experimental evidence and theoretical
considerations suggest, however, that these precipitates have local bonding and structure very
similar to crystalline B3Si [7].
A second Si specimen (#2) was prepared to allow M¨ossbauer spectroscopy of 57Co, gettered
to the B-Si precipitates. In this case the sample was implanted with 1017 B/cm2 at 100 keV,
followed by annealing at 1000 ◦C for 1 h, forming a shallower precipitate layer at a depth of
0.3-0.4 µm beneath the surface. The sample was then implanted with 5×1013 57Co/cm2 at an
energy of 75 keV, corresponding to an average range of approximately 0.06 µm, on the same
side of the wafer.
The third specimen (#3) consisted of bulk polycrystalline B3Si produced by reacting sto-
ichiometric amounts of B and Si at 1225 ◦C for 213 h [7]. The B3Si phase occurs only as a
transient phase in the reaction of Si with B: prolonged heating yields the stable mixture of Si
and B6Si [9]. Despite its metastability, the sample of B3Si used in this study contained only

Fig. 2. – SIMS proﬁle of sample #1 of the implanted B and gettered Co for a B concentration above
the solid solubility. The gettering eﬀect of the precipitates is clearly visible. No indication of an
association between non-precipitated B and Co is found.
minor amounts of Si and B6Si and has the expected crystalline structure and composition,
as observed by Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), X-ray diﬀraction and Rutherford
Backscattering Spectrometry [7]. This material was similarly implanted with 57Co as sam-
ple #2, and it was also examined by M¨ossbauer spectroscopy before and after annealing. The
objective was to provide comparison spectra to aid in interpreting the nature of the gettered
state in the sample with the B-Si particles.
The 57Co M¨ossbauer experiments were performed as source measurements in transmission
geometry, using a thin single-line SFC [Na4Fe(CN)6 · 10H2O] absorber with a thickness of
1 mg/cm2.
In addition to room temperature measurements, spectra were obtained as a
function of the measuring temperature between 10 K and 475 K to give access to the M¨ossbauer
temperature θM [6], which is indicative of the binding strength of the probe atom at a speciﬁc
atomic site:

Results and discussion. – Figure 2 shows the SIMS proﬁle of sample #1 after annealing,
clearly showing the gettering eﬀect of the B-Si precipitates. Part of the Co in the originally
present silicide is dissolved in favour of a strongly gettered site at the B-Si precipitates on the
opposite side of the wafer. Even the scaling between the depth proﬁle of the B-Si precipitates
and that of the gettered Co is visible from this graph. There is no indication of an association

label: unrelated
text:
710
EUROPHYSICS LETTERS
Fig. 3
Fig. 4
Fig. 3. – Fitted M¨ossbauer spectra of sample #2, as-implanted (a) and after annealing at 900 ◦C for
2 h (b). The normalized distribution on the quadrupole splitting of spectrum (b) is given in (c).
Fig. 4. – Fitted M¨ossbauer spectra of sample #3, as-implanted (a) and after annealing at 900 ◦C for
2 h (b). The normalized distribution on the quadrupole splitting of spectrum (a) is given in (c).
between the Co impurities and substitutional atomic B, dissolved in the Si lattice in the area
where the B has not precipitated.
While such a pairing reaction may in fact be present,
its inﬂuence is minimal and insigniﬁcant in the presence of the stronger gettering process at
the relatively elevated temperatures of this study. This observation does not give a picture
of the nature of this gettered site, however we can partly access information via M¨ossbauer
spectroscopy.
Figures 3(a) and 4(a) show the M¨ossbauer spectra of samples #2 and #3 after the 57Co-
implantation but before annealing.
For the Co implanted Si sample, we observe a well-
documented spectrum, consisting of a single line and a quadrupole doublet, which are due
to Co ending up in a substitutional lattice site for the ﬁrst spectral component and Co ending
up in its own collision cascade for the latter [5,6,10]. Within the experimental uncertainty, the
ﬁtted values correspond with those taken from the literature. The bulk crystalline B3Si sample

The normalized distribution (shown in ﬁg. 4(c)) consists of a superposition of Gaussian

as reﬂected by the large quadrupole splitting, whose exact nature remains as yet undetermined.
The average splitting corresponds to an electric-ﬁeld gradient of Vzz = 6.0(5) × 1017 V/cm2.

the c-Si sample and 4(b) for the B3Si sample. Figure 3(c) shows the quadrupole distribution
of the spectrum depicted in ﬁg. 3(b).
The apparent change stems from a radical change
in the local atomic environment of the 57Co probe. For the Co-implanted Si case, there is
a striking resemblance to the as-implanted B3Si sample and, equally important: it is certain
that no CoSi2 is formed, since the latter has a very distinct spectrum, consisting of a dominant
(≈80% of the total spectrum) single line at δ ≈0.03 mm/s and a smaller quadrupole doublet
at δ ≈−0.30 mm/s with a splitting of ∆≈0.35 mm/s [12]. We have again ﬁtted the spectrum
with a single distributed quadrupole component, the hyperﬁne interaction parameters being
given in table I. The M¨ossbauer temperature is found to be θM = 550(50) K, very similar to
the value for the as-implanted bulk sample.
Previous Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy measurements supported the SIMS obser-
vations by showing that the gettered atoms are associated with the B-Si particles and also
showed that the metal signal from the bulk of the particles was considerably stronger than
from their periphery, suggesting that the atoms go into solution in the B-Si phase [7]. We
argue that the above observations support these previous ﬁndings.
The implanted Co atoms within the B3Si sample, prior to annealing, can be said with
conﬁdence to reside within the bulk matrix, and to exist as isolated impurity atoms rather than

file: Mössbauer study of the proximity gettering of ionimplanted 57Co impurities by B-Si precipitates in Si.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
712
EUROPHYSICS LETTERS
small clusters or precipitates in view of the low dose and mobility. Further, the hﬁ-parameters
of the probes in the c-Si sample after thermal treatment and the as-implanted B3Si sample
are in excellent agreement, taking into account that this technique is very sensitive to local
distortions and the fact that the atomic environments in both cases are not expected to be
rigorously identical due to, e.g., residual damage and a diﬀerent long-range order [6]. The above
comparison of spectra, although inconclusive by themselves, therefore provides additional
support that the Co gettered to the precipitates indeed resides within the precipitate matrix,
rather than its periphery, in accord with previous suggestions. It indicates that the gettered
atoms are present as isolated atoms, dissolved within the B-Si particles and not as the CoSi2
phase.
Additionally, the similarity of the spectra is consistent with the proposal that the
amorphous B-Si precipitates have a local bonding and structure closely similar though not
identical to crystalline B3Si.
The bulk crystalline B3Si sample displays the characteristic CoSi2 shape after the annealing
treatment, as can be seen from ﬁg. 4(b). The single line is found at δ = −0.01(2) mm/s
(0.03(2) mm/s in the literature), while the quadrupole doublet lies at δ = −0.28(5) mm/s
(−0.30(3) mm/s in the literature), with a splitting of ∆= 0.42(5) mm/s (0.35(5) mm/s in
the literature) [11]. The M¨ossbauer temperature θM is found to be 400 (50) K, slightly lower
than the value of 450 K stated in literature for bulk CoSi2. The small diﬀerences between the
ﬁtted hyperﬁne parameters and M¨ossbauer temperature and those found in the literature can
be attributed to minor atomic variances. It is known that the ﬁt parameters are subjected
to large changes as the local composition or structure change only slightly [12]. It is however
certain that this spectrum stems from the CoSi2 phase and it stands in notable contrast to
the Co solutionization apparently occurring within the B-Si precipitates after annealing.
While the detailed reason for the diﬀerence between the two samples after annealing remains
to be deﬁnitively established, there are several points to bear in mind. Firstly it is observed
that no CoSi2 precipitates are formed when B-Si precipitates are present. Instead, the im-
planted atoms prefer a gettered site, clearly diﬀerent from CoSi2 as observed by M¨ossbauer
spectroscopy, at the B-Si precipitates as observed by SIMS, even though the binding free
energy can be less than for the precipitated silicide phase [7] due to an additional entropy-driven
occupation of pre-existing low-energy sinks as explained elsewhere [4]. Moreover, the implanted
dose is so low that after this gettering process, hardly a few atoms (∼10–20) can be present
per precipitate under equilibrium conditions, making local CoSi2 formation unfeasible. For the
B3Si sample, however, a whole diﬀerent situation arises: all the Co atoms are free to diﬀuse
through the bulk of the sample as they see ﬁt and precipitate at any possible nucleation center
during subsequent cooling. The M¨ossbauer spectrum proves that under these conditions, the
Co atoms segregate and CoSi2 forms. Secondly, it may also be that the solution sites are
speciﬁcally associated with point defects present in the amorphous B-Si particles but not the
crystalline B3Si phase, facilitating the trapping of a single dissolved atom.
The requisite
imperfections can then be initially introduced into the crystalline B3Si sample by the damage
cascade associated with the Co implantation, and lost by lattice recovery during subsequent
annealing in favour of secondary defects, leading to CoSi2 precipitation.
Conclusion. – The present investigation using M¨ossbauer spectroscopy probed for the ﬁrst
time the local state of metal atoms which are strongly gettered to B-Si precipitates in c-Si.
Our ﬁndings support a previous model in which gettered Co atoms, and by extension other
transition metals as well, go into atomic solution states within the matrix of the amorphous
B-Si precipitates. Other types of experiments and simulations, however, might prove valuable
to further conﬁrm these ﬁndings. No sign of CoSi2 formation or association between Co and
atomic B is found after the employed annealing.

Strong segregation gettering of transition metals 
by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon 
S.M. Myers, G.A. Petersen, D.M. Follstaedt, T.J. Headley, J.R. Michael, and C.H. Seager 
Sandia National Laboratories, P.O. Box 5800, Albuquerque, New Mexico 871 85-1056 USA 
. 
Abstract 
I 
We have mechanistically and quantitatively characterized the binding of transition-metal 
impurities in Si to cavities formed by He implantation and to B-Si precipitates resulting from B 
implantation. Both sinks are inferred to act by the segregation of metal atoms to pre-existing 
low-energy sites, namely surface chemisorption sites in the case of cavities and bulk solution 
sites in the case of the B-Si phase. These gettering processes exhibit large binding energies, and 
they are predicted to remain active for arbitrarily small initial impurity concentrations as a result 
of the segregation mechanisms. Both appear promising for gettering in Si devices. 
1. Introduction 
The removal of detrimental transition-metal impurities from the device region of Si 
wafers by gettering at remotely located sinks is a widely used and successful supplement to 
stringent clean-room procedures [ 1,2]. Currently, most gettering is based upon the introduction 
into the Si lattice of imperfections, such as extended defects and Si02 precipitates, which serve 
as nucleation centers for precipitation of the silicide phases of the metal impurities. While 
effective, this approach has the inherent limitation that the solution concentration of a metal 
impurity can be reduced to the thermodynamic solid solubility and no further. Since the 
solubilities exceed acceptable impurity levels at the elevated temperatures of device processing, 
provisions must be made for subsequent gettering at lower temperatures. Moreover, the impact 
of this constraint will increase with the continuing reduction of metal-impurity tolerances [3]. A 
further important point is that gettering by silicide precipitation cannot directly dissolve the 
silicide particles that form at such critical locations as the base of gate oxides because the metal 
chemical potential is the same at the particles and sinks. 
The above considerations point to a need for gettering methods that remain effective at

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
-2- 
concentrations below the metal solubility and provide useful gettering at the high temperatures of 
device processing. A process of this type that has received wide attention is phosphorus- 
n 
diffusion gettering, where P is diffused into the back of the wafer fiom a layer of phosphosilicate 
glass [2]. The underlying mechanisms may include P-related enhancement of metal solubilities 
and localized kinetic enrichment of the metal concentration due to interstitial-defect flows 
associated with P diffusion. While P-difision gettering is known to be effective, available 
information does not allow quantitative prediction of the reductions in impurity concentrations. 
Gettering fiom the front, device side of the wafer is not readily feasible with this process. 
In this paper we examine two impurity sinks formed by ion implantation that combine 
large binding energies with the absence of a cut-off concentration, thereby satisfying the needs 
discussed above. The first is a microscopic cavity, formed by He implantation and annealing, 
whose reactive internal surfaces trap metal atoms by chemisorption. Such cavities are also 
nucleation sites for precipitation of bulk metal-silicide phases, so that this gettering mechanism is 
active as well when the solution is supersaturated. We have quantified the strength of cavity- 
wall chemisorption for Cu, Au, Coy and Fe. The second type of sink is a B-rich B-Si precipitate, 
formed by implanting B to a concentration above its solubility and annealing. Experiments on 
Fe and Cu impurities indicate that the metal atoms go into solution in the B3Si phase with an 
energy substantially lower than in the Si phase. 
The cavity and B-Si sinks share a property that should result in trapping no matter how 
small the pre-gettering concentration, unlike gettering based on metal-silicide precipitation. This 
property is the segregation of metal atoms to pre-existing low-energy sites, which at small 
impurity concentrations leads to a configurational-entropy term in the metal chemical potential of 
kTln(B), 0 being the fractional site occupation. Since the chemical potential for metal atoms in 
solution in the Si lattice varies with the solution concentration, C,, as kTln(Cs), equilibrium 
between sinks and solution leads to a constant of proportionality between 6 and C,. 
2. Gettering by cavities 
Cavities have been shown by several groups to getter transition metals effectively, with

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
-3- 
both chemisorption reaction and the precipitation of bulk silicide phases taking place at the 
internal surfaces [see, e.g., Refs. 4-10 and citations therein]. To predict gettering behavior 
quantitatively, knowledge of the underlying energetics is needed. For silicide precipitation it 
suffices to determine the temperature-dependent solubilities of the metals, and extensive data on 
this property are available [1,2,11]. In contrast, the literature provides little quantitative 
information on the strengths of the chemisorption reactions. For this reason, chemisorption 
binding on the cavity walls was the principal focus of the present study. 
Before presenting experimental results, we outline the formalism used to describe the 
binding of metal atoms by chemisorption and silicide precipitation. The strengths of these 
reactions are expressed in terms of a Gibbs free energy change associated with the transfer of one 
metal atom from the bound state to interstitial solution,

Figure 1 shows a cross-section transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of 
cavities formed by He implantation and annealing. When He is implanted at room temperature 
to concentrations above a threshold of about 2 at.%, a dense distribution of nanometer-size 
bubbles results. Subsequent heating at temperatures 2973 K causes 1) diffusion of He from the 
cavities, 2) coalescence, enlargement, and faceting of the resulting voids, and 3) extensive 
annealing of implantation damage [12]. The internal surfaces of such cavities are initially 
pristine and are highly reactive as a result of dangling bonds. The cavity wall area is typically 
several times the specimen area. 
Concentration profiles exhibiting the gettering of Co and Au by cavities are shown in 
Fig. 2. In both cases, the source of metal atoms was an excess of the equilibrium metal-silicide 
phase, formed at a different depth by implantation and annealing prior to the introduction of the 
cavities. This procedure is expected to stabilize the solution concentration at the solubility 
during subsequent gettering anneals. Confirmation was obtained for Fe by deep-level transient 
spectroscopy (DLTS): after annealing at 1223 K, DLTS indicated a solution concentration about 
20% below the published value [ 1 11, within combined uncertainties. By thus preventing 
supersaturation, gettering due to silicide formation in the cavities was avoided, leaving only the 
wall-chemisorption reaction of interest. 
The profiles in Fig. 2 illustrate the two types of cavity-gettering behavior that we 
observed in studies of Cu, Au, Co, and Fe impurities in equilibrium with the metal-silicide phase. 
In the case of Cu and Au, the acquisition of metal atoms continued until the areal density

densities of gettered Co and Fe corresponded to wall coverages of <O.O 1 ML. These 
observations can be related to gettering energetics by combining Eqs. (2a) and (3) through use of

The interlayer redistributions are illustrated by the results for Au in Fig. 4, where the

The transfer from silicide to cavity sinks exhibits an abrupt saturation at a level corresponding 
within experimental uncertainty to 1 ML on the cavity walls, with a modest temperature 
dependence ascribed to changes in cavity microstructure. No comparable discontinuity is seen 
for the redistribution between two cavity layers, where the @-dependent chemical potentials in the

leading us to hypothesize that the cause was ordered islands of chemisorbed Au coexisting with

chemisorption [13], with adjustable binding free energies being included for both the ordered and

description of all of the experimental data.

The binding free energies for cavity-wall chemisorption and silicide precipitation of Cu 
and Au are plotted in Fig. 3 with the previously discussed findings for Co and Fe. (In the case of 
Au chemisorption, the plotted values are for islands coexisting with random-site chemisorption. 
They are slightly higher than for random-site chemisorption alone.) These results allow the 
prediction of gettering behavior. Two prominent trends are apparent, and both can be 
qualitatively understood on physical grounds. First, AGcav > AGsil for Cu and Au, whereas the 
opposite is true for Co and Fe. This is ascribed to the fact the monovalent metals can optimize 
their bonding by reacting with the single dangling orbitals on Si surface atoms, whereas the 
higher valences of Co and Fe drive these species to precipitate as three-dimensional phases 
where the metal atom can bond to a larger number of Si atoms. Second, most of the energies 
trend upward with decreasing temperature, consistent with a positive value of TASex. We 
hypothesize that this reflects a relatively large vibrational entropy of metal atoms in the 
tetrahedral interstitial solution site, where there is substantial open volume and the metal atoms 
are not strongly bonded into the host-lattice network. The exceptions to this trend occur for 
chemisorbed Cu, where the range of data is too limited to establish a temperature dependence, 
and for the precipitated Si-Au phase, which is molten at the anneal temperatures and therefore 
expected to have a relatively high entropy. 
3. Gettering by precipitated boron silicide 
When implantation of B into Si is followed by annealing at a temperature where the B is 
both mobile and supersaturated, the concentration profile exhibits a central peak at the 
implantation range with wings on either side [see, e.g., Ref. 141. An example from our work of 
this widely reported phenomenon is seen in Fig. 5(a). The generally recognized cause is 
precipitation of a B-Si phase at the central peak accompanied by defect-enhanced difhsion of B 
in the Si lattice at concentrations below the solubility. When the implanted concentration is less

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
.
.

-8- 
than the solubility, the enhanced-diffusion broadening occurs without a precipitation-related 
peak, & seen in Fig. 5@). The unprecipitated B is substitutional and is a shallow acceptor. The 
negatively charged B atoms trap Fe impurities through formation of B-Fe+ pairs with a binding 
energy of about 0.65 eV [15], and at higher B concentrations the attraction relative to the 
unimplanted Si substrate can be augmented by several additional tenths of an eV due to band 
bending [ 161. The resulting gettering, while relatively modest in strength, has been shown to 
remove Fe impurities from solution during cooling from elevated temperatures [ 161. 
We are exploring the gettering of metal atoms by the precipitated B-Si phase, with initial 
experiments being performed on Fe and Cu impurities. The experimental approach parallels that 
used to examine cavity gettering of Co and Fe, with SIMS being employed to measure the 
number of gettered metal atoms in equilibrium with remotely located precipitates of the metal- 
silicide phase. Use of the known thermodynamic properties of the silicides then permits the 
strength of the metal reactions with the B-Si phase to be assessed, as for cavity trapping. 
Selected results from our experiments on Fe are shown in Fig. 5. Before B was 
implanted, excess FeSi2 was formed on the opposite side by implantation of Fe and annealing. 
As a result, the solution concentration of Fe was stabilized at the solubility during the gettering 
anneal at 1273 K. The profiles in Fig. 5(a) exhibit an association of Fe with the B, but only in 
the region of B-Si precipitation. Furthermore, the similarity of B and Fe peak shapes in the 
figure suggests that the concentration of gettered Fe scales with the amount of precipitated B, and 
this is confirmed by plotting the depth profiles with a linear concentration scale. It thus appears 
that Fe is gettered more strongly by the B-Si precipitates than by substitutional By and the scaling 
of B and Fe concentrations leads us to propose that the bound Fe resides in solution in the B-Si 
phase. Similar experiments were carried out for Cu impurities with analogous results, the 
amount of gettered Cu being about an order of magnitude larger. 
Figure 6(a) shows a TEM micrograph of Si after high-dose B implantation and annealing 
at 1373 K, and B-Si precipitates -10 nm in size are apparent. Compositional analysis of these 
particles using electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) indicates that they are B rich, with a B- 
. .

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
-9- 
to-Si atomic ratio near 2.5 or somewhat greater. No evidence of crystallinity in the B-Si phase 
was found by electron diffraction, bright-field contrast, or lattice imagini; moreover, lattice 
images, such as the one in Fig. 6(b), exhibit the irregular granularity characteristic of solids 
lacking long-range structural order. (The TEM specimen was thinned chemically rather than by 
ion milling to avoid amorphization by the latter treatment.) Since 1373 K is the highest 
temperature of this study, we conclude that the observed gettering occurred in a disordered phase 
of approximate composition B3Si. 
When initially supersaturated Si(B) reaches fidl thermodynamic equilibrium at 
temperatures 5 1543 K, the Si host phase is believed to coexist with a crystalline phase of 
approximate composition B3Si [17,18]. Like many B-rich solids, B3Si has a complex structure 
based on icosahedral units at the corners of a large rhombohedral cell with additional atoms 
along the three-fold axis of the cell. Boron-rich structures of this kind have several 
characteristics that are expected to promote the incorporation of transition-metal atoms; these 
include the strong electron affinity and high stability of the icosahedral units, the presence of 
relatively large open volumes outside the icosahedra, and the considerable variability and 
flexibility of the overall structure and bonding configuration [ 19,201. In such a material, 
transition metals can be expected to serve as electron donors and to reside in the high-volume 
interstices with relatively little steric hindrance. 
Although the B-Si precipitates of the present experiment apparently lack long range 
order, we hypothesize that the local bonding and structure are similar to those of crystalline 
B3Si. Indications of this include the similarity of composition and the near coincidence of 
chemical potentials; the latter property is inferred from the observation that the diffusion tails in 
the B depth profiles, such as the one in Fig. 5(a), intersect the central peak at a concentration 
equal within experimental uncertainty to the published equilibrium solubility. That the 
precipitates do not attain full long-range order after 2 h at 1373 K is not surprising in view of the 
structural complexity and highly refractory character of B-rich solids and the small precipitate 
size. Based on these considerations, we provisionally interpret the observed gettering of Fe and

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
-10- 
Cu as arising from solution of the metal atoms in the precipitated, disordered B-Si phase, this 
process being promoted by the properties of B3Si discussed above. 
n 
Figure 7 shows an Arrhenius plot of the ratio of gettered metal atoms to precipitated B 
atoms in equilibrium with metal-silicide phase at a different depth, usually on the opposite side 
of the wafer. The areal density of precipitated B was evaluated by integrating the central peak of 
the depth profile after subtraction of the solution concentration. Results are shown for two doses 
of implanted B that differ by a factor of 5, and the consistency of the ratio between these doses 
reinforces the inference of scaling between gettered-metal concentration and the amount of 
precipitated phase. Diffusion modeling indicates that changes in the amount of gettered metal 
during cooling after annealing should not have significantly perturbed these results. 
With the above interpretation, and if in addition the metal-binding sites within the B-Si 
phase are assumed to be of a single type and not to interact with one another, Eq. (2) is 
applicable. Although the actual number of available sites is not yet known and 8 can therefore 
not be evaluated, if the sites are far from saturation Eq. (2b) can be modified to obtain 
c s  a {AM,gett / hB,ppt> exP(-QkT) 
(6) 
where a is a constant, AM,gett is the areal density of gettered metal atoms, hB,ppt is the areal 
density of precipitated By and Q is an activation energy. While the levels of Cu in Fig. 7 may or 
may not correspond to site saturation, it seems plausible that the much smaller quantities of Fe do 
not. We therefore apply Eq. (6) to Fe: using the fitted line through the Fe data in Fig. 7 and 
Eq. (3) with AGsil[Fe] = 2.94 eV - 8.2 kT gives a = 6100 and Q = 2.21 eV. This result allows 
the extent of Fe gettering to be predicted so long as the sinks are unsaturated. 
As a test of the linear dependence of Cs on (AM,gefi / hB,ppt} in Eq. (6), we performed 
an additional experiment on Fe gettering where silicide was not introduced to stabilize the 
solution concentration at the solubility, so that only pre-existing Fe was present. After the 
gettering anneal at 1273 K, SIMS profiling showed a decrease in {hM,gett / hB,ppt} from the 
silicide-buffered experiments of about a factor of 6, while DLTS indicated that the final solution 
concentration was about one tenth of the published solubility. This semiquantitative consistency

file: Strong segragation gettering of transition metals by implantation-formed cavities and boron-silicide precipitates in silicon.pdf
label: unrelated
text:
-1 1- 
is within the combined experimental uncertainties. 
4. Prediction of gettering performance 
-l 
The implications of our findings for the effectiveness of gettering by cavities and B-Si 
precipitates are illustrated in Fig. 8, where model calculations based on Eqs. (2a), (3), and (6) are 
shown. A wafer a thickness of 0.5 mm was assumed to contain initially a uniform concentration 
of Fe or Cu in solution. The gettering proceeded to equilibrium at 973 K, a temperature where 
metal-atom mobilities are still sufficient to sweep the wafer. The assumed areal density of 
cavity-wall sites was 5x 1 O1 5 cm-2, and the areal density of precipitated B was 1 x 1017 cm-2, 
both typical values. The solution concentration after gettering is plotted in Fig. 8 as a fimction of 
the initial concentration. The curves for cavity gettering include the effect of silicide 
precipitation when the solution concentration exceeds the solid solubility. The gettering by 
B-silicide particles was not extended into this regime; although these precipitates may also 
support nucleation of the metal silicides, this has still to be demonstrated. 
A noteworthy aspect of these computational results is the substantial gettering for initial 
impurity concentrations below the threshold for silicide precipitation: the concentration 
reduction is two orders of magnitude even for Fe in cavities, the least favorable case. When this 
finding is considered in conjunction with the demonstrated microstructural stability of the two 
gettering centers above 1300 K and the anticipated feasibility of gettering on the device side as 
well as the back side of wafers, technological application appears potentially attractive. 
Acknowledgments 
P. J. Feibelman illuminated the competition between chemisorption and silicide 
precipitation on cavity walls. D. Emin and T. L. Aselage provided valuable insights into the 
nature of B-rich solids and the origins of their affinity for transition metals.

text:
Using Phosphorus-Doped a-Si Gettering Layers to Improve
NILC Poly-Si TFT Performance
BAU-MING WANG1 and YEWCHUNG SERMON WU1,2
1.—Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Chiao Tung University, 1001
Ta-Hsueh Road, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan, ROC. 2.—e-mail: SermonWu@StanfordAlumni.org
Ni-metal-induced lateral crystallization (NILC) has been utilized to fabricate
polycrystalline silicon (poly-Si) thin-ﬁlm transistors (TFTs). However, the
current crystallization technology often leads to trapped Ni and NiSi2 pre-
cipitates, thus degrading device performance. In this study, phosphorus-doped
amorphous silicon (p-a-Si) and chemical oxide (chem-SiO2) ﬁlms were used as
Ni-gettering layers. After a gettering process, the Ni impurity within the
NILC poly-Si ﬁlm and the leakage current were both reduced, while the on/off
current ratio was increased. This gettering process is compatible with NILC
TFT processes and suitable for large-area NILC poly-Si ﬁlms.
Key words: Ni-metal-induced lateral crystallization (NILC), polycrystalline
silicon (poly-Si) thin-ﬁlm transistors (TFTs), Ni-gettering layers
INTRODUCTION
High-performance low-temperature polycrystal-
line silicon (poly-Si) thin-ﬁlm transistors (TFTs)
have attracted considerable interest for application
in liquid-crystal displays and organic light-emitting
diode displays on cheap glass substrates.1 Various
methods, including excimer laser crystallization
(ELC), nickel-metal-induced crystallization (NIC),
and nickel-metal-induced laterally crystallization
(NILC), for fabricating high-quality poly-Si have
been
extensively
investigated.2,3
Among
these
techniques,
NILC
could
not
only
obtain
high-
uniformity poly-Si but also reduce manufacturing
costs. However, NILC poly-Si grain boundaries trap
Ni and NiSi2 precipitates, which increase the leak-
age current and shift the threshold voltage.4–8
To improve device performance, Ni contamination
inside the NILC poly-Si ﬁlm should be reduced.
In previous work we used an amorphous silicon
(a-Si) layer and an etch-stop layer to reduce Ni
residues inside NILC poly-Si.9–11 In this study, the
performance
of
these
devices
was
extensively
investigated.
EXPERIMENTAL PROCEDURES
Typical top-gated NILC poly-Si TFTs were used in
this study. Figure 1 shows a top view of the fabri-
cated device. NILC/NILC boundaries were located
at the middle of the channel region. Two kinds of
poly-Si ﬁlms were investigated in this study. One
was a ‘‘NILC-Si’’ poly-Si ﬁlm fabricated by the tra-
ditional NILC method, and the other was a ‘‘GETR-
Si’’ poly-Si ﬁlm fabricated by the same NILC method
with an additional Ni-gettering process.
The basic NILC fabrication process of both poly-Si
ﬁlms began with 4-inch Si(100) wafer substrates, on
which 500 nm wet oxide ﬁlms were grown using a
H2/O2 mixture. Silane-based a-Si ﬁlms with a
thickness of 100 nm were deposited using low-
pressure chemical vapor deposition (LPCVD) at
550C. The photoresist was patterned to form the
desired Ni lines, and a 5-nm-thick Ni ﬁlm was
deposited onto the a-Si. The samples were then dip-
ped into acetone for 5 min to remove the photoresist
and subsequently annealed at 540C for 24 h to form
the NILC-Si ﬁlm. To reduce Ni contamination,
unreacted Ni metal was removed by chemical etch-
ing. The active islands of poly-Si ﬁlms were deﬁned by
reactive ion etching (RIE), as shown in Fig. 2a.
To fabricate the GETR-Si ﬁlm, a NILC-Si ﬁlm was
dipped into a mixed solution of H2SO4 and H2O2 for
10 min to form a chem-SiO2 layer on top of the
(Received July 18, 2009; accepted November 27, 2009;
published online December 29, 2009)
Journal of ELECTRONIC MATERIALS, Vol. 39, No. 2, 2010
DOI: 10.1007/s11664-009-1027-5
 2009 TMS
157
